领域 必须输入 Kubernetes 版本 目标版本、版本偏移支持、插件兼容性、升级窗口。 网络 VPC 或 VNet、CIDR 规划、Pod/Service 网段、DNS、出站 NAT、私有端点要求。 身份 集群管理员模型、用户组映射、工作负载身份机制、应急账号。 节点池 CPU 与内存画像、按量或竞价策略、GPU 或特殊硬件、污点、标签。 存储 默认 StorageClass、有状态负载需求、备份与恢复预期、扩容策略。 Ingress 公网或内网暴露、控制器、TLS 签发者、DNS 归属、WAF 或网关边界。 可观测性 指标、日志、事件、链路、告警路由、仪表盘归属。 交付 GitOps 或流水线归属、命名空间 onboarding、回滚路径、发布策略。 治理 命名、标签、成本归集、资源配额、镜像策略、平台评审周期。

供给记录不需要很长，但必须明确。依赖默认值的集群，会在第一次事故中变成运营惊喜。

docs.title: 1. 定义平台边界
docs.text:
先决定这个集群允许拥有什么。不要因为安装时顺手配置了 DNS、Secret、证书、Ingress、CI/CD、数据库和监控，就让集群意外成为这些能力的所有者。

至少明确：

  • 生产、预发、开发或共享平台范围。
  • 允许承载的工作负载类型。
  • 网络暴露模型。
  • 团队接入流程。
  • 平台插件所有权。
  • 事故升级路径。

docs.title: 2. 冻结版本与插件矩阵
docs.text:
选择 Kubernetes 版本时必须同时选择要运行的插件版本。不要先选集群版本，再事后发现插件不兼容。

矩阵必须包含：

  • CNI 插件及版本。
  • CoreDNS。
  • kube-proxy 或替代组件。
  • CSI 驱动。
  • Ingress 控制器。
  • metrics-server。
  • Cluster Autoscaler 或 Karpenter 类组件。
  • 日志与指标 agent。
  • 如使用策略控制器，也要纳入矩阵。

最低可接受产物是一张表：哪些版本已批准、哪些版本被阻断、需要什么升级顺序。

docs.title: 3. 先验证网络，再创建节点
docs.text:
网络比集群更难改。创建节点池前先验证网络。

必查项：

  • Pod 与 Service CIDR 不与 VPC/VNet、对等网络、VPN 网段或本地数据中心网段冲突。
  • DNS 可以解析云 API、内部服务和私有端点。
  • 节点可以访问必要的镜像仓库和控制面端点。
  • 出站策略是刻意设计的，不是偶然全开放。
  • 如有需要，入口子网与内网负载均衡子网分离。

如果网络规划不清楚，就停止。重建一个年轻集群，比长期忍受网段冲突便宜得多。

docs.title: 4. 应用接入前配置身份与访问
docs.text:
人员访问和工作负载访问必须在应用团队进入前设好。

必须决策：

  • 哪些用户组拥有 cluster-admin。
  • 哪些用户组拥有命名空间级管理员权限。
  • 服务账号如何获得云权限。
  • 应急访问如何授予、记录和回收。
  • 哪些审计日志需要保留。

避免把永久 cluster-admin 绑定到个人用户。使用用户组、限时应急访问和有记录的定期评审。

docs.title: 5. 把节点池当作工作负载契约
docs.text:
节点池不只是容量，而是工作负载放置契约。

至少定义：

  • 通用无状态节点池。
  • 如有需要，系统或平台插件节点池。
  • 如有要求，有状态或存储敏感节点池。
  • 竞价或可抢占节点池只能给可容忍中断的工作负载。
  • GPU 或特殊硬件节点池必须通过污点与标签隔离。

每个节点池都应有标签、必要的污点、自动伸缩边界、镜像生命周期规则和补丁或替换策略。

docs.title: 6. 在工作负载接入前安装存储
docs.text:
应用部署前先安装 CSI 驱动和 StorageClass。没有经过测试的默认存储路径，集群就不适合承载有状态工作负载。

最小验证：

  • PVC 可以动态创建。
  • Pod 可以挂载并写入文件系统。
  • 扩容行为已知。
  • 回收策略是刻意选择的。
  • 备份或快照预期已记录。
  • zone 或拓扑约束对调度器可见。

存储细节见 Kubernetes 存储与 Ingress 标准。

docs.title: 7. 发布服务前安装 Ingress 与 TLS
docs.text:
Ingress 必须是平台托管能力，不能让每个团队自行 improvisation。

验证：

  • IngressClass 归属。
  • 公网或内网暴露方式。
  • TLS 证书签发者与续期责任。
  • DNS 归属。
  • 请求超时、请求体大小和 WebSocket 预期。
  • 访问日志和指标可用。
  • 如需要，明确 WAF 或网关边界。

docs.title: 8. 业务流量进入前安装可观测性
docs.text:
可观测性是上线依赖，不是事故后补装项。

最小信号：

  • 节点健康和资源饱和。
  • Pod 重启、调度失败、拉镜像失败。
  • 如平台暴露，API Server 健康。
  • Ingress 请求量、延迟和错误率。
  • 存在有状态工作负载时的 PVC 使用率与扩容风险。
  • 用于排障的事件采集。
  • 告警路由到责任团队。

docs.title: 9. 加入交付与回滚控制
docs.text:
第一个部署机制先要能回滚，再追求速度。

必须控制：

  • 命名空间 onboarding 模式。
  • 发布负责人。
  • 镜像来源预期。
  • 滚动发布策略。
  • 回滚命令或 GitOps revert 路径。
  • 发布健康检查。
  • 变更记录。

操作回滚流程见 部署回滚 Runbook。

docs.title: 托管集群画像
docs.text:
对托管 Kubernetes 服务，优先使用云厂商托管控制面，避免自管那些云厂商能更稳定运营的组件。

必须决策：

  • 控制面端点是私有还是公开。
  • 控制面访问白名单。
  • 托管节点组还是自管节点组。
  • IAM 或工作负载身份集成。
  • 云厂商支持的 CSI 和负载均衡控制器。
  • 云厂商版本支持窗口。
  • 平台团队与云厂商之间的升级责任边界。

不要把“托管”理解成“生产就绪”。云厂商只运营平台的一部分；你的团队仍然拥有工作负载访问、插件、交付、可观测性、数据保护和事故响应。

docs.title: kubeadm 自建画像
docs.text:
对 kubeadm 集群，团队必须显式拥有控制面。

额外要求：

  • 高可用 API Server 入口。
  • etcd 备份与恢复测试。
  • 证书轮换流程。
  • 控制面节点补丁流程。
  • CNI 安装与升级策略。
  • kubelet 与容器运行时生命周期。
  • 灾难恢复 Runbook。

如果这些能力无法配人并测试，除非有硬约束阻止，否则应使用托管集群。

docs.title: 应用接入就绪门禁
docs.text:
接收业务工作负载前，执行以下门禁：

门禁 通过条件 节点 目标节点池全部 Ready，标签、污点符合设计，自动伸缩边界正确。 系统 Pod CoreDNS、CNI、CSI、Ingress、metrics-server、可观测性 agent 健康。 存储 测试 PVC 可以创建、挂载、写入、按支持情况扩容，并符合回收策略。 Ingress 测试服务能通过预期公网或内网路径访问，TLS 有效。 身份 工作负载能通过预期云身份访问资源，不依赖静态密钥。 可观测性 测试故障能产生可搜索的日志、指标、事件和告警。 交付 测试部署能通过批准机制前进和回滚。 成本与所有权 命名空间、标签、配额和升级路径可见。

每个门禁都有负责人和结果后，集群才可以开放 onboarding。

docs.title: 验证
docs.text:
从干净的运维工作站或 CI runner，以正常平台权限执行验证：

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get ingressclass
kubectl top nodes
kubectl auth can-i create deployments --namespace <test-namespace>
kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as <non-admin-user>

然后创建并删除一个小型验证工作负载：

  • 带 readiness probe 的无状态 Deployment。
  • 使用 PVC 的 Pod。
  • 内部 Service。
  • 带 TLS 的 Ingress 路由。
  • 一次故意失败的发布，以及随后的回滚。

把结果记录在集群就绪文档中。截图不如可重复命令和预期输出可靠。

docs.title: 常见失败模式
docs.text: 失败模式 为什么危险 预防方式 CIDR 冲突 对等、VPN 和服务路由后续失败。 创建集群前验证全部地址范围。 插件版本不匹配 CNI、CSI 或 Ingress 在升级后损坏。 冻结并评审版本矩阵。 未测试 StorageClass 有状态工作负载阻塞或丢数据。 执行 PVC 创建、挂载、写入、扩容和回收测试。 先做 Ingress 后定 TLS 归属 服务公开了，但无人负责证书续期。 暴露前明确 DNS 与证书所有权。 应用先于可观测性上线 第一次事故没有有用证据。 把可观测性作为上线依赖。 个人永久 admin 权限 审计和离职回收变弱。 使用用户组、应急访问和定期评审。 节点自动伸缩无边界 成本暴涨或调度不稳定。 设置最小、最大、标签、污点、配额和告警。

docs.title: 回滚与重建边界
docs.text:
有些供给错误应该通过重建集群解决，而不是持续打补丁。

优先重建的情况：

  • Pod 或 Service CIDR 与其他网络冲突。
  • 集群创建在错误 VPC、区域或账号边界。
  • 身份集成围绕长期静态凭据建立。
  • 插件所有权不清晰，没人能安全升级。
  • 集群已经接入业务但缺少基线可观测性，且迁移成本仍低。

优先原地修正的情况：

  • 节点池需要扩容、缩容或替换。
  • 插件配置错误，但版本兼容。
  • StorageClass 或 IngressClass 需要在迁移计划后更换默认值。
  • RBAC 需要通过可审计访问评审收紧。

问题发现得越早，就越应认真考虑重建。

docs.title: 安全说明
docs.text:
• 不要在公开文档中保存 kubeconfig、token、证书或云密钥。
  • 不要公开私有端点地址、客户域名或内部 CIDR。
  • 使用 <pod-cidr> 或 <service-cidr> 这类符号占位符，不复制生产网段。
  • 优先使用工作负载身份，不把静态云凭据挂进 Pod。
  • 在授予团队访问前启用审计日志。
  • 每次团队接入和事故之后，评审 cluster-admin 绑定。

docs.title: 评审周期
docs.text:
在以下时点评审本标准：

  • 每次新建集群前。
  • 每次 Kubernetes minor 版本升级前。
  • 每次网络、身份、存储或 Ingress 事故后。
  • 新工作负载类型需要新节点池或存储画像时。

docs.title: 相关文档
docs.text:
• Kubernetes 存储与 Ingress 标准
  • NFS PVC 容量管理
  • 部署回滚 Runbook
  • Prometheus 告警标准

docs.title: Kubernetes 存储与 Ingress 标准
目的
docs.text:
本标准定义在业务工作负载依赖 Kubernetes 存储和 Ingress 之前，如何设计和验证这两项能力。

核心判断是：

存储和 Ingress 不是插件，而是平台与每个应用团队之间的生产契约。

存储决定状态能否保留、扩容、迁移和恢复。Ingress 决定用户如何访问服务、TLS 如何续期、流量如何记录，以及集群的外部边界从哪里开始。如果把它们当作默认安装组件，生产风险会一直不可见，直到第一次故障才爆出来。

docs.title: 适用场景
docs.text:
在以下情况使用本标准：

  • 供给新的 Kubernetes 集群。
  • 引入或变更 CSI 驱动、StorageClass、IngressClass 或 Ingress 控制器。
  • 接入有状态工作负载。
  • 通过公网或内网 Ingress 发布服务。
  • 在集群成为共享生产平台前进行评审。

docs.title: 非目标
docs.text:
本标准不覆盖：

  • 完整数据库备份架构。
  • 服务网格流量策略。
  • 云负载均衡成本优化细节。
  • 应用层数据模型设计。
  • 某个 Ingress 控制器的所有功能。

docs.title: 设计原则
docs.text:
生产平台应暴露少量命名清晰的选择，而不是一堆隐式默认值。

应用团队应该能回答：

  • 普通持久化数据应使用哪个 StorageClass？
  • 哪个 StorageClass 支持扩容？
  • 如有，哪个存储画像支持 ReadWriteMany？
  • 谁拥有备份和恢复？
  • 哪个 IngressClass 用于公网流量？
  • 哪个 IngressClass 用于内网流量？
  • 谁拥有 DNS 和 TLS 续期？
  • 默认请求限制和超时是多少？

如果这些答案必须翻安装历史才能找到，平台契约就不够清晰。

docs.title: 显式定义存储画像
docs.text:
至少定义这些存储画像：

画像 典型用途 访问模式 扩容 备份预期 说明 standard-rwo 普通应用持久化 ReadWriteOnce 支持 应用或平台策略 只有对大多数应用安全时才设为默认。 fast-rwo 延迟敏感状态 ReadWriteOnce 支持 必须 仅在性能需求被证明后使用。 shared-rwx 共享文件、上传、模型产物 ReadWriteMany 平台定义 必须 通常是 NFS 或托管文件存储；必须监控真实文件系统使用率。 ephemeral 可丢弃缓存或临时状态 视实现而定 不保证 无 不得保存业务关键数据。

在创建、挂载、扩容、删除和失败行为都被验证前，不要把某个 StorageClass 设为默认。

docs.title: 把回收策略作为刻意决策
docs.text:
Delete 很方便，但 PVC 被删除时可能直接销毁数据。Retain 能保护数据，但会制造清理责任。

使用以下规则：

  • 只有可丢弃或易重建数据才使用 Delete。
  • 业务数据默认使用 Retain，除非备份和恢复契约另有说明。
  • 记录谁可以删除保留卷，以及如何确认数据已经不再需要。

docs.title: 把扩容当作 Runbook，而不是勾选项
docs.text:
allowVolumeExpansion: true 只能说明 Kubernetes 接受扩容请求，不证明底层文件系统、CSI 驱动、配额和应用都能安全承受这个变化。

批准某个画像支持扩容前，验证：

  • CSI 驱动支持在线或离线扩容。
  • 文件系统能看到新的容量。
  • 应用能容忍扩容路径。
  • 扩容后监控仍跟踪真实文件系统。
  • 回滚预期明确。缩小卷通常不是安全回滚。

docs.title: 把备份责任与存储供给分开
docs.text:
创建 PV 不等于保护其中的数据。

每个存储画像都要记录：

  • 是否需要备份。
  • 快照是崩溃一致还是应用一致。
  • 谁拥有恢复测试。
  • 预期恢复耗时。
  • 哪些工作负载不得使用该画像。

docs.title: CSI 验证
docs.text:
有状态工作负载接入前，验证 CSI：

kubectl get csidrivers
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

然后运行真实 PVC 测试：

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: storage-validation
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: standard-rwo
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: storage-validation
spec:
  restartPolicy: Never
  containers:
    - name: writer
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "date > /data/proof && cat /data/proof"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
  volumes:
    - name: data
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: storage-validation

通过条件：

  • PVC 变为 Bound。
  • Pod 调度到预期节点。
  • 文件写入成功。
  • Events 中没有 CSI 错误。
  • 删除测试资源后，表现符合预期回收行为。

docs.title: 把 IngressClass 定义为暴露契约
docs.text:
IngressClass 名称应表达暴露范围和所有权。

推荐模式：

IngressClass 暴露范围 典型所有者 必须控制 public-web 面向互联网 平台或边缘团队 TLS、DNS、访问日志、如需要则 WAF 或网关评审。 private-web 内部网络 平台团队 内网负载均衡、私有 DNS、访问日志。 partner-web 受限外部访问 平台与安全团队 白名单、TLS、审计、显式审批。

避免在没有默认规则的情况下运行多个 Ingress 控制器。应用不应因为漏了一个注解就暴露到错误控制器。

docs.title: 把 TLS 当作生命周期来拥有
docs.text:
TLS 不是证书首次签发后就结束。

必须记录：

  • 证书签发者。
  • 续期机制。
  • DNS 验证所有者。
  • 过期告警。
  • Secret 所在命名空间与访问策略。
  • 应急替换流程。

如果 TLS 续期是手工流程，服务必须有过期告警和明确负责人。

docs.title: 标准化 Ingress 默认值
docs.text:
设置并记录：

  • 请求超时。
  • 请求体大小。
  • WebSocket 支持。
  • HTTP 到 HTTPS 跳转。
  • 访问日志格式。
  • 真实客户端 IP 处理方式。
  • 健康检查路径。
  • 如使用，限流或 WAF 边界。

这些值会影响应用行为，不能隐藏在 Ingress 控制器 chart 中而没有平台文档。

docs.title: Ingress 验证
docs.text:
暴露真实流量前，先使用小型验证服务：

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ingress-validation
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-production
spec:
  ingressClassName: public-web
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - example.devops.test
      secretName: ingress-validation-tls
  rules:
    - host: example.devops.test
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: ingress-validation
                port:
                  number: 80

通过条件：

  • Ingress 被分配给预期控制器。
  • 负载均衡或网关地址出现。
  • DNS 解析到预期目标。
  • TLS 证书有效并可续期。
  • 访问日志和请求指标出现。
  • 404、5xx 和慢请求能在预期仪表盘或查询路径里看到。

docs.title: 验证清单
docs.text: 领域 检查 StorageClass 默认值是刻意设置、已记录、已验证。 CSI 控制器和节点插件在各节点池健康。 PVC 创建、挂载、写入、删除和扩容行为已知。 回收 数据保留行为匹配工作负载重要性。 备份 恢复负责人和恢复测试预期已记录。 IngressClass 公网与内网暴露路径清晰分离。 TLS 签发者、续期、过期告警和应急替换明确。 日志与指标 存储和 Ingress 故障可观测。 所有权 平台责任和应用责任分离。

docs.title: 常见失败模式
docs.text: 失败模式 症状 预防方式 缺少默认 StorageClass PVC 一直 Pending。 定义安全默认值，或要求显式设置 storageClassName。 访问模式错误 Pod 无法挂载卷，或多写工作负载造成数据损坏。 将工作负载映射到批准的画像。 CSI IAM 或身份失败 PVC 无法创建，Events 显示云权限错误。 接入前验证工作负载身份。 回收策略不清 数据过早删除或永久保留。 记录回收策略和清理负责人。 把 NFS 当作隐形容量池 PVC 看似正常，后端文件系统却被写满。 同时监控真实文件系统使用率和 PVC 元数据。 IngressClass 错误 服务不可达或意外公网暴露。 使用显式 ingressClassName，必要时加准入检查。 TLS 续期缺失 证书过期后服务失败。 添加过期告警和明确续期负责人。 控制器默认值隐藏 大文件上传、WebSocket 或慢请求异常失败。 发布默认超时和请求体大小策略。

docs.title: 回滚与变更边界
docs.text:
存储和 Ingress 变更的回滚属性不同。

通常可逆：

  • 新增 StorageClass。
  • 新增 IngressClass。
  • 修改验证 Ingress 的注解。
  • 在 canary 后替换 Ingress 控制器配置。

高风险或无法安全回滚：

  • 在已有工作负载依赖后改变默认 StorageClass。
  • 没有迁移计划就改变回收策略。
  • 缩小存储。
  • 没有备份与恢复证明就迁移有状态工作负载到新存储后端。
  • 没有规划 DNS、TLS 和客户端缓存就替换公网入口。

如果回滚路径是“从备份恢复”，这就不是普通配置回滚，而是数据恢复操作。

docs.title: 安全说明
docs.text:
• 不要在示例中公开真实主机名、证书细节、私有 IP 或客户域名。
  • 限制 TLS Secret 访问。
  • 避免把过宽云凭据挂载给 CSI 组件。
  • 存储驱动使用最小权限工作负载身份。
  • DNS 委派前评审公网 Ingress 暴露。
  • 保留访问日志用于事故复盘，但避免记录敏感请求体。

docs.title: 评审周期
docs.text:
在以下时点评审本标准：

  • 新集群接收工作负载前。
  • 变更 CSI 驱动、StorageClass、Ingress 控制器或 IngressClass 前。
  • 任何数据丢失、存储饱和、证书过期或公网暴露事故后。
  • 接入新的有状态工作负载类别前。

docs.title: 相关文档
docs.text:
• Kubernetes 集群供给标准
  • NFS PVC 容量管理
  • PVC 使用率告警 Runbook
  • TLS 证书过期 Runbook

docs.title: 目的
docs.text:
本标准定义如何管理由 NFS 或类似共享文件存储支撑的 Kubernetes PVC 容量。

核心判断是：

NFS-backed PVC 容量，本质是披着 Kubernetes 元数据外衣的共享文件系统问题。

PVC request 只告诉 Kubernetes 这个工作负载申请了多少容量。它不一定告诉你后端文件系统真实剩余多少、还有多少 PVC 共享同一个后端，也不一定说明后端是否能强制每个 claim 的容量限制。容量管理必须把两层信息连接起来。

docs.title: 适用场景
docs.text:
在以下情况使用本标准：

  • Kubernetes PVC 来自 NFS 服务器、托管文件服务或共享文件系统。
  • 多个 PVC 映射到同一个后端 export 下的不同目录。
  • PVC 使用率告警与后端文件系统使用率不一致。
  • 应用团队使用共享文件、上传、模型产物、报表或批处理输出。
  • 平台需要容量所有权和告警阈值标准。

当 NFS 被作为便利的 ReadWriteMany 方案时，本标准尤其重要。便利不等于可以放弃容量隔离、监控和清理规则。

docs.title: 非目标
docs.text:
本标准不覆盖：

  • 从零设计高可用 NFS。
  • 数据库存储调优。
  • 用 NFS 替代对象存储保存大型不可变产物。
  • 共享文件的完整备份架构。
  • 厂商特定文件服务操作。

docs.title: 双层容量模型
docs.text:
把 NFS PVC 容量作为两个相关但不同的层来管理：

层 能看到什么 不能证明什么 Kubernetes PVC 元数据 Namespace、claim 名、申请容量、StorageClass、PV、挂载关系。 真实后端剩余空间、共享使用量、文件系统压力。 NFS 后端文件系统 实际总量、已用、可用、inode 使用、目录增长。 Kubernetes 所有者、命名空间、工作负载意图、数据重要性。

任何单层都不够。

只看 Kubernetes，可能错过多个 claim 共同写满后端文件系统。只看 NFS，可能知道某个目录很大，却不知道它属于哪个 namespace、工作负载和团队。

docs.title: 必需 Inventory
docs.text:
维护每个 PVC 到后端现实的映射：

字段 示例 Namespace payments-prod PVC uploads-data PV pvc-abc123 StorageClass shared-rwx Backend export nfs-shared-01:/exports/k8s Backend path /exports/k8s/payments-prod/uploads-data Workload payments-api Owner 平台或应用团队别名 Data class 上传、缓存、报表、产物、日志、未知 Retention 30 天、负责人定义或无 Backup 必须、不需要或未知 Expansion model 后端池扩容、目录 quota 或迁移

如果平台无法把某个目录完整映射到负责人，就不得删除该目录下内容。

docs.title: 标准阈值
docs.text:
使用两组阈值：后端文件系统阈值，以及每 PVC 或每目录增长阈值。

信号 Warning Critical 动作 后端文件系统使用率 75% 90% 评审增长，计划扩容或清理批准数据。 后端 inode 使用率 70% 85% 找出小文件来源和负责人。 单 PVC 目录增长 24 小时增长 20% 24 小时增长 40% 识别写入者和数据类别。 预计写满 7 天 72 小时 准备容量动作。 未知负责人数据 任何持续增长 任何 critical 压力 升级；不要盲删。

按后端规模和业务风险调节阈值。大型文件系统通常需要比百分比告警更早的预测告警。

docs.title: 收集 Kubernetes 元数据
docs.text:
收集：

  • PVC 名。
  • Namespace。
  • PV 名。
  • StorageClass。
  • 申请容量。
  • Volume mode。
  • Access mode。
  • 使用该 claim 的工作负载。

Kubernetes 指标不总是暴露后端路径。如果 provisioner 把路径写在 PV attributes 或 annotations 中，应把它纳入 inventory。

docs.title: 收集后端文件系统使用量
docs.text:
从 NFS 服务器、托管文件服务指标或批准的观测主机收集：

  • 文件系统总量、已用、可用和使用率。
  • inode 总量、已用、可用和使用率。
  • 每个 export 使用量。
  • 可行时的每目录使用量。
  • 增长速率。
  • 按大小和文件数排序的 top 目录。

对于 Linux NFS 服务器，平台侧采集可能使用：

df -h /exports/k8s
df -i /exports/k8s
du -xh --max-depth=2 /exports/k8s 2>/dev/null | sort -h

在繁忙文件服务器的高峰期，不要在不了解影响的情况下运行昂贵的递归扫描。

docs.title: 连接两层信息
docs.text:
容量仪表盘和告警应显示：

  • 后端文件系统压力。
  • Top PVC 目录。
  • Namespace 和 owner。
  • Workload 和数据类别。
  • 增长趋势。
  • 最近评审日期。

真正有用的问题不是“哪块盘满了”，而是“哪个工作负载、哪类数据正在消耗共享容量，什么动作是安全的？”

docs.title: 1. 行动前先分类数据
docs.text:
使用以下分类：

数据类别 默认动作 缓存 通过批准的应用方法清理或重建。 临时导出 应用保留策略，并把长期产物迁到其他位置。 用户上传 未经负责人批准和备份确认，不删除。 应用状态 当作业务数据；扩容或迁移。 日志 迁移到日志管道或对象存储，按保留期清理。 未知 升级；不做破坏性操作。

docs.title: 2. 选择容量动作
docs.text: 情况 动作 后端容量充足，但单个 PVC 目录增长。 与负责人协作；执行保留策略、迁移数据或调整 claim 预期。 多个 claim 共享一个池导致后端写满。 扩容后端、拆分租户或迁移高增长 claim。 inode 先于字节耗尽。 找到小文件工作负载，归档或压缩，并改变应用行为。 NFS 在负载下性能下降。 降低扫描压力、拆分工作负载或迁移到更合适后端。 PVC request 与实际使用不匹配。 更新 inventory 和告警；不要只依赖申请容量。

docs.title: 3. 谨慎扩容
docs.text:
对 NFS，扩容可能意味着增加后端文件系统或调整 quota，而不是 patch 某个 PVC。

扩容前确认：

  • 后端池余量或云厂商 quota。
  • 备份或快照姿态。
  • 扩容是否影响所有租户。
  • 扩容后的告警阈值。
  • 新容量和预期 forecast 已记录。

docs.title: 4. NFS 抽象错误时迁移
docs.text:
NFS 经常被误用于本该放在其他地方的数据。

以下情况考虑迁移：

  • 大型不可变产物更适合对象存储。
  • 数据库被放在共享文件存储上且缺少强理由。
  • 单个租户主导了共享文件系统容量。
  • 应用性能依赖 NFS 无法保证的文件系统语义。
  • 清理依赖高风险手工删除。

docs.title: 清理安全
docs.text:
绝不能因为某个目录很大就直接从 NFS 删除。

最低删除要求：

  • 负责人已识别。
  • 数据类别已识别。
  • 保留策略或明确审批已确认。
  • 备份或非关键分类已确认。
  • 精确路径已评审。
  • 命令范围限定在目标目录。
  • 结果已验证。

优先使用应用级删除，而不是平台 shell 删除。应用通常更知道哪些文件被索引、被引用或可以安全移除。

docs.title: 验证
docs.text:
本标准落地的条件：

检查 通过条件 Inventory 每个 NFS-backed PVC 都映射到 namespace、workload、owner、backend export 和 path。 指标 后端字节、后端 inode 和 PVC 元数据能一起看到。 告警 后端压力和每目录快速增长都能被发现。 所有权 未知 owner 目录被作为风险跟踪。 响应 清理、扩容、迁移和升级路径已记录。 安全 未经 owner 和数据分类，不做破坏性清理。

docs.title: 常见失败模式
docs.text: 失败模式 后果 预防 只监控 PVC 申请容量 后端写满而 PVC 看似正常。 监控后端文件系统使用率。 只监控 NFS 服务器 大目录没有清晰 Kubernetes owner。 维护 PVC 到路径的 inventory。 共享池没有租户隔离 单个工作负载消耗所有人的容量。 使用 quota、独立 export 或独立 StorageClass。 手工删除未知文件 数据丢失或应用损坏。 清理前要求 owner 和数据类别。 高峰期递归扫描 调查时进一步拉高 NFS 延迟。 安排扫描窗口或使用轻量指标。 没有 inode 告警 大量小文件在字节未满前破坏写入。 对 inode 使用率和文件数增长告警。

docs.title: 安全说明
docs.text:
• 不公开真实 export 路径、服务器名、IP 或客户目录名。
  • 限制 NFS 服务器 shell 访问。
  • 避免对共享应用文件赋予过宽读取权限。
  • 用户上传和报表都按潜在敏感数据处理。
  • 公开文档中的清理命令必须是通用占位示例。

docs.title: 评审周期
docs.text:
在以下时点评审本标准：

  • 接入新的 NFS-backed StorageClass 前。
  • 每次 NFS 容量或性能事故后。
  • 某个租户成为 top 容量消费者时。
  • 显著增加后端容量前。
  • 应用数据保留策略变化时。

docs.title: Blog
docs.text:
The Blog publishes articles with clear context, engineering judgment, and long-term maintenance value. It is not a command dump or a temporary changelog. Each article should help readers understand a real DevOps production problem.

Architecture AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Break down production-grade EKS high availability through constraints, failure modes, delivery phases, and rollback strategy. Cases & Reviews Cases, Reviews, and Deep Dives Build counterintuitive judgment around high availability, rollback, alerts, cost, platform engineering, and security governance. Capability Map DevOps Capability and Self-Assessment Understand DevOps as a layered capability system, judge maturity, and position your own level.

docs.title: Publishing Standard
docs.text:
Before an article enters the Blog, it should satisfy four requirements:

Standard Requirement Problem context Readers can see the production problem, not only a tool name Boundary conditions The article explains where it applies, where it does not, and what organizational or cost constraints matter Evidence of judgment It includes failure modes, metrics, incident symptoms, migration stages, or trade-off reasoning Reusable conclusion Readers can reuse the conclusion for assessment, design, or review in their own systems

docs.title: Subscription And Discovery
docs.text: RSS Subscribe to English article updates. Tags Browse by Architecture, Reliability, Delivery, Platform, and other themes. Cases & Reviews Extract reusable judgment from wrong decisions, changing constraints, and trade-off evidence.

docs.title: Future Article Sources
docs.text: Future posts should come from official sections: Deep Dives, Case Studies, Decision Reviews, Incident Reviews, and Production Architecture. An article should enter the Blog only when it has real constraints, failure modes, and verifiable conclusions.

docs.title: Expert DevOps Knowledge Base
docs.text:
DevOps is not "someone who writes scripts" and it is not "operations with Kubernetes". It is an engineering capability system that connects software delivery, production operations, reliability, security governance, and organizational feedback.

This site is not organized by tools. It is organized the way a senior DevOps engineer looks at production systems:

How systems are designed, how change is delivered, how failure is recovered, how problems become visible, how capability is platformed, and how experience is reviewed.

docs.title: Start With The Capability Map
docs.text:
The site's constitution is the Capability Map. It answers three questions before you decide what to read next:

  1. DevOps Capability Topology — which layered capabilities make up DevOps? The stack from foundation to organizational feedback.
  2. Value and Maturity — how is DevOps value defined? The five-level model from L1 Tooling to L5 Adaptive.
  3. Self-Assessment and Growth Path — are you Ops, junior DevOps, or close to architect or expert? A six-dimension self-scoring matrix.

Not sure what to read first? See the Content Maturity Map, which honestly labels the status of each section.

docs.title: Read By Production Situation
docs.text:
Use these paths when you want an answer, not a tour of the navigation.

Situation Read First Then Read What You Should Get I want to judge whether this site is serious. AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration A benchmark for production-grade judgment: constraints, failure modes, validation, rollback, and a non-obvious operating model. I want to know where I stand as a DevOps engineer. Self-Assessment and Growth Path DevOps Capability Topology A level model that separates tool familiarity from architecture, reliability, governance, and feedback capability. I am designing or upgrading Kubernetes for production. AWS EKS Production HA Cluster EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade, Kubernetes Storage and Ingress A way to reason about availability, node migration, add-ons, ingress, storage, and rollback as one operating system. I need safer production change delivery. Rollback Is a Product Capability Argo CD GitOps, Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline, Deployment Rollback Runbook A rollback contract, release flow, auditability, promotion control, and concrete recovery instead of one-way deployment automation. I need to improve operations reliability. Prometheus Alerting Exporter Monitoring, TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook Alert ownership, target coverage, escalation, and recovery procedures that can be verified under production pressure. I need a reusable operating baseline. Linux Server Baseline Docker Host Operations, GitLab Operations, JumpServer Bastion Access A practical baseline for hosts, containers, source control, privileged access, backups, and auditability.

docs.title: Site Structure
docs.text: Section Production question it answers Capability Map How do I understand DevOps instead of memorizing a tool list? System Design How do I design a production-grade system with boundaries and trade-offs? Change Delivery How do I deliver change to production safely and reduce failure rate? Reliable Operations When the system breaks, can I see it, locate it, recover, and review? Platform Governance How do teams share standards while controlling permissions, security, and cost? Cases & Reviews What reusable judgment comes from real projects and wrong decisions? Standards Manual Where do I look up exact standards, steps, templates, and tags?

docs.title: Most Worth Reading First
docs.text:
• AWS EKS Production High Availability Cluster Design and Delivery Standard — the site quality benchmark, covering conclusion, scope, failure modes, and rollback.
  • Self-Assessment and Growth Path — score your own DevOps level.
  • Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration — a deep-dive example of the site's preferred style: expose the hidden operating contract, then validate it.
  • Rollback Is a Product Capability, Not a Kubernetes Command — a release-safety deep dive connecting architecture, data compatibility, automation, and user-path recovery.

docs.title: Capability Map
docs.text:
DevOps is not "someone who writes scripts" and it is not "operations with Kubernetes". It is an engineering capability system that connects software delivery, production operations, reliability, security governance, and organizational feedback.

This site is not a tool directory. It is organized the way a senior DevOps engineer looks at production systems: how systems are designed, how change is delivered, how failure is recovered, how problems become visible, how capability is platformed, and how experience is reviewed.

This section is the site's constitution. It answers three questions before you decide what to read next:

Page Question it answers DevOps Capability Topology Which layered capabilities make up DevOps? Value and Maturity How is DevOps value defined and maturity rated? Self-Assessment and Growth Path Am I Ops, junior DevOps, or close to architect or expert? Content Maturity Map Which sections are mature, which are under construction, what should I read first?

After this section, you should be able to judge whether this site is valuable to you, and which content is most worth reading first.

docs.title: Content Maturity Map
docs.text: This site is under construction. Instead of using empty sections to fake completeness, it tells you the real status of each area and what is most worth reading first.

docs.title: Maturity Legend
docs.text:
• Mature: Credible, directly usable content already exists.
  • Reference: Operational standards, good for looking up exact steps, but not a substitute for deep articles.
  • Flagship pending: The section frame exists, but the deep flagship article is not yet written.
  • Under construction: Only a positioning statement exists; content is still being accumulated.

docs.title: Section Status
docs.text: Section Status What you can read now Capability Map Mature Capability topology, value and maturity, self-assessment matrix — the site's judgment framework System Design Mature (flagship) AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Standard is the site quality benchmark; plus 5 Kubernetes production standards Change Delivery Strong (rollback flagship) Rollback Is a Product Capability, Argo CD GitOps, Jenkins→K8s pipeline, deployment rollback runbook Reliable Operations Reference 6 observability standards + 4 recovery runbooks (PVC, disk expansion, cert expiry, health check) Platform Governance Reference GitLab, JumpServer bastion access, Redash, platform ownership controls Cases & Reviews Mature (deep dives) Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration and Rollback Is a Product Capability; anonymous cases remain secondary examples until rewritten Standards Manual Mature (largest) ~30 operational standards organized by technical domain + document templates, ready for lookup

docs.title: Three Recommended Reading Paths
docs.text:
1. To judge whether this site is serious: read the AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Standard directly — it is the site quality benchmark.
  2. To position yourself: read Self-Assessment and Growth Path and score yourself on the six-dimension matrix.
  3. To look up a tool's standard practice: go to the Standards Manual and search by technical domain or tag.

Flagship-pending sections will not waste your time with empty writing-rule pages — they are clearly marked "under construction" and link to the nearest real content.

docs.title: Value and Maturity
docs.text:
Definition: DevOps is an engineering collaboration model that uses automation, standardization, observability, and feedback loops to connect development delivery and production operations into a continuous improvement system.

Its value is not measured by the number of tools used. It is measured by whether this loop keeps improving:

flowchart LR A["Smaller changes"] --> B["Faster delivery"] B --> C["Lower failure rate"] C --> D["Faster recovery"] D --> E["More trustworthy business cadence"] E --> A Value Dimension Low Maturity High Maturity Quantitative Signals Delivery Speed Manual releases and remembered steps Standardized pipelines and clear environment promotion Deployment frequency, lead time Change Safety Release depends on experience; failures are handled ad hoc Canary, rollback, freeze windows, release gates Change failure rate, rollback time Recovery Capability Incidents depend on individual heroics SLOs, alert tiers, drills, and reviews MTTR, incident impact duration System Transparency Monitoring exists but diagnosis is slow Metrics, logs, traces, and business context are connected MTTD, alert usefulness Cost Efficiency Idle resources have no ownership Capacity models, cost attribution, elasticity strategy Unit business cost, idle resource ratio Security Governance Secrets sprawl and scanning happens late Secrets, audit, supply chain, and security gates shift left Critical vulnerability repair time, permission drift count

docs.title: Five Maturity Levels
docs.text:
Level State Characteristics Main Risk L1 Tooling Scripts and tools exist Repetition is reduced, but capability depends on individuals Automating the wrong process L2 Process Standard processes exist Release, rollback, approval, and troubleshooting become reusable Process exists but execution quality varies L3 Platform Shared capabilities exist Multiple teams use common templates and platforms Platform becomes a new ticket queue L4 Measured Engineering metrics exist Delivery, reliability, cost, and security can be evaluated with data Metrics replace judgment L5 Adaptive Feedback loops exist Incidents, cost, and delivery data improve platforms and processes Organizational complexity requires governance

Real DevOps maturity is not "having a platform". It is whether platforms, processes, and team behavior continuously reduce production risk.

Once you know your position, score yourself with the Self-Assessment and Growth Path.

docs.title: Self-Assessment and Growth Path
docs.text: Start by separating role boundaries. Not all operations work is DevOps, and not all DevOps work requires building a platform. The key question is whether you can connect system operations with change delivery and improve both through engineering methods.

docs.title: Level Bands
docs.text: Level Common State What You Can Do Independently What Is Still Missing Not DevOps Only uses applications or follows simple deployment steps Operate a few tools by documentation Missing systems, networking, release, and incident foundations Below Ops Can run commands but cannot judge blast radius Restart services, copy files, read simple logs Missing Linux, networking, permissions, backup, and recovery capability Ops Can maintain production systems Hosts, services, logs, backups, routine incidents Missing delivery automation, platform thinking, and reliability design Junior DevOps Can script repeated work Basic CI/CD, container builds, simple deployment Missing production risk awareness and rollback design Mid-Level DevOps Can own a delivery path Pipelines, Kubernetes deployment, monitoring, routine troubleshooting Missing cross-team standards, capacity, security, and cost governance Senior DevOps Can design production-grade solutions HA architecture, canary release, SLOs, incident reviews, platform templates Missing organization-level trade-off and long-term evolution capability DevOps Architect Can define multi-team standards Platform architecture, governance model, golden paths, migration strategy Needs stronger business constraint, cost, and organizational impact judgment DevOps Expert Can handle complex systems and organizational problems Identify wrong paths, build feedback loops, use reviews to upgrade the system Risk is leaving frontline details and only speaking in principles

docs.title: Self-Scoring Matrix
docs.text:
Score each dimension from 0 to 4:

  • 0: You do not understand it and cannot judge independently.
  • 1: You can follow documentation.
  • 2: You can deliver routine work independently.
  • 3: You can design solutions and handle exceptions.
  • 4: You can define standards, guide others, and improve with data.
Dimension 0-1 Points 2 Points 3-4 Points Linux / Network / Storage Only common commands Can troubleshoot common system issues Can design capacity, isolation, recovery, and security baselines Containers / Kubernetes / Cloud Can deploy examples Can maintain routine clusters and services Can design production HA, upgrade, and recovery strategies CI/CD / Artifacts / Environments Can run a pipeline Can maintain release flow Can design safe, reversible, auditable delivery systems Observability / Incident Handling Can read logs and dashboards Can diagnose common incidents Can design alert tiers, SLOs, and review loops Security / Compliance / Cost Knows basic concepts Can run scans and configure permissions Can embed governance into platform and delivery flow Platform / Standardization / Influence Serves only yourself Can package scripts and templates Can serve multiple teams and quantify platform value

docs.title: Score Interpretation
docs.text:
Total Score Judgment Recommendation 0-6 Below Ops or just starting Build Linux, networking, permissions, logging, backup, and incident foundations first 7-12 Ops to junior DevOps Script repeated work and build basic CI/CD and containerization capability 13-18 Mid-level DevOps Focus on reliability, rollback, observability, and shift-left security 19-22 Senior DevOps Work on cross-team standards, platform abstraction, capacity, and cost governance 23-24 Architect / expert candidate Prove system-level impact with real cases, incident reviews, and metrics

Do not judge progress by how many tools you have learned. Better evidence is whether you made releases safer, recovery faster, incidents rarer, cost clearer, and teams less dependent on individual memory.

Once you know your band, use the Content Maturity Map to find what to read next.

docs.title: Core Claim
docs.text:
DevOps capability is a layered stack — but the capability that matters lives in the contracts between layers, not inside any single layer.

Most engineers grow vertically: they get strong at Linux, then containers, then pipelines, then monitoring. That growth feels like progress, and it is. But production rarely fails inside a layer you are weak at. It fails at the seam between two layers you both believe you are good at — where a delivery pipeline meets reliability, where observability is supposed to feed back into delivery, where a platform is supposed to encode organizational judgment. The tools on each side work. The handoff between them was never designed.

This page maps the stack so you can see the layers. Its real purpose is to make the seams visible, because that is where capability is actually tested.

flowchart TB A["Foundation<br/>Linux / Network / Storage / Security baseline"] --> B["Runtime<br/>Containers / Kubernetes / Cloud resources / Middleware"] B --> C["Delivery<br/>Git / CI / CD / Artifacts / Environment governance"] C --> D["Reliability<br/>SLO / Capacity / Change risk / DR / Rollback"] D --> E["Observability<br/>Metrics / Logs / Traces / Alerting / Reviews"] E --> F["Platform<br/>Self-service / Standardization / Reuse / Cost governance"] F --> G["Organizational Feedback<br/>Metrics / Ownership / Decision reviews / Engineering culture"] A -.supports.-> D C -.constrains.-> D E -.feeds back.-> C G -.improves.-> F

docs.title: The Layers
docs.text:
Each layer answers one production question. The artifacts are the easy part to point at; the evidence of capability is what someone can do under production pressure, not what they can install on a calm afternoon.

Capability Domain Core Question Typical Artifacts Evidence Of Capability Foundation Can the system be installed, hardened, and recovered? Baseline images, network plans, storage policies, access controls Under an unplanned host or network failure, you can reason about blast radius and recover without guessing Runtime (Containers & Cloud) Can services be portable, schedulable, and survive node loss? Dockerfiles, Helm charts, Kubernetes clusters, cloud modules You can predict how a workload behaves when a node, zone, or dependency disappears, and design release, scaling, and isolation around that Delivery Engineering Are changes repeatable, auditable, and reversible? CI/CD pipelines, artifact repositories, environment promotion, release gates You lower change failure rate and can reverse a bad change quickly — not just ship faster Reliability Engineering Does failure have boundaries, playbooks, and recovery paths? SLOs, capacity models, degradation plans, drills, incident reviews You convert an incident into a structural change that makes the same class of failure cheaper next time Observability Can a team locate a problem quickly, not just "have monitoring"? Metrics, logs, traces, alert tiers, on-call dashboards A real failure produces the right signal to the right owner; noise goes down while diagnosis speed goes up Platform Engineering Can multiple teams deliver and operate through shared standards? Self-service platform, golden paths, templates, permission models, cost reports Teams need fewer tickets and make fewer repeated mistakes because judgment is encoded into the path, not into your memory Security & Governance Is speed built on controlled risk? Secrets management, audit, supply chain scanning, least privilege, compliance checks Risk controls live inside the delivery flow instead of being bolted on right before release

docs.title: The Seams: Where Capability Is Actually Tested
docs.text:
A layered diagram invites a comforting misreading: that you climb it one level at a time and each level is independent. The dependency arrows in the diagram say otherwise. The hardest, most expensive failures cluster at the transitions between adjacent layers — places where each side works in isolation and the contract between them was never written down.

Seam What each side believes How the gap shows up in production Foundation → Runtime "The host is hardened" and "the cluster is healthy" A node-level limit (file descriptors, conntrack, disk, kernel params) silently caps a workload that scheduled fine; the cluster looks green while pods stall Runtime → Delivery "The pipeline deploys" and "the cluster runs it" Deployments succeed but carry no notion of health, surge, or ordering, so a "successful" rollout degrades a live service without ever failing the pipeline Delivery → Reliability "The change shipped" and "the system is reliable" Delivery automation has no reverse gear: there is a deploy path but no rehearsed, owned rollback, so the first bad change becomes an incident instead of a non-event Reliability → Observability "We have SLOs" and "we have monitoring" Alerts fire on symptoms nobody owns, or the signal that would prove an SLO breach is not collected, so reliability targets exist on paper but cannot be defended in real time Observability → Delivery "We have dashboards" and "we ship fast" Incident and alert data never re-enters the delivery process, so the same regression ships again; monitoring becomes a record of repeated mistakes rather than a feedback loop Platform → Organization "We built a platform" and "teams should adopt it" The platform encodes tooling but not ownership or judgment, so it becomes a new ticket queue and a single point of contention instead of removing toil

The pattern is consistent: each side is individually competent, and the failure lives in the undocumented assumption between them. This is why an engineer who is "strong at everything" on paper can still preside over fragile production — strength was measured inside layers, but production charges the seams.

docs.title: Why This Is Misjudged
docs.text:
Capability is usually self-assessed and hired-for as a vertical tool inventory: how deep is your Kubernetes, how good is your Terraform, how fluent is your Prometheus. That inventory is real and necessary, but it systematically overrates people who have never had to own a seam.

Three forces keep the misjudgment alive:

  • Seams have no owner. A layer has an obvious expert; the contract between two layers usually belongs to no one, so no one is graded on it.
  • Seam failures are invisible until an incident. A missing rollback path, an uncollected SLO signal, or a platform with no ownership model all look fine in a demo. They only surface under real pressure, long after the work was called "done."
  • Tools are legible; judgment is not. It is easy to verify that someone installed a stack and hard to verify that they designed the handoff correctly, so reviews drift toward what is easy to check.

docs.title: Counterexamples
docs.text:
This claim does not mean intra-layer depth is unimportant or that every team must master every seam:

  • A small system with one team, one service, and a fully GitOps-managed stack has already paid down most seam risk structurally — the contracts are version-controlled and visible. Here, vertical depth genuinely is the constraint.
  • Early in a career, building vertical depth is exactly right; you cannot reason about a seam between two layers you do not yet understand.
  • Some seams are legitimately out of scope for a given role. The point is not that everyone owns every contract, but that someone must, and an unowned seam is a latent incident regardless of how strong the adjacent layers are.

The risk concentrates specifically when adjacent layers are each strong, the contract between them is implicit, and no one is accountable for it.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Read the stack top to bottom to find your weakest layer; read it seam by seam to find your most dangerous gap. They are rarely the same place.
  • When you assess a system (or a candidate, or yourself), ask seam questions, not inventory questions: What happens to a live service during a "successful" deploy? Who owns rollback, and have they rehearsed it? Which SLO can you actually defend with collected data right now? Does incident data change the next deploy?
  • Treat every undocumented assumption between two layers as a latent incident, and give it an explicit owner before production does it for you.
  • Grow vertically early; grow across the seams as you become senior. The move from senior to architect is largely the move from owning layers to owning contracts.

docs.title: How This Maps To The Site
docs.text:
This topology is the source of the site navigation, and each section is one part of the stack:

  • System Design covers the foundation and runtime layers.
  • Change Delivery covers the delivery layer and its seam with reliability.
  • Reliable Operations covers reliability and observability, including the feedback seam back into delivery.
  • Platform Governance covers the platform and organizational-feedback layers.

To turn this map into a judgment about yourself, continue to Value and Maturity and the Self-Assessment and Growth Path.

docs.title: Cases & Reviews
docs.text:
Cases and reviews prove experience. Deep dives, case studies, decision reviews, and incident reviews record the judgment that only someone who has actually done the work can write.

This content proves experience more strongly than success stories. It preserves scale, constraints, symptoms, trade-offs, and outcomes, but removes identifying information.

docs.title: Deep Dives
docs.text:
• Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration
  • Rollback Is a Product Capability, Not a Kubernetes Command

Deep dives are the primary proof of this section. They are problem-first essays that expose hidden operating contracts, failure modes, and validation models.

docs.title: Case Studies
docs.text:
• Anonymous Kubernetes Upgrade Case
  • Anonymous Object Storage Migration Case
  • Anonymous Application Containerization Migration Case

The anonymous cases are public-safe secondary examples. They should not be treated as the strongest evidence until they are rewritten with sharper metrics, decision points, and failure evidence.

docs.title: Anonymization Boundary
docs.text:
• Can retain: service scale, node count ranges, failure symptoms, migration phases, constraints, decision reasons, and outcome ranges.
  • Must remove: customer names, company names, real domains, accounts, secrets, private repositories, internal IPs, personal data, and non-public business details.

docs.title: Pending
docs.text:
• Decision Reviews: how a wrong technology decision was discovered and corrected.
  • Incident Reviews: timeline, symptoms vs root cause, misjudgment, structural fixes.

Decision and incident review formats are still under construction. See the Content Maturity Map.

docs.title: Anonymous Application Containerization Migration Case
docs.text: All identifiers in this case are generic. No original service names, hostnames, domains, images, or credentials are preserved.

docs.title: Core Lesson
docs.text: The hard part of containerization is rarely the Dockerfile. It is the implicit state the old host carried for you — local files, ambient config, startup order, and cron-style side jobs. Containers make those assumptions explicit, and that is exactly where the migration breaks if you do not surface them first.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: A legacy application stack ran on manually managed hosts and needed to move to a containerized deployment model for repeatable deploys and easier scaling. The team owned the application and its deployment but had limited tolerance for downtime.

docs.title: Starting State
docs.text:
• Services deployed and configured by hand on long-lived hosts.
  • Configuration spread across local files, environment, and operator memory.
  • Some local on-disk state and log paths assumed to be persistent.
  • Startup order between services was implicit, not declared.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• The service had to stay available during the migration.
  • Credentials could not move into the new platform in plaintext.
  • Rollback to the original hosts had to remain possible until the new deployment was proven.

docs.title: Plan
docs.text:
1. Inventory every service: ports, dependencies, data paths, startup order, and cron/side jobs.
  2. Build images from pinned base images; no floating latest.
  3. Externalize configuration (env/config maps) and move credentials into secret management.
  4. Define explicit health/readiness checks.
  5. Deploy to staging and run smoke tests.
  6. Cut over traffic gradually.
  7. Keep the original host deployment as the rollback path until stable.

docs.title: Execution
docs.text: The team containerized one service at a time, starting with a stateless component. Configuration was pulled out of host files into environment/config; secrets moved to a secret manager rather than being baked into images. Health checks were added so the orchestrator could tell "started" from "ready". Each image was validated in staging before any production traffic shifted.

docs.title: Problems Encountered
docs.text:
• Hidden local state: a service wrote files to a local path the host had always provided. In a container this vanished on restart until it was moved to a managed volume.
  • Implicit startup order: one service assumed a dependency was already up; containers started in parallel, so it crash-looped until readiness gating was added.
  • Config drift surfaced: settings that lived only on the host (and only in one operator's memory) had to be discovered by diffing behavior, not by reading a file.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: All services migrated with no sustained outage. The cutover was gradual and the original hosts were retained through an observation window, so each issue was caught against a working fallback rather than in a one-way move.

docs.title: Reusable Lessons
docs.text:
• Inventory dependencies, config, and on-disk state before writing any Dockerfile — that is where the risk is.
  • Make startup order explicit with readiness probes; do not rely on timing.
  • Move secrets to a secret manager as part of the migration, never into images.
  • Migrate one service at a time and keep the old deployment as a live rollback path until validation passes.
  • Treat "config only one person knows" as a migration risk to surface early.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Docker Host Operations Standard

docs.title: Anonymous Kubernetes Upgrade Case
docs.text: All identifiers in this case are generic. No original service names, domains, regions, accounts, repositories, or customer details are preserved.

docs.title: Core Lesson
docs.text: The control-plane version bump is the part everyone plans for, and rarely the part that breaks. The real risk lived in the add-ons that had to move in lockstep with the new version, and in how worker nodes were replaced. Treating add-ons as first-class upgrade objects, and using new node groups as the rollback boundary, is what made this safe.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: An organization ran a Kubernetes cluster on an older minor version, hosting multiple stateless services, ingress routes, monitoring components, and stateful dependencies. It needed to move to a newer supported version with minimal interruption.

docs.title: Starting State
docs.text:
• Single cluster, older supported minor version.
  • Mixed workloads: stateless services, ingress, monitoring, and stateful components on persistent storage.
  • Core add-ons (CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CSI, ingress controller, observability agents) at versions tied to the current Kubernetes version.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• The target version required add-on compatibility checks.
  • Some workloads depended on ingress and load-balancer behavior.
  • Stateful components used persistent storage and could not tolerate data loss.
  • Monitoring and alerting had to stay available throughout the change.
  • Worker nodes could not all be replaced at once without risking capacity pressure.

docs.title: Plan
docs.text:
The upgrade was modeled as a phased migration, not a single switch:

  1. Build a baseline inventory of workloads, nodes, ingress, PVCs, add-ons, and alerts.
  2. Freeze unrelated infrastructure changes for the window.
  3. Upgrade the control plane via the platform-supported method.
  4. Upgrade core add-ons to target-compatible versions.
  5. Create a new worker node group on the target version.
  6. Migrate workloads by cordoning and draining old nodes in small batches.
  7. Validate after each batch.
  8. Keep old nodes until the observation window passed, then remove them.

docs.title: Execution and Validation
docs.text:
The team validated in three layers and stopped at the first failed layer:

Layer Examples Cluster health API readiness, node readiness, system pods, events. Workload health Pod readiness, rollout status, logs, probes, job completion. Business smoke tests Login, core API calls, scheduled jobs, external callbacks, dashboard visibility.

Workloads were moved in small batches; application health was confirmed against each batch before draining the next set of old nodes.

docs.title: Problems Encountered
docs.text:
• Add-on compatibility, not the control plane, caused the friction. Add-ons that lagged the new version produced DNS and networking flakiness until they were upgraded to match.
  • Storage pressure was nearly misdiagnosed as an upgrade bug. A PVC nearing capacity produced symptoms that looked upgrade-related; checking PVC headroom up front would have avoided the confusion.
  • Ingress and CSI behavior needed explicit test cases — they were the workloads most sensitive to the change.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: The cluster reached the target version with no sustained outage. Because old node groups were retained through the observation window, every issue was caught against a working fallback, and no irreversible storage change was made in the same window.

docs.title: Reusable Lessons
docs.text:
• Add-on compatibility usually carries more practical risk than the control-plane version change itself.
  • New node groups give a cleaner rollback boundary than in-place node mutation.
  • Ingress, CSI drivers, and monitoring agents deserve explicit test cases.
  • Check PVC capacity before migrating, so storage pressure is not mistaken for an upgrade fault.
  • Every critical alert should link to a runbook before the maintenance window opens.
  • Treat the control-plane upgrade as forward-only; build rollback from node groups, manifest snapshots, and app-level rollback.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Anonymous Object Storage Migration Case
docs.text: All identifiers in this case are generic. No original bucket names, domains, regions, customer data, or access credentials are preserved.

docs.title: Core Lesson
docs.text: Matching object counts and total size proves you copied something, not that you copied it correctly. Integrity needs sample checksums and a real application read; and the moment that actually carries risk is the write cutover — before it, rollback is trivial, and after it, rollback means reconciliation.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: An engineering team needed to move application files from one object storage provider to another while preserving availability and minimizing data inconsistency. The application both read and wrote objects continuously.

docs.title: Starting State
docs.text:
• Live object storage on the source provider, under continuous read and write traffic.
  • Objects organized by prefixes that mapped to application meaning.
  • No maintenance window long enough to "stop, copy everything, switch".

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• The application had to stay available; a long freeze was not acceptable.
  • Data inconsistency between source and target had to be minimized.
  • Access had to be granted to the target without leaking long-lived credentials.

docs.title: Plan
docs.text:
The migration used an incremental sync model so writes could continue during the bulk copy:

  1. Inventory source buckets and prefixes.
  2. Estimate object count and total size.
  3. Create target buckets and access policies.
  4. Run the initial bulk sync.
  5. Compare object counts and sample checksums.
  6. Run incremental sync for objects changed during the bulk copy.
  7. Switch application reads to the target.
  8. Switch application writes to the target.
  9. Keep the source read-only during the rollback window.

docs.title: Execution
docs.text: Bulk sync ran while the application kept serving from the source. After bulk copy, the team reconciled the delta with an incremental sync, then moved reads to the target first (low risk — the source was still authoritative for writes). Only after reads were validated did they cut over writes, which is the irreversible step.

docs.title: Problems Encountered
docs.text:
• Count and size agreed while content did not, in a sample. Equal object counts and total bytes still allowed a few mismatched objects; only sample checksums caught it, prompting a targeted re-sync.
  • Prefix mapping needed review before the first sync. A prefix assumption that did not match the target layout would have mis-filed objects; reviewing the mapping up front avoided a large re-copy.
  • Temporary credentials issued for the migration had to be tracked so they could be revoked immediately afterward.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: The migration completed with reads and then writes moved to the target, no extended outage, and no detected data loss. Source storage was kept read-only through the rollback window, so the risky write cutover had a defined fallback.

docs.title: Reusable Lessons
docs.text:
• Count and size checks are necessary but not sufficient — add sample checksums and an application read test.
  • The write cutover is the true point of risk; sequence reads-then-writes so the dangerous step is last and isolated.
  • Review prefix/path mapping before the first sync, not after.
  • Issue temporary credentials for the migration and revoke them immediately when done.
  • Keep the source read-only through the rollback window so rollback is "repoint", not "reconstruct".

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Terraform IaC Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management

docs.title: Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration
Core Claim
docs.text:
A monitoring migration — moving or rebuilding a Prometheus, Alertmanager, Grafana, and notification stack — is mostly a migration of labels, alert rules, and alert routing, not a migration of binaries.

The binaries are the easy part. Teams reliably get the new Prometheus, Alertmanager, and Grafana running. What breaks is subtler: alerts that silently never route, dashboards that point at the wrong data source, scrape labels that changed so old rules no longer match, and notification templates that either go quiet or leak sensitive data. The system looks migrated because the UIs load, but its job — telling humans when production is unhealthy — has quietly regressed.

docs.title: Why It Is Misunderstood
docs.text:
The migration is planned as an infrastructure task ("stand up the new servers, point things at them, decommission the old ones"), so success is measured by "the new stack is up." That is the wrong success criterion.

The real success criterion is: a real failure still produces the right alert to the right people. That property lives in labels, rule expressions, routing trees, and receiver secrets — none of which are validated by checking that pods are Ready.

Two forces make this worse:

  • Alert rules and routing are usually owned by different people than the platform doing the migration, so continuity assumptions are never written down.
  • The failure is invisible until an incident. A monitoring stack that has stopped alerting looks identical to a healthy quiet system.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
Any monitoring migration operates under constraints that shape the plan:

  • The old stack must stay authoritative until the new one is proven, because you cannot detect a gap with the same system you are migrating.
  • Scrape target labels (job, instance, namespace, app, and application-specific labels) are part of the alerting contract. Changing them changes which alerts fire.
  • Alert rules and Alertmanager routing must be deployed together and version-controlled together; a rule with no matching route is silent.
  • Notification receivers carry secrets (webhook URLs, SMTP credentials, chat tokens) that must move as secrets, not as copied config.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
A migration validated only by "the stack is up" repeatedly produces the same outcomes:

  • An alert that fired daily on the old stack is never seen again on the new one, because a relabel step renamed the label its expression matched on.
  • A dashboard shows data, but from the old data source, so the team believes the new Prometheus is scraping when it is not.
  • A receiver was copied without its secret, so Alertmanager logs delivery failures that nobody reads.
  • A notification template that once included raw request context is carried over and now leaks that context into a chat channel.

The common thread: every one of these passes a "does the UI work" check and fails a "does a real failure alert" check.

docs.title: Migration Checklist
docs.text:
Treat this as the migration's real definition of done:

  • Existing dashboards still answer real operational questions, not just render panels.
  • Prometheus scrape targets and labels are preserved, or changed intentionally with the dependent rules updated in the same change.
  • Alert rules and Alertmanager routing are deployed together from version control.
  • Notification templates do not leak secrets, request bodies, or personal data.
  • Webhook and SMTP endpoints are stored as secrets or platform-managed values, never inline.
  • Application exporters expose the expected application label that rules depend on.
  • The rollback path keeps the old monitoring stack running and authoritative through the observation window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Prometheus target count on the new stack matches the expected inventory, not just "more than zero."
  • All alert rules load without syntax errors.
  • A synthetic alert traverses the full path and reaches the real notification channel.
  • Grafana dashboards are confirmed to use the new data source.
  • A controlled "application down" scenario produces the expected alert with the expected routing — the single most important test.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Cause Silent alert gap A known alert never fires after migration Relabeling changed a label the rule expression matched on Wrong data source Dashboards render but show stale or no data Panels still bound to the old Prometheus Dead receiver Alertmanager logs delivery errors Receiver copied without its secret Routing drop Rule fires but no human is notified Rule has no matching route in the new routing tree Template leak Sensitive data appears in a notification channel Notification template carried over unredacted

docs.title: Counterexamples
docs.text:
This claim does not mean every monitoring change is high risk:

  • Adding a new dashboard or a new exporter alongside an unchanged alerting path is a low-risk additive change, not a migration.
  • Upgrading the Prometheus binary in place, with labels, rules, and routing untouched, is genuinely an infrastructure task and can be validated as one.
  • A fully GitOps-managed stack where rules, routing, and dashboards already live in version control reduces this to a controlled apply — the routing migration risk was paid down earlier.

The risk concentrates specifically when ownership, labels, or routing change at the same time as the servers.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Plan the migration around alert routing continuity, and make "a real failure still alerts the right people" the acceptance test.
  • Run old and new stacks in parallel; let the old one stay authoritative until the new one passes a live failure test.
  • Version-control rules and routing together and migrate them as one change.
  • Treat notification secrets and templates as a security review item, not a copy step.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Loki Logging Standard
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Rollback Is a Product Capability, Not a Kubernetes Command
Core Claim
docs.text:
Rollback is not mainly the ability to run kubectl rollout undo, revert a Git commit, or click a button in a pipeline.

Those actions matter, but they are only the visible tip of the capability. A real rollback capability means the product, data model, runtime architecture, release process, observability, ownership model, and customer communication path are all designed so that a bad change can be stopped or reversed without creating a second incident.

The painful truth is that many teams "have rollback" only for the part of the system that is easiest to reverse: stateless application code. The production risk usually lives somewhere else: database migrations, shared configuration, feature flags, message schemas, external callbacks, cached data, irreversible writes, or client behavior that has already changed.

If rollback is treated as a command, it fails when the command meets state. If rollback is treated as a product capability, the system is designed so recovery has a path before the failure happens.

docs.title: Why It Is Misunderstood
docs.text:
Delivery tooling makes rollback look deceptively simple. Kubernetes can retain ReplicaSets. Git can revert commits. Argo CD can sync an older desired state. A pipeline can redeploy a previous image tag. These are useful mechanisms, but they only answer one narrow question:

Can we put an older artifact back into the runtime?

Production recovery asks a harder question:

After this change has partially interacted with users, data, dependencies, and other services, can the business safely return to a known-good operating state?

That second question is why rollback belongs to product and architecture, not only to DevOps automation.

Teams miss this because rollback is often tested in a clean environment before release, while production failure happens after partial adoption. A migration has run. A message has been consumed. A user has created data with the new validation rule. A cache has been warmed with a new key shape. A downstream service has already observed the new behavior.

At that point, "redeploy the old image" may only change the binary. It may not restore compatibility.

docs.title: The Hidden Rollback Contract
docs.text:
Every release carries a rollback contract, whether the team writes it down or not.

That contract answers:

  • Which parts of the change are reversible?
  • Which parts are forward-only?
  • Which state may be written before rollback?
  • Which clients or downstream services may observe the new behavior?
  • Which metrics prove the rollback recovered the user path?
  • Which automation might reapply the bad version after manual recovery?
  • Who can decide that rollback is safer than continued debugging?

If these questions are not answered before release, they are answered during an incident, under time pressure, with incomplete information.

A mature delivery process makes the contract explicit. A risky process hides it inside hope.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
Rollback design is shaped by constraints that cannot be solved by a pipeline alone:

  • Data compatibility: Old code must be able to read data written by new code, or the rollback is unsafe.
  • Schema evolution: Database migrations need expand-and-contract sequencing when old and new versions must overlap.
  • Feature exposure: If users already saw a new workflow, rollback may require product messaging or a feature flag, not only redeployment.
  • Distributed systems: Different services may roll forward or back at different times, so API and message contracts must tolerate skew.
  • External integrations: Webhooks, callbacks, and partner APIs may observe a new format and keep sending it.
  • Automation ownership: GitOps, CI/CD, autoscalers, and release controllers may undo the emergency recovery if the source of truth is not corrected.
  • Observation window: A rollback that looks healthy for five minutes may still fail after queues drain, caches expire, or batch jobs resume.

These constraints mean rollback must be designed during product and architecture planning, not attached at the end as an operations procedure.

docs.title: Evidence In Production
docs.text:
Rollback failures often have a recognizable shape:

  • The old application version is redeployed, but it crashes because the new version already applied a non-backward-compatible database migration.
  • A feature flag is disabled, but background jobs continue processing messages produced by the new feature.
  • A Kubernetes Deployment rolls back successfully, but Argo CD sees the old commit as drift and reapplies the bad manifest.
  • Error rate drops after rollback, then rises again when delayed queue messages hit a consumer that no longer understands the payload.
  • The backend rolls back, but mobile or browser clients have already cached or submitted data in the new shape.
  • A "safe" config change is reverted, but the cache, CDN, or sidecar still serves the old value.

None of these are failures of the rollback command. They are failures of the rollback contract.

docs.title: Composite Example
docs.text:
Consider a common anonymized pattern.

An application release adds a new optional field to a business object, enables a feature flag for a subset of users, and starts writing the new field into both the database and an asynchronous event. The Deployment becomes unhealthy after release because a downstream call path times out under real traffic. The team redeploys the previous image, and Kubernetes reports a successful rollout.

The user path does not fully recover.

The old application can ignore the new database column, but an older background consumer cannot parse the new event payload. Queue lag rises, retries increase, and the user-facing path looks partially healthy while delayed processing keeps failing. The actual recovery is not only "rollback the image"; it is:

  • Disable the feature flag that creates new writes.
  • Stop or drain the incompatible consumer path.
  • Deploy a consumer version that ignores the new field, or run a safe compensating transform.
  • Revert the GitOps source so automation does not reapply the failed image.
  • Validate the affected user journey, queue lag, consumer error rate, and alert recovery.

The lesson is not that the team should never change the event. The lesson is that the rollback contract should have named the event compatibility risk before release. Without that contract, the team rolled back the Deployment but not the product behavior.

docs.title: 1. Reversibility Classification
docs.text:
Every meaningful change should be classified before release:

Change Type Rollback Model Stateless code change Redeploy previous artifact, validate behavior, block bad version. Configuration change Revert source of truth, reload or restart safely, confirm propagation. Feature flag Disable exposure, confirm background paths also respect the flag. Database schema expansion Roll back application first; keep expanded schema until cleanup is safe. Destructive data migration Treat as forward recovery unless restore or compensating action was planned. API or message contract change Maintain compatibility across versions or gate consumers before producers.

If a release contains a forward-only part, the release should say so explicitly.

docs.title: 2. Backward-Compatible State
docs.text:
Safe rollback usually depends on old and new versions coexisting for a period of time. This pushes teams toward:

  • Additive schema migrations before code depends on new fields.
  • Dual-read or fallback-read logic during transition.
  • Dual-write only when reconciliation and ownership are clear.
  • Message contracts that allow unknown fields.
  • Feature flags that gate writes, not just UI exposure.

The goal is not theoretical elegance. The goal is making the old version still safe after the new version has touched production.

docs.title: 3. Source-Of-Truth Alignment
docs.text:
Rollback fails when runtime state and declarative state disagree.

For GitOps systems, emergency runtime rollback must be followed by a Git revert, image tag correction, or sync suspension according to the platform process. For pipeline-driven systems, the failed version must be blocked so the next promotion does not reintroduce it.

Recovery is incomplete while automation still points at the bad version.

docs.title: 4. Observability That Proves Recovery
docs.text:
Rollback validation must be tied to user impact, not only deployment status.

Useful validation includes:

  • Error rate, latency, saturation, and restart metrics returning to guardrail.
  • The affected user journey passing a smoke test.
  • Queue lag and background job failures stabilizing.
  • Logs showing the release-specific error pattern has stopped.
  • Alert state recovering through the normal alerting path.
  • The bad version no longer receiving traffic.

kubectl rollout status can prove that Kubernetes finished a rollout. It cannot prove the product recovered.

docs.title: 5. Decision Authority
docs.text:
Rollback is also an organizational decision. Someone must be allowed to say:

Stop debugging. Recover the user path first.

That authority should be clear before incidents. Otherwise, teams spend the most expensive minutes debating whether rollback is "giving up" instead of asking whether continued diagnosis is increasing customer impact.

docs.title: Validation Model
docs.text:
A release has credible rollback capability when the team can answer and test these checks:

Check Evidence Previous artifact exists Image, chart, package, or commit is retained and deployable. State is compatible Old version can read or ignore new state written during the rollout. Automation will not fight recovery GitOps or CI/CD source is updated, suspended, or reverted. User path recovered The affected journey passes after rollback. Observability confirms recovery Metrics and alerts return to guardrail through the normal path. Forward-only risk is declared Irreversible migrations or external effects have a planned recovery route. Bad version is blocked The failed artifact cannot be promoted again by accident.

If any row is unknown, rollback is a hypothesis, not a capability.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Symptom Hidden Cause Binary-only rollback Deployment succeeds but errors continue State, config, or dependency behavior changed outside the binary. Incompatible data Old version crashes or corrupts behavior New version wrote state the old version cannot parse. Automation replays failure Bad version returns after manual recovery Source of truth still points at the failed release. Partial rollback One service recovers, another keeps failing API, message, or cache contract changed across service boundaries. False recovery Metrics look normal briefly, then fail later Queues, caches, or scheduled jobs were not validated. Organizational delay Rollback starts too late Authority and rollback criteria were not agreed before release.

docs.title: Counterexamples
docs.text:
Not every release needs a heavy rollback design.

  • A small stateless change behind a feature flag may be safely recovered by disabling the flag and redeploying later.
  • A documentation-only change usually needs review and correction, not production rollback planning.
  • A purely additive database migration that is not yet used by code can often be left in place until a normal cleanup window.

The point is proportionality. The deeper the change touches data, contracts, dependencies, or user behavior, the more rollback must be treated as architecture.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Ask "what state can this release create?" before asking "how do we roll back the artifact?"
  • Treat database, queue, cache, API, and feature-flag changes as part of the same release contract.
  • Design changes so old and new versions can overlap during the observation window.
  • Keep the previous artifact deployable until the release is proven.
  • Validate rollback with the user journey and alert path, not only the platform object.
  • Fix the source of truth immediately after emergency rollback.
  • Record forward-only risks in the release plan instead of discovering them during an incident.

A team that can roll back only the binary has a deployment mechanism. A team that can recover the user path after a bad change has a production capability.
