docs.text:
• Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Change Delivery
docs.text:
Change delivery focuses on one thing: how changes enter production safely, auditably, and reversibly.

Delivery articles must explain pre-release checks, in-release observation, failure criteria, rollback triggers, and access boundaries. Automating commands is only the start; the real goal is to reduce release failure rate.

docs.title: CI/CD and GitOps
docs.text:
• Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard

docs.title: Rollback Design
docs.text:
• Rollback Is a Product Capability — why rollback is a product, data, architecture, automation, and ownership contract.
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook — the operational procedure for a Kubernetes Deployment rollback.

docs.title: Pending
docs.text: Release strategies such as canary, release gates, freeze windows, and change failure rate remain a content gap. See the Content Maturity Map.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Argo CD as a GitOps deployment controller for Kubernetes: what belongs in Git, how aggressively to let Argo CD reconcile, and how to recover both an application and Argo CD itself.

The core judgment of this standard:

GitOps moves the risk from "who ran the command" to "what is in Git and how fast it reconciles." selfHeal and prune are not convenience flags — they decide whether a bad commit or a manual hotfix repairs itself or gets reverted under you. Choose them per risk tier, deliberately.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Deploy to Kubernetes and want Git to be the source of truth for cluster state.
  • Run, or plan to run, Argo CD as the reconciliation controller.
  • Need a defensible sync policy, secret-handling model, and recovery plan for GitOps.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • CI (building and testing artifacts). GitOps is the CD half; see the Jenkins-to-Kubernetes pipeline standard for the CI half.
  • Manifest templating choice (Helm vs Kustomize vs plain YAML) beyond how it interacts with sync.
  • Multi-cluster fleet design and ApplicationSets generators beyond the single-Application model shown here.

docs.title: GitOps Principles
docs.text:
1. Git is the desired state. Cluster changes flow through reviewed commits, not kubectl edit.
  2. Secrets are referenced through a secret-management process, never committed in plain text.
  3. Every Application has a deliberate sync policy matched to its risk tier.
  4. Drift is visible and handled by policy, not silently overwritten everywhere.
  5. Notifications expose status, not webhook secrets or tokens.

docs.title: Installation Model
docs.text:
Use the official manifests or Helm chart. For production:

  • Run Argo CD in high-availability mode.
  • Keep Argo CD in its own namespace, separate from application namespaces.
  • Restrict who can create/modify Application and AppProject resources via RBAC.
  • Scope AppProject to the repos, destinations, and resource kinds each team may deploy — this is the real blast-radius control.

Validation:

kubectl get pods -n argocd
kubectl get applications -n argocd
argocd app list

docs.title: Sync Policy by Risk Tier
docs.text:
This is the central decision. Match the policy to the service, not the convenience of the operator.

Tier automated selfHeal prune Use for Production / stateful off (manual sync) off off High-risk services; a human approves each sync. Standard service on on off Most stateless services; drift is auto-corrected, deletions stay manual. Mature, well-tested on on on Strong review + rollback; Git fully owns lifecycle including deletion. 
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: example-service
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: example-team
  source:
    repoURL: https://git.example.com/example/example-repo.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: deploy/kubernetes
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: example-namespace
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: false      # deletions stay manual for this tier
      selfHeal: true    # revert out-of-band drift

Two flags worth understanding before you enable them:

  • selfHeal: true reverts any change made directly to the cluster. That is the point — but it also means an emergency kubectl hotfix will be undone within minutes. The correct emergency path is a commit, or temporarily disabling auto-sync, not fighting the controller.
  • prune: true lets Argo CD delete resources that disappear from Git. Powerful for lifecycle hygiene, dangerous if someone deletes a manifest by mistake. Reserve it for services with strong review.

docs.title: Secret Handling
docs.text:
Plain secrets must never live in Git. Choose one model and apply it consistently:

  • Sealed Secrets (encrypted in Git, decrypted in-cluster), or
  • An external secret operator pulling from a secret manager, or
  • A CSI secrets driver mounting from a vault.

Argo CD Application manifests themselves contain repo URLs, project names, and cluster references — treat exports as sensitive topology.

docs.title: Rollback an application
docs.text:
Because Git is the source of truth, rollback is a Git operation:

# Preferred: revert the commit, let Argo CD reconcile.
git revert <bad-commit> && git push

# Or roll back to a previous synced revision directly:
argocd app history example-service
argocd app rollback example-service <revision>

A direct argocd app rollback is fine for a fast recovery, but follow it with a Git revert so Git and the cluster do not diverge (especially with selfHeal, which will otherwise pull the cluster back to the Git HEAD).

docs.title: Disaster recovery of Argo CD itself
docs.text:
Argo CD is a production dependency; plan for losing it.

argocd admin export > argocd-backup.yaml   # store securely; contains topology
# restore:
argocd admin import - < argocd-backup.yaml

Because desired state lives in Git, a rebuilt Argo CD plus the Git repos can reconstruct the cluster — but only if the Application/AppProject definitions are also captured (in Git or in the export).

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
argocd app get example-service
kubectl get applications -n argocd

Confirm:

  • Sync status is Synced and health is Healthy for changed apps.
  • The synced revision matches the intended Git commit.
  • No unexpected OutOfSync resources (drift) remain after sync.
  • For prune-enabled apps, nothing was deleted that you did not intend.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Check / response App stuck OutOfSync Live drift, ignored diffs, or generated-manifest mismatch argocd app diff; reconcile or add ignoreDifferences deliberately. Sync fails RBAC, missing namespace, missing CRD, admission policy Read the sync error; create prerequisites; check AppProject allow-lists. Emergency fix keeps reverting selfHeal is reverting out-of-band changes Commit the fix, or temporarily disable auto-sync; don't fight the controller. Health stays Unknown Custom resource without health assessment Add a resource health customization. Repo connection fails Bad credentials, network, or unknown host key Re-check repo credentials and connectivity. Unexpected deletion prune: true plus a removed/misgenerated manifest Restore via Git revert; reconsider prune for that tier.

docs.title: Rollback (Operational Summary)
docs.text:
1. Prefer git revert of the offending commit; let Argo CD reconcile back to good state.
  2. For speed, argocd app rollback to a known-good revision, then reconcile Git to match.
  3. If Argo CD itself is the problem, disable auto-sync on critical apps to freeze the cluster while you recover the controller.
  4. Restore Argo CD from argocd admin export; verify apps return to Synced/Healthy.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never commit secrets, webhook URLs, or chat tokens in plain text.
  • Scope AppProject to allowed repos, destinations, and resource kinds — this bounds blast radius.
  • Use SSO and least-privilege RBAC; restrict who can sync production-tier apps.
  • Treat argocd admin export output as sensitive internal topology; store it encrypted.
  • Pin Argo CD versions and patch promptly; it has cluster-wide reconciliation power.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/delivery team.
  • Re-review each Application's risk tier and sync flags on significant change.
  • Test Argo CD disaster recovery (export/import) on a cadence, not just on paper.
  • Re-check AppProject allow-lists when a team's deployment scope changes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Argo CD documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines a safe Jenkins pipeline pattern for building container images and deploying them to Kubernetes: how to keep builds reproducible, how to handle credentials, and how to make production deploys gated, verified, and reversible.

The core judgment of this standard:

The pipeline's job is not "to deploy" — it is to make every deploy reproducible, attributable, and reversible. The single decision that delivers most of that is using an immutable image tag (the commit SHA), so what you tested is exactly what you ship and exactly what you can roll back to.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Build container images and deploy to Kubernetes from Jenkins.
  • Need a consistent stage model, credential model, and rollback path across services.
  • Want CI (this standard) to hand off to GitOps CD, or to deploy directly with verification.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • GitOps reconciliation — if Argo CD owns cluster state, the pipeline pushes the image and updates Git, and Argo CD deploys (see the Argo CD GitOps standard).
  • Jenkins controller/agent infrastructure and scaling.
  • Build-tool specifics for a given language.

docs.title: Pipeline Stages
docs.text:
Use a predictable stage model so failures are localized and promotion is explicit:

checkout → test → build image → scan → push image →
deploy to staging → smoke test → manual approval → deploy to production → verify

Each stage must fail the build on error; a failing test or image scan must stop promotion, not warn and continue.

docs.title: Immutable image tags
docs.text: Tag images with the Git commit SHA (and optionally a release tag), never a moving tag like latest. A moving tag means "what ran in staging" and "what runs in prod" can silently differ, and rollback has nothing precise to point at.

docs.title: Credential handling
docs.text:
Store all credentials in Jenkins credentials or an external secret manager — never hardcoded in Jenkinsfile, scripts, or docs:

  • Git, registry, Kubernetes deploy, notification, and cloud credentials.
  • Inject at the stage that needs them via withCredentials; never echo them.

docs.title: Verified, gated promotion
docs.text:
• Deploy to staging and run a real smoke test before any production stage.
  • Gate production behind a manual approval (or a GitOps PR), recording who approved.
  • After the production rollout, verify — do not declare success on "kubectl applied".

docs.title: Example Jenkinsfile skeleton
docs.text:
pipeline {
  agent any
  environment {
    IMAGE      = 'registry.example.com/example/app'
    NAMESPACE  = 'example-namespace'
    DEPLOYMENT = 'example-service'
  }
  stages {
    stage('Checkout') { steps { checkout scm } }
    stage('Test')     { steps { sh 'make test' } }
    stage('Build')    { steps { sh 'docker build -t ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT} .' } }
    stage('Scan')     { steps { sh 'trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}' } }
    stage('Push') {
      steps {
        withCredentials([usernamePassword(credentialsId: 'registry-credentials',
            usernameVariable: 'REG_USER', passwordVariable: 'REG_PASS')]) {
          sh 'echo "$REG_PASS" | docker login registry.example.com -u "$REG_USER" --password-stdin'
          sh 'docker push ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}'
        }
      }
    }
    stage('Deploy staging') {
      steps {
        sh 'kubectl -n ${NAMESPACE}-staging set image deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} app=${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}'
        sh 'kubectl -n ${NAMESPACE}-staging rollout status deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} --timeout=5m'
        sh 'make smoke-test ENV=staging'
      }
    }
    stage('Approve') { steps { input message: 'Promote to production?' } }
    stage('Deploy production') {
      steps {
        sh 'kubectl -n ${NAMESPACE} set image deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} app=${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}'
        sh 'kubectl -n ${NAMESPACE} rollout status deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} --timeout=5m'
      }
    }
  }
}

The image scan (trivy ... --exit-code 1) blocks promotion on HIGH/CRITICAL vulnerabilities — a scan that only logs is theater.

docs.title: Pre-Production Deploy Checklist
docs.text:
Before the production stage:

  • Image tag is immutable (SHA) and is the exact artifact tested in staging.
  • The target Kubernetes context and namespace are correct.
  • The Deployment has readiness probes and a sane rollout strategy (maxUnavailable/maxSurge).
  • The rollback command is known and tested.
  • Monitoring dashboard and alert ownership exist for the service.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deployment/example-service --timeout=5m
kubectl -n example-namespace get pods -l app=example-service

Confirm:

  • Rollout completed; new pods are Ready with the expected image SHA.
  • Smoke test passed in staging; post-deploy checks (error rate, latency) are within threshold in production.
  • No crash-looping or readiness failures after the rollout window.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Staging ≠ production Works in staging, breaks in prod Moving tag (latest) Use immutable SHA tags end to end. Credentials leaked Secret in logs/repo Hardcoded in Jenkinsfile/echoed Use withCredentials; rotate the exposed secret. "Deployed" but broken Pipeline green, service failing Declared success on apply, not verified Use rollout status + smoke/post-deploy checks. Scan ignored Vulnerable image shipped Scan logs but doesn't fail build Make the scan fail on HIGH/CRITICAL. Rollout hangs Stuck at rollout status Bad readiness probe or insufficient capacity Roll back; fix probe/capacity in staging.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-service
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deployment/example-service --timeout=5m
  1. Because tags are immutable SHAs, rolling back means redeploying the previous known-good SHA (or rollout undo to the prior ReplicaSet).
  2. If the pipeline updates Git (GitOps), roll back by reverting the commit so the controller does not re-apply the bad version.
  3. Verify after rollback exactly as after a deploy — confirm pods, image SHA, and service health.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Keep all credentials in Jenkins/secret manager; never in Jenkinsfile, scripts, or notifications.
  • Scope deploy credentials to the target namespace; do not use cluster-admin in pipelines.
  • Block promotion on image scans; pin base images.
  • Do not include webhook URLs, tokens, or sensitive logs in notification templates — notify with job, build, commit, environment, status, and a runbook link only.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the delivery/platform team.
  • Re-review the gate and scan thresholds when risk tolerance changes.
  • Confirm rollback works (test it) on a cadence, not just on paper.
  • Re-check deploy-credential scope when namespaces or clusters change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This runbook defines how to roll back a Kubernetes Deployment when a release causes user impact, reliability degradation, or operational risk.

The core judgment is:

A rollback is a controlled recovery action, not a panic command.

kubectl rollout undo is only one step. A safe rollback also freezes further change, captures current state, verifies that the previous version is healthy, prevents the bad version from being redeployed, and leaves enough evidence for a later fix.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when any of the following happens after a deployment:

  • Error rate, latency, saturation, or restart count increases beyond the release guardrail.
  • Readiness or liveness failures affect serving capacity.
  • A critical user journey fails after release.
  • A dependency or configuration change makes the new pods unhealthy.
  • The release owner, incident commander, or service owner decides that recovery speed is more important than continuing the rollout.

Do not use rollback as a substitute for diagnosis when the current version is not related to the symptom. First confirm that the release is a plausible cause.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before executing rollback, confirm:

  • You know the namespace and deployment name.
  • You have permission to update the deployment.
  • The previous ReplicaSet or image revision is known or can be inferred from rollout history.
  • The previous version is expected to be safe.
  • No concurrent automation will immediately reapply the bad version.
  • Someone is watching application health while the rollback runs.

If the deployment is managed by GitOps, decide whether the rollback will be performed through Git revert, image tag change, or emergency kubectl operation followed by Git reconciliation.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
Record impact before changing the workload:

kubectl -n <namespace> get deploy <deployment> -o wide
kubectl -n <namespace> get rs -l app=<app-label>
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -l app=<app-label> -o wide
kubectl -n <namespace> describe deploy <deployment>

Capture:

  • Current image or release version.
  • Desired, updated, available, and unavailable replicas.
  • Failing pod names.
  • Last deployment time.
  • Primary symptom and affected user path.

This does not need to delay recovery. A short command transcript is enough.

docs.title: Rollback Decision
docs.text:
Choose the action:

Condition Action New version is clearly causing impact and previous version is known good. Roll back immediately. Rollout is still progressing and only a small subset is affected. Pause rollout, verify impact, then roll back or resume. Bad config or secret caused all versions to fail. Fix config or secret; rollback alone will not recover. Database migration is incompatible with the previous app version. Stop and escalate; use the release-specific recovery plan. Unknown root cause but user impact is severe. Roll back if previous version is likely safe, then investigate.

If the rollback could make data incompatible, do not proceed without the service owner or incident commander.

docs.title: 1. Freeze further changes
docs.text:
Pause automation or stop the pipeline that could reapply the bad release:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout pause deployment/<deployment>

For GitOps-managed services, also suspend or update the source of truth according to the platform process. A manual rollback that GitOps immediately overwrites is not recovery.

docs.title: 2. Review rollout history
docs.text:
kubectl -n <namespace> rollout history deployment/<deployment>
kubectl -n <namespace> rollout history deployment/<deployment> --revision=<revision>

Confirm the target revision. If rollout history lacks useful change cause, identify the previous image from ReplicaSets:

kubectl -n <namespace> get rs -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}'

docs.title: 3. Execute rollback
docs.text:
Rollback to the previous revision:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout undo deployment/<deployment>

Rollback to a specific revision:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout undo deployment/<deployment> --to-revision=<revision>

Then watch status:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout status deployment/<deployment> --timeout=10m
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -l app=<app-label> -o wide

docs.title: 4. Verify service health
docs.text:
Validate both Kubernetes state and user-facing behavior:

kubectl -n <namespace> get deploy <deployment>
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -l app=<app-label>
kubectl -n <namespace> logs deploy/<deployment> --tail=100

Check the service dashboards for:

  • Error rate returning to baseline.
  • Latency returning to baseline.
  • Restart count stabilizing.
  • Successful readiness.
  • Successful business smoke test.
  • Ingress or gateway 5xx rate dropping.

docs.title: 5. Prevent redeployment of the bad version
docs.text:
After recovery, block the bad version from being applied again:

  • Revert or amend the GitOps commit.
  • Disable the failed pipeline promotion.
  • Mark the failed image or release as blocked in the release record.
  • Notify the service owner and platform owner.

Do not close the incident while the automation source still points at the bad release.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The rollback is successful only when all conditions are met:

Check Pass Condition Deployment status Desired replicas equal available replicas. Pods New pods are running the intended rollback image or revision. Readiness No sustained readiness failure. Logs The release-related error pattern has stopped or returned to baseline. Metrics Error rate, latency, saturation, and restart metrics are back within guardrail. User path The affected critical path passes a smoke test. Automation GitOps or CI/CD will not redeploy the bad version.

docs.title: Rollback Is Not Enough When
docs.text:
Rollback alone may fail or be unsafe when:

  • The release included an irreversible or unreviewed database migration.
  • The failure is caused by an external dependency, not the application version.
  • A shared config, secret, or feature flag is broken across versions.
  • Data written by the new version is incompatible with the old version.
  • The previous image has been removed from the registry.
  • The failure is resource exhaustion caused by current traffic, not the release.

In these cases, escalate to the incident commander and service owner. The recovery path may require config repair, traffic shedding, feature flag disablement, database recovery, or image rebuild.

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text:
Escalate immediately when:

  • Rollback does not complete within the expected window.
  • Available replicas stay below the service minimum.
  • Error rate does not recover after the old version is running.
  • Data compatibility is uncertain.
  • Multiple services are affected.
  • You lack permission to update the deployment or source of truth.

Escalation should include namespace, deployment, failed version, target rollback version, current symptoms, and commands already executed.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
After recovery:

  • Record the failed version, rollback version, time to detect, and time to recover.
  • Attach the command transcript or dashboard links to the incident or change record.
  • Create a follow-up item for the root fix.
  • Add or tighten release guardrails if the failure escaped detection.
  • Review whether the deployment strategy, probes, or canary policy should change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Platform Governance
docs.text:
Platform engineering turns repeated delivery, operation, security, and governance work into reusable platform capabilities. It is not wrapping scripts in a UI.

Platform articles must distinguish platform capability from platform interface. If a feature does not reduce cognitive load, repeated risk, or feedback time, it may not deserve to be platformized.

Security is also not an isolated checklist; it is part of the platform and delivery system. Least privilege, credential lifecycle, supply chain risk, audit, and change control should be embedded in DevOps workflows.

docs.title: What Is Here
docs.text:
• GitLab Operations Standard
  • JumpServer and Bastion Access Standard
  • Redash Deployment Standard

docs.title: Pending
docs.text: Golden paths, self-service, cost attribution, and organizational feedback are the flagship direction for platform governance and will be filled as deep content. See the Content Maturity Map.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate GitLab as a source-control and delivery platform.

The core judgment:

GitLab is not only a repository server. It is a production change authority.

When GitLab controls source code, CI/CD, protected branches, release approvals, container registry, and secrets, its availability and governance directly affect production risk.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • GitLab hosts production source code or infrastructure code.
  • GitLab CI/CD deploys workloads.
  • GitLab runners have access to cloud, Kubernetes, registry, or secrets.
  • Multiple teams use GitLab for release governance.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define every GitLab feature or UI setting. It defines the platform operating contract.

docs.title: Protect change authority
docs.text:
Define:

  • Group and project ownership.
  • Protected branches.
  • Required reviews.
  • Merge permissions.
  • Release approvers.
  • Emergency change process.
  • Audit log retention.

No individual user should be the only administrator for a production-critical group.

docs.title: Isolate runners by trust level
docs.text:
Runner access is often more dangerous than repository read access.

Runner Type Use Rule Shared low-trust runner Build and test unprivileged code. No production credentials. Project runner Project-specific pipelines. Scoped credentials only. Protected runner Deploy from protected branches or tags. Runs protected jobs only. Privileged runner Container build or special operations. Isolated, audited, and minimized.

Do not let untrusted merge requests run jobs with deployment credentials.

docs.title: Back up platform state
docs.text:
Backups must cover:

  • Repositories.
  • Database.
  • GitLab configuration.
  • Secrets file.
  • CI/CD variables.
  • Container registry if used.
  • Package artifacts if required.

Restore must be tested. A GitLab backup without restore proof is not a recovery plan.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Regular checks:

gitlab-rake gitlab:check
gitlab-rake gitlab:doctor:secrets
gitlab-ctl status

Operational pass conditions:

  • Web, Git SSH or HTTP, registry, and CI are healthy.
  • Protected branch rules match release policy.
  • Runner fleet is online and scoped.
  • Backups complete and restore test is scheduled.
  • Admin access is reviewed.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Overbroad runner credentials CI compromise becomes production compromise. Scope runners and secrets. No restore test Outage recovery fails. Test restore to isolated environment. Shared admin account No accountability. Use named users and break-glass process. Protected branch disabled Unreviewed code can deploy. Enforce branch protection. Artifact retention unmanaged Storage fills or release evidence disappears. Define retention policy. Secrets stored in repo Credential leak. Use masked variables or secret manager.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery options include:

  • Restore GitLab from tested backup.
  • Disable compromised runner.
  • Rotate CI/CD variables.
  • Revert protected branch change.
  • Restore repository from backup or mirror.

If GitLab is unavailable during an incident, follow the emergency release process and reconcile changes back into GitLab after recovery.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enforce MFA for privileged users.
  • Keep admin membership small and reviewed.
  • Separate build and deploy credentials.
  • Protect deploy tokens and project access tokens.
  • Review webhook targets.
  • Keep GitLab and runners patched.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review after runner incidents, access changes, GitLab upgrades, backup failures, or changes to release governance.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard
  • Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Shell Automation Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate JumpServer or a bastion platform for privileged infrastructure access.

The core judgment:

A bastion is not a login shortcut. It is the audit boundary for privileged operations.

If engineers use it to reach servers, databases, Kubernetes nodes, or network devices, it must enforce identity, authorization, recording, review, and emergency controls.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Engineers need SSH, RDP, database, or web console access to private assets.
  • Direct access to servers should be removed or reduced.
  • Operations require session recording.
  • Vendors or temporary users need controlled access.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not approve shared root access or unmanaged direct SSH. It also does not replace least-privilege cloud IAM.

docs.title: Treat assets as governed inventory
docs.text:
Each asset needs:

  • Owner.
  • Environment.
  • Asset type.
  • Criticality.
  • Allowed access method.
  • Approved user group.
  • Emergency owner.
  • Review cadence.

Unknown-owner assets should not be exposed through the bastion as a convenience.

docs.title: Separate roles
docs.text:
Minimum roles:

  • Platform administrator.
  • Auditor.
  • Asset owner.
  • Operator.
  • Temporary vendor or emergency user.

Administrators should not be the only people who can review their own sessions.

docs.title: Record and review sessions
docs.text:
Privileged sessions should be logged and recorded where policy allows.

Review triggers:

  • Production root or sudo access.
  • Database administrative access.
  • Emergency access.
  • Vendor access.
  • Failed login bursts.
  • Access outside maintenance window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before onboarding an asset:

  • Direct access path is known and reduced where possible.
  • User can access only approved assets.
  • Session recording works.
  • Command audit works where supported.
  • Revocation removes access.
  • Emergency path is documented.

Operational test:

ssh <asset-through-bastion>

Use placeholder hostnames in public docs. Do not publish real asset names.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Shared privileged account No accountability. Map access to individual identity. Direct SSH remains open Bastion audit is bypassed. Restrict direct network paths. No session review Recording exists but risk is not managed. Schedule and trigger reviews. Vendor access never expires Temporary access becomes permanent. Expiry and owner approval. Bastion unavailable Operators lose emergency access. Document break-glass path. Asset owner unknown Access approval has no authority. Maintain asset inventory.

docs.title: Rollback and Revocation
docs.text:
To remove access:

  • Remove user from role or group.
  • Revoke asset authorization.
  • Rotate shared asset credentials if they were exposed.
  • Disable temporary account.
  • Review past sessions if access was risky.

For bastion platform failure, use break-glass access only with incident record and reconcile after recovery.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enforce MFA for privileged users.
  • Restrict admin access to JumpServer itself.
  • Protect session recordings and audit logs.
  • Avoid storing unmanaged shared credentials.
  • Review sudo and root access separately.
  • Do not expose the bastion admin interface publicly without additional controls.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review access monthly or quarterly based on risk, after staff changes, vendor offboarding, bastion upgrades, or privileged-access incidents.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• OpenVPN Access Standard
  • Server Baseline Standard
  • GitLab Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to deploy and operate Redash or a similar internal BI query tool.

The core judgment:

A BI tool is a data access surface, not just a dashboard application.

Redash can make operational data easier to inspect, but it can also expose production databases, credentials, query results, and business-sensitive dashboards.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Internal teams need SQL-based dashboards.
  • Data sources are read-only or analytics-oriented.
  • Query access can be governed by group and data source.
  • Dashboard sharing must be reviewed.

Do not connect Redash directly to production databases with broad write or administrative privileges.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define data warehouse architecture or analytics modeling. It only covers operational deployment controls.

docs.title: Separate Redash from production write paths
docs.text: Use read-only accounts, replicas, warehouses, or curated views where possible. Redash should not be a path for modifying production data.

docs.title: Govern data source credentials
docs.text:
For every data source, record:

  • Owner.
  • Purpose.
  • Credential storage location.
  • Permission scope.
  • Query timeout.
  • Network access path.
  • Review date.

Avoid shared credentials that cannot be rotated or attributed.

docs.title: Control query and dashboard access
docs.text:
Define:

  • Who can create data sources.
  • Who can write queries.
  • Who can publish dashboards.
  • Whether public links are disabled.
  • How sensitive dashboards are reviewed.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Redash web service and worker are healthy.
  • Metadata database is backed up.
  • Data source credentials are read-only where possible.
  • Test query succeeds within timeout.
  • Public sharing is disabled or governed.
  • Admin users are reviewed.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Broad production credential BI compromise exposes or changes production data. Use read-only scoped accounts. Public dashboard sharing Sensitive data leaks. Disable or review public links. Long-running queries Database performance impact. Set query timeout and use replicas. No metadata backup Dashboards and queries are lost. Back up metadata database. Shared admin access No accountability. Use named users and MFA.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback may include:

  • Disable a data source.
  • Revoke a credential.
  • Remove public sharing.
  • Restore metadata database.
  • Route users back to the previous BI tool.

If a credential is suspected leaked, rotate it before restoring access.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish query results or customer data.
  • Protect data source credentials.
  • Use least-privilege database accounts.
  • Audit admin and data-source changes.
  • Keep Redash off the public internet unless additional controls exist.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review data sources, admin access, public sharing, and backups after onboarding new datasets, after access changes, and after security incidents.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• MySQL Operations Standard
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Reliable Operations
docs.text:
Reliable operations is not a collection of monitoring tools. It manages system failure through objectives, signals, response, drills, and review.

The core idea of observability is not "do we have monitoring" but "can the system explain how it broke". Metrics, logs, traces, and events should serve diagnosis, not dashboard count.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Loki Logging Standard
  • ELFK Logging Standard
  • SigNoz Observability Standard

docs.title: Recovery
docs.text:
• Kubernetes PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook
  • Application Maintenance and Health Check Standard

docs.title: Pending
docs.text: SLOs, error budgets, MTTR/MTTD, failure drills, and incident reviews are the flagship direction for this section and will be filled in over time. Incident reviews live under Cases & Reviews.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run an ELFK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Filebeat, Kibana) logging stack so that it stays available and affordable, and does not become an outage or a data-leak vector itself.

The core judgment of this standard:

A logging stack fails in two predictable ways: it fills its own disk and goes read-only (taking your logs down exactly when you need them), or it quietly indexes secrets and personal data into a searchable store. Retention/lifecycle and what-you-index are not tuning — they are the design.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run a self-managed Elasticsearch-based logging stack (ELFK/ELK or OpenSearch-equivalent).
  • Need controlled retention, index lifecycle, and access to logs.
  • Decide what to collect and how to keep the store from filling up.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Choosing between ELFK and a metrics-style logging system (Loki) — see the Loki logging standard for that trade-off.
  • Full-text search relevance tuning or APM.
  • Cluster sizing math beyond the capacity-safety principles below.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Use structured logs (JSON with consistent fields) so logs are queryable, not just greppable.
  2. Control retention with Index Lifecycle Management (ILM) — every index has a defined rollover and delete policy.
  3. Protect against the disk watermark: Elasticsearch goes read-only when disk fills, so capacity and retention must keep you below it.
  4. Restrict Kibana and the Elasticsearch API; logs are sensitive.
  5. Do not index secrets, tokens, credentials, full request bodies, or personal data.

docs.title: Structured logs and a consistent shape
docs.text: Emit JSON logs with stable fields (timestamp, level, service, env, trace id). Do the parsing/enrichment in Filebeat or Logstash, not by indexing free-form text — unstructured logs explode storage and are hard to query.

docs.title: Retention via ILM
docs.text:
Define an ILM policy so indices roll over by size/age and are deleted on schedule. Without it, indices grow until the disk watermark trips and the cluster goes read-only.

PUT _ilm/policy/logs-policy
{
  "policy": {
    "phases": {
      "hot":    { "actions": { "rollover": { "max_size": "50gb", "max_age": "1d" } } },
      "delete": { "min_age": "14d", "actions": { "delete": {} } }
    }
  }
}

Set retention from a deliberate decision (cost vs. how far back you actually investigate), not "keep everything".

docs.title: Capacity and the disk watermark
docs.text: Elasticsearch has disk watermarks (low/high/flood-stage). At flood stage it sets indices read-only — a logging outage that often coincides with the incident you are trying to debug. Keep headroom: size storage for peak ingest × retention, alert on disk usage well before the watermark, and ensure ILM deletion is actually running.

docs.title: Access control
docs.text:
• Put Kibana behind authentication and least-privilege roles; do not expose it openly.
  • Restrict the Elasticsearch HTTP API to trusted networks/clients.
  • Logs frequently contain sensitive data — treat read access as a privilege.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
curl -sS http://<es-host>:9200/_cluster/health?pretty
curl -sS http://<es-host>:9200/_cat/indices?v
curl -sS http://<es-host>:9200/_cat/allocation?v   # disk usage per node

Confirm:

  • Cluster health is green (or a justified yellow), not red.
  • ILM is rolling over and deleting old indices on schedule (_cat/indices shows bounded growth).
  • Disk usage is below the high watermark with headroom.
  • Filebeat/Logstash are shipping; no large ingest backlog.
  • Spot-check that logs do not contain secrets or full sensitive payloads.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Cluster read-only Indexing stops; read_only_allow_delete flag Disk hit flood-stage watermark Free space / delete old indices; clear the block; fix retention. Unbounded growth Disk fills steadily No ILM, or ILM delete not running Apply ILM with rollover + delete; verify it executes. Storage blowup Cost/disk spikes Indexing unstructured/high-cardinality fields Structure logs; drop noisy fields at Filebeat/Logstash. Secrets in index Sensitive data searchable Indexed request bodies/headers/tokens Filter at the shipper; purge affected indices; fix the source. Kibana exposed Unauthorized log access No auth / open network Put Kibana behind auth; restrict network. Red cluster Shards unassigned Node loss, disk, or shard allocation issue Check _cat/allocation and allocation explain; restore capacity.

docs.title: Rollback / Recovery
docs.text:
1. To recover a read-only cluster: free disk (delete or move old indices), then clear the read_only_allow_delete block; afterwards fix ILM/retention so it does not recur.
  2. To roll back an ILM/policy change, restore the previous policy; ILM applies going forward, so verify index ages against the intended retention.
  3. Keep snapshot backups of indices you must retain for compliance; restore from snapshot rather than relying on a live cluster alone.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never index secrets, tokens, credentials, authorization headers, full request bodies, or personal data — filter at Filebeat/Logstash before they reach the store.
  • Restrict Kibana and the ES API; use TLS and authentication.
  • Do not publish real hostnames, endpoints, or index contents.
  • Treat snapshots as sensitive (they contain log data).

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/observability team.
  • Re-confirm retention vs. cost and investigation needs on a regular cadence.
  • Verify ILM rollover/delete is actually running and disk headroom holds.
  • Re-check what fields are indexed when a new log source is onboarded.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Loki Logging Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Elasticsearch Index Lifecycle Management

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to add Prometheus exporters (Node Exporter, database exporters, Nginx, JVM, blackbox, etc.) so that the metrics are trustworthy, the exporter does not become its own risk, and every exporter ships with the alerts that make it useful.

The core judgment of this standard:

An exporter you can scrape is not the same as monitoring you can trust. An exporter with no alerts is decoration; and the difference between "exporter down" and "target down" is the difference between a blind spot and a real outage — your alerts must tell them apart.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Add Prometheus monitoring for a host, database, web server, or application runtime.
  • Standardize how exporters are deployed, labeled, and secured across services.
  • Need a minimum alert baseline so a new exporter is actually actionable.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Prometheus server sizing, federation, or long-term storage design.
  • Dashboard design beyond requiring that one exists.
  • Application-level custom metrics instrumentation (use OpenTelemetry/SDK instrumentation for that).
  • Alert routing and on-call policy (see the Prometheus alerting standard).

docs.title: Exporter Principles
docs.text:
1. Run the exporter close to the target service (same host or sidecar), so exporter health reflects target reachability locally.
  2. Use a least-privilege metrics account for database/service exporters — read-only stats access, never an admin account.
  3. Do not expose exporters publicly; restrict the metrics port to Prometheus.
  4. Add labels that identify service, environment, team, and owner so alerts are routable.
  5. Every exporter ships with a dashboard and alert rules, or it is not done.

docs.title: Exporter Coverage Checklist
docs.text: Exporter Key signals to scrape Node Exporter CPU saturation, memory pressure, disk capacity & inodes, filesystem/read-only errors, network errors, load average MySQL Exporter Up, connections vs max_connections, replication running & lag, slow queries, buffer pool Redis Exporter Up, memory vs maxmemory, evictions, clients, persistence status, replication MongoDB Exporter Connections, locks/contention, replication lag, storage Nginx Exporter Up, requests, active connections, 5xx rate (via status endpoint) JVM Exporter Heap usage, GC pause/frequency, threads, class loading Blackbox Exporter HTTP/TCP/DNS probe success, and TLS certificate expiry

docs.title: Node Exporter specifics
docs.text:
Node Exporter is the canonical host exporter; run it on every Linux host and restrict its port to Prometheus. The signals that most often catch real incidents:

  • Disk capacity and inodes (a full inode table fails writes while df still shows free space).
  • Read-only / failing filesystems.
  • Memory pressure and OOM indicators.
  • CPU saturation and sustained load average.

docs.title: Scrape Configuration Pattern
docs.text:
Attach identifying labels at scrape time so every series is routable and ownable.

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: node
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - <exporter-host>:9100
        labels:
          service: example-service
          env: prod
          team: platform

Do not publish real internal targets or hostnames. Prefer service discovery over hand-maintained static target lists at scale.

docs.title: Required Alert Coverage
docs.text:
A new exporter is not "monitored" until these exist, as applicable:

  • Exporter down — up == 0 for the exporter job. Means you are blind, not that the service is down. Page or warn accordingly.
  • Target down — the service the exporter watches is unreachable/unhealthy. This is the real outage signal.
  • Disk capacity and inode exhaustion.
  • Memory pressure.
  • Error-rate (5xx, failed connections) above threshold.
  • Replication lag (databases).
  • TLS certificate expiry (blackbox).
  • JVM GC pressure / heap exhaustion (JVM apps).

Distinguishing exporter down from target down is the single most important design point. If they collapse into one alert, you cannot tell "we lost visibility" from "the service is failing" — and you will eventually trust a green dashboard that is green only because the exporter died.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
# Exporter serves metrics locally:
curl -sS http://<exporter-host>:9100/metrics | head

In Prometheus:

up{job="node"}                      # 1 = scraped OK, 0 = exporter unreachable
rate(node_filesystem_avail_bytes[5m]) # signal is present and changing

Confirm:

  • The target appears in Prometheus targets as UP with the expected labels.
  • Key metrics for the exporter type are present and non-stale.
  • The dashboard renders and the alert rules load without error (promtool check rules).
  • A deliberately failed scrape (stop the exporter briefly in staging) fires the "exporter down" alert.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Green dashboard, dead service All looks fine but users report errors "Exporter down" and "target down" not separated Split the alerts; alert on up == 0 distinctly. Missing labels Alert fires but nobody owns it No service/team/owner labels at scrape Add identifying labels in scrape_configs. Exporter is a security hole Metrics reachable from untrusted networks Exporter port exposed publicly Bind to private interface; restrict port to Prometheus. Over-privileged exporter DB exporter has write/admin access Reused an admin account Create a least-privilege read-only stats user. Stale metrics Values frozen, no alert Exporter up but not collecting Check exporter logs and the target connection; alert on staleness. Cardinality blowup Prometheus memory/CPU spike High-cardinality labels (per-request IDs) Drop/aggregate high-cardinality labels at scrape.

docs.title: Rollback / Removal
docs.text:
1. Removing an exporter removes visibility — confirm nothing depends on its alerts before doing so.
  2. Remove the scrape job and the associated alert rules together; an orphaned alert rule referencing a gone job is noise.
  3. If an exporter caused load or a security concern, restrict its port or stop it first, then remove cleanly.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Exporters expose service metadata; treat the metrics endpoint as internal and restrict it to Prometheus.
  • Use least-privilege, read-only credentials for database/service exporters; store them in a secret manager.
  • Use TLS or private networking where the metrics are sensitive.
  • Avoid high-cardinality labels that can leak identifiers (user IDs, request IDs) into metrics.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/observability team.
  • Re-check the alert baseline whenever a new exporter type is introduced.
  • Re-validate the exporter-down vs target-down split after Prometheus or exporter upgrades.
  • Review metrics-account privileges when a database exporter is added or rotated.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when you need to add OpenTelemetry traces, and optionally metrics and logs, to a JVM service that runs outside Kubernetes auto-instrumentation — for example a Docker or VM-hosted Java service exporting to an OTLP backend such as SigNoz.

For Kubernetes workloads, prefer the OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Standard instead of attaching the agent by hand.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• A reachable OTLP-compatible backend or collector endpoint exists.
  • The target is a non-critical Java service for the first run, with a known rollback owner.
  • You have a baseline of startup time, latency, error rate, and memory before attaching the agent.
  • The OpenTelemetry Java agent version is chosen and pinned, downloaded only from the official OpenTelemetry release source.
  • A generic, documentation-safe service name is agreed for the example (for example example-service).

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
• Attaching the agent changes JVM startup and adds memory and CPU overhead. Treat it as a change, not a passive setting.
  • Exported telemetry leaves the process. Confirm where it goes before enabling logs export.
  • Keep application runtime JVM options separate from observability options so a telemetry change cannot silently alter heap or GC behavior.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. If using a self-hosted backend, deploy it with a pinned version and remove any bundled sample applications before treating the environment as a real test target. Bundled demo data otherwise pollutes service maps.
  2. Place the pinned agent jar at a controlled path, for example /opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar.
  3. Configure the agent through environment variables rather than baking values into the image:
export JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS="-javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar"
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="example-service"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="http://<otel-collector-endpoint>:4317"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL="grpc"
export OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES="service.namespace=example-namespace,deployment.environment=staging,service.version=1.0.0"
export OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER="otlp"
export OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER="none"
export OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER="none"

JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS is read by every JVM started in that environment, not just the target service. On a shared host this can attach the agent to unintended JVMs. For a single service, prefer adding -javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar directly to that service's launch command instead of exporting JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS globally. 4. Set service.name through OTEL_SERVICE_NAME or resource attributes — never leave it as the default unknown_service:java. 5. Enable metrics and logs export only intentionally. Start with traces only; turn on OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER and OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER as separate, deliberate steps. 6. Start the Java service and generate a known test request.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• The Java process starts with the agent attached and no startup error in the application log.
  • The backend shows the service under the agreed generic service.name, not a leftover production name and not unknown_service:java.
  • Traces appear after the test request, within the agreed time window. Expand the UI time range when first validating.
  • Metrics appear only if the metrics exporter was deliberately enabled.
  • Logs export, if enabled, contains no credentials, tokens, request bodies, or personal data.
  • Startup time, latency, and memory remain within the agreed threshold versus the baseline.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
1. Remove the -javaagent option from JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS (or the launch command) and restart the service.
  2. Confirm the process starts cleanly without the agent and returns to its baseline.
  3. Keep the previous launch configuration until the observation window passes.
  4. Escalate to the backend or platform owner if traces never arrive despite a reachable endpoint and a correctly attached agent — the issue is usually endpoint, protocol, or collector-side, not the application.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Endpoint uses a stable internal address, not a hardcoded internal IP or a developer machine.
  • The example and any committed config use placeholders only.
  • The service name and resource attributes are safe to expose and free of customer or tenant identifiers.

docs.title: Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run Loki as a binary systemd service, with Alertmanager-based alerting, in environments where a full Kubernetes Helm deployment is not appropriate.

It complements the Loki Logging Standard, which covers label design, retention principles, and Kubernetes collection. This page covers the operational boundary specific to a non-Helm install: service isolation, lifecycle, ruler configuration, and keeping notification secrets out of published configuration.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • You run a small or single-node environment where a binary install is simpler to operate than Helm.
  • You need log-based alerting through the Loki ruler and Alertmanager.
  • You want a controlled, reproducible systemd service rather than an ad hoc process.

Prefer the Helm deployment model (see the Loki Logging Standard) for multi-tenant, scalable, or Kubernetes-native environments.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Loki label and cardinality design (see the Loki Logging Standard).
  • Microservice-mode or simple-scalable Loki topologies.
  • Grafana dashboard design.
  • Notification provider internals beyond keeping their credentials out of config.

docs.title: Service isolation
docs.text:
• Run Loki and Alertmanager under a dedicated, non-login service user, not as root and not as a human account.
  • Keep binaries under a controlled, version-pinned install path.
  • Separate the data, configuration, and rule directories so retention, backup, and permission rules can differ per directory.
  • Manage the lifecycle with systemd units, not with nohup or a terminal session.

Example directory layout:

/opt/loki/bin/            # pinned binaries
/etc/loki/                # configuration
/etc/loki/rules/          # ruler rule groups
/var/lib/loki/            # chunk and index data

docs.title: Retention and ingestion limits
docs.text:
• Set retention explicitly and validate it against the data disk capacity before enabling.
  • Set ingestion rate and burst limits so a misbehaving log source cannot fill the disk.
  • Keep compactor retention enabled when you rely on retention for cost control; retention settings without a working compactor do not actually delete data.

docs.title: Ruler and Alertmanager
docs.text:
• Configure the Loki ruler to load rule groups from /etc/loki/rules/.
  • Point the ruler at an internal Alertmanager address only. Use a generic placeholder in any published example:
ruler:
  alertmanager_url: http://<alertmanager-host>:9093
  storage:
    type: local
    local:
      directory: /etc/loki/rules

The Alertmanager v2 API is the default on current Loki, so no extra flag is needed. On much older Loki versions you may still need to opt in explicitly; verify against the version you run.

  • Keep alert routing in Alertmanager, not embedded in each rule.

docs.title: Keep notification secrets out of configuration
docs.text:
This is the highest-risk part of a binary alerting stack.

  • Never embed webhook URLs, bot tokens, chat IDs, or SMTP credentials in Loki, ruler, Alertmanager, or Grafana configuration that could be committed or published.
  • Load notification secrets from a separate file referenced by environment, a secret store, or systemd credentials — not from the tracked config.
  • In any documentation or example, use placeholders only: <webhook-url>, <smtp-host>, <alert-email>.

Example Alertmanager receiver using an environment-provided secret rather than an inline value:

receivers:
  - name: platform-oncall
    webhook_configs:
      - url: ${ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL}   # provided via systemd EnvironmentFile, not committed

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
1. systemctl status loki and systemctl status alertmanager report active services with no restart loop.
  2. The Loki HTTP endpoint responds locally: 
    curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3100/ready
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3100/metrics | head
  3. The ruler loads a test rule group without parse errors (check /loki/api/v1/rules and service logs).
  4. A synthetic alert reaches Alertmanager through a non-secret placeholder receiver during the test.
  5. Grafana queries Loki through the internal service address; the Loki port is not exposed publicly.
  6. Disk usage trend after enabling retention matches the expected steady state.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Cause Correct response Secret in config Webhook or SMTP credential visible in tracked config Notification credentials embedded inline Move to EnvironmentFile or secret store; rotate the exposed credential. Disk fills Loki stops ingesting or the host runs out of space Retention set without validating disk capacity, or compactor disabled Lower retention or expand disk; confirm compactor is running. Silent ruler Rules present but no alerts fire Ruler not pointed at Alertmanager, or rule parse error Check ruler logs and alertmanager_url; validate rule syntax. Public Loki port Loki API reachable from outside the trust boundary Service bound to a public interface Bind to internal interface; place access behind the platform proxy. Log volume spike Sudden storage pressure Debug logging left enabled Set log level back to info or warn before steady-state operation.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Stop the affected service with systemd; the unit file makes start and stop deterministic.
  2. Restore the previous configuration and rule directory from backup.
  3. Restart and re-run the validation steps.
  4. If a secret was exposed, rotate it regardless of rollback success.

Keep the previous configuration and binary version until the observation window passes.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Notification secrets must never live in tracked or published configuration.
  • The Loki API must stay inside the trust boundary; do not expose it directly to the internet.
  • Run services under a dedicated low-privilege user.
  • Do not log credentials, tokens, or full authorization headers into Loki itself.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform or observability team that runs the binary stack.
  • Re-validate retention against disk capacity whenever log volume grows materially.
  • Re-confirm that no notification secret has drifted into tracked configuration on each change.
  • Re-check binary and ruler compatibility on each Loki upgrade.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines a safe baseline for collecting Kubernetes application logs with Loki-compatible tooling.

docs.title: Logging Principles
docs.text:
1. Logs should support troubleshooting without exposing secrets.
  2. Labels should be low-cardinality.
  3. Retention should match compliance and cost requirements.
  4. Application logs should use structured fields where possible.
  5. Sensitive fields must be filtered before storage.

docs.title: Label Guidance
docs.text:
Recommended labels:

cluster
namespace
pod
container
app
environment
team

Avoid labels such as request ID, user ID, email, order ID, or full URL because they create high cardinality or privacy risk.

docs.title: Kubernetes Collection Flow
docs.text:
1. Deploy log collector as a DaemonSet.
  2. Collect container logs from node log paths.
  3. Add Kubernetes metadata.
  4. Drop sensitive or high-cardinality labels.
  5. Send logs to Loki.
  6. Validate queries in Grafana.

docs.title: Deployment Models
docs.text:
Choose the deployment model deliberately; it changes retention, scaling, and access design.

Model Use when Note Single binary / monolithic Small or single-tenant environments Simplest to operate; see the Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard for the non-Helm systemd variant. Simple scalable Moderate volume, want read/write separation Good default for most Kubernetes installs. Microservices High volume, multi-tenant Highest operational cost; only when scale requires it.

docs.title: Helm deployment sequence
docs.text:
1. Add and pin the Helm chart repository and chart version. Do not track latest.
  2. Install or upgrade Loki with an explicit values file under version control.
  3. Install Grafana only if no platform Grafana already exists; otherwise reuse it.
  4. Configure the Grafana data source using the in-cluster service name, not a kubectl port-forward address.
  5. Confirm the collection agent (DaemonSet) is shipping logs before querying by label.

docs.title: Deployment validation
docs.text:
• Loki pods are Ready and the gateway or query frontend is reachable from Grafana.
  • The collection agent targets are healthy.
  • A known pod emits logs and appears in LogQL.
  • The retention setting matches the agreed operational requirement.

docs.title: Deployment failure modes
docs.text: Failure mode Cause port-forward treated as production access Using a developer convenience as an access pattern Storage pressure later Deploying with default retention and never validating disk capacity Unqueryable logs Missing the labels needed for useful queries Duplicate Grafana Installing a new Grafana when a platform Grafana already exists

docs.title: Validation Queries
docs.text:
{namespace="example-namespace"}
{app="example-service"} |= "error"

docs.title: Retention and Cost
docs.text:
Define:

  • Default retention.
  • Extended retention for critical services.
  • Excluded namespaces.
  • Log volume budget.
  • Query performance expectations.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not log credentials, tokens, session IDs, or full authorization headers.
  • Mask personal data at application or collector layer.
  • Restrict access to logs by team and environment.

docs.text:

  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
