docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to enable OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation on Kubernetes workloads without treating it as a blind namespace-wide toggle.

OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation is implemented through the OpenTelemetry Operator and Kubernetes admission mutation. It can add init containers, agents, environment variables, and runtime configuration to new pods. That means it changes application startup, memory use, network egress, and the telemetry data leaving the cluster.

The core judgment of this standard:

Auto-instrumentation is a production change, not an observability toggle. Roll it out one workload at a time, with validation and rollback, the same way you would ship any change that mutates a running pod.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • You run the OpenTelemetry Operator, or plan to install it, on Kubernetes.
  • You want traces, and optionally metrics or logs, from services that cannot be manually instrumented yet.
  • You need consistent runtime agent configuration across multiple services.
  • You want a safe bridge from "no traces" to "manual instrumentation for critical paths".

This is most useful for platform, SRE, and DevOps teams that own observability enablement but do not own every application codebase.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Manual SDK instrumentation inside application code.
  • Designing the trace backend itself. See SigNoz Observability Standard for backend selection.
  • Full sampling strategy design beyond the rollout-safe baseline below.
  • Non-Kubernetes agents for virtual machines or bare metal.
  • Vendor-specific OpenTelemetry distributions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before enabling injection, confirm:

  • A reachable OTLP-compatible collector or backend exists.
  • The OpenTelemetry Operator and its admission webhook are installed and healthy.
  • The target namespace has an Instrumentation resource before the target application pod is created.
  • The first target workload is non-critical and has a known rollback owner.
  • The workload has a latency, error-rate, restart, and resource baseline from before injection.
  • The runtime language is known: Java, Node.js, Python, .NET, Go, Deno, or SDK-only injection.

This standard focuses on application runtime instrumentation. Apache HTTPD and NGINX module injection need a separate webserver-module review because their compatibility and configuration risks are different from application agents.

Do not start with a high-traffic, latency-sensitive, or incident-prone service. Auto-instrumentation is safer as a staged rollout than as a platform-wide campaign.

docs.title: Use a collector as the default boundary
docs.text:
Send application telemetry to an in-cluster OpenTelemetry Collector unless there is a deliberate exception.

The collector gives the platform team one place to control:

  • Backend credentials.
  • Retries and queue limits.
  • Memory limiting.
  • Attribute normalization.
  • Filtering of unsafe span, metric, and log attributes.
  • Export routing to one or more backends.

Do not point workloads at localhost, a kubectl port-forward, a node IP, or a developer laptop endpoint. A production Instrumentation resource must reference a stable service name.

Example endpoint pattern:

http://<otel-collector-service>:4318

Use the fully qualified service name when the collector is in a different namespace from the application.

docs.title: Set the OTLP protocol explicitly; match the collector port
docs.text:
Do not rely on a per-language default OTLP protocol. The default protocol for each auto-instrumentation runtime is version-dependent and has changed across both the language SDKs and the Operator. Treating "Node.js uses gRPC, everything else uses HTTP" as a fixed rule will eventually point a workload at a receiver port the collector is not listening on, and traces will silently disappear.

The reliable model is a three-part contract you set deliberately, not infer:

  1. Protocol — set OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL explicitly in the runtime section of the Instrumentation resource (grpc, http/protobuf, or http/json).
  2. Endpoint port — must match the chosen protocol:
OTLP protocol Collector receiver port Endpoint example grpc 4317 http://<otel-collector-service>:4317 http/protobuf or http/json 4318 http://<otel-collector-service>:4318
  1. Collector receiver — the collector must expose the matching OTLP receiver (gRPC on 4317, HTTP on 4318) for the port you chose.

The http:// scheme on the endpoint is correct for both protocols here; it is the value the agent reads from OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT. What must agree is protocol ↔ port ↔ collector receiver, not the scheme.

Runtime-specific notes that still matter:

  • Python: older Operator versions may require OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL=http/protobuf explicitly; verify during upgrade.
  • .NET: check the runtime identifier for musl-based images.
  • Go: feature-gated, needs OTEL_GO_AUTO_TARGET_EXE, uses an eBPF sidecar, and does not support multi-container pods (see the Go security exception below).
  • Deno: support may require runtime flags; do not treat it as a stable default without testing.
  • SDK-only: protocol and endpoint come from the SDK configuration you inject; match the collector receiver to whatever you set.

Whenever the Operator version is upgraded, re-confirm the injected protocol against the receiver you run. The protocol is a value you set, not an architecture boundary you inherit.

docs.title: Keep injection opt-in
docs.text:
Use per-workload pod template annotations. Do not use broad namespace-wide injection as the default operating model.

For a Deployment, the annotation belongs under:

spec.template.metadata.annotations

A metadata annotation on the Deployment object itself does not mutate pods.

Language-specific opt-in annotations:

Runtime Injection annotation Java instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "true" Node.js instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-nodejs: "true" Python instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python: "true" .NET instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-dotnet: "true" Go instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-go: "true" Deno or SDK-only instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-sdk: "true"

Use the Instrumentation resource name instead of "true" when the namespace contains more than one Instrumentation object:

instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "java-standard"

docs.title: Target the container explicitly
docs.text:
Pods with more than one container need explicit targeting.

For a single language instrumentation, use:

instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/container-names: "example-app"

For multiple languages in the same pod, the Operator requires the multi-instrumentation feature flag and language-specific container name annotations:

instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "true"
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/java-container-names: "example-api"
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python: "true"
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/python-container-names: "example-worker"

Do not let the webhook guess the target container in multi-container pods. It creates a common failure mode: the pod is healthy, the annotation exists, but the service you care about produces no useful telemetry.

docs.title: Keep the first Instrumentation resource minimal
docs.text:
Use one Instrumentation resource per runtime and namespace until the operating model is stable.

Example Java baseline:

apiVersion: opentelemetry.io/v1alpha1
kind: Instrumentation
metadata:
  name: java-standard
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  exporter:
    endpoint: http://<otel-collector-service>:4318
  propagators:
    - tracecontext
    - baggage
  sampler:
    type: parentbased_traceidratio
    argument: "0.05"
  java:
    env:
      # Set the protocol explicitly so it matches the 4318 HTTP receiver above.
      - name: OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL
        value: http/protobuf

This example intentionally starts with a low sampling ratio. A sampling value of "1" traces all traffic and is unsafe as a default for high-throughput services.

The protocol is set in the runtime-specific section (spec.java.env here) rather than left to the SDK default, so the 4318 endpoint, the http/protobuf protocol, and the collector's HTTP receiver all agree. Use spec.nodejs.env, spec.python.env, and so on for other runtimes.

Add runtime-specific environment overrides only when they express a deliberate decision, such as disabling a noisy library or setting a compatibility flag. Do not paste a large vendor example into production without reducing it to the behavior you actually want.

docs.title: Treat Go as a security exception
docs.text:
Go auto-instrumentation is different from Java, Python, Node.js, and .NET. It uses eBPF and requires an injected sidecar with elevated privileges. It also requires the target executable path through OTEL_GO_AUTO_TARGET_EXE or the matching annotation.

Do not enable Go auto-instrumentation unless all of these are true:

  • The target pod has only the Go container you intend to instrument.
  • The executable path is known and stable.
  • The security owner accepts the privileged sidecar requirement.
  • A rollback owner is present during the rollout window.

For many Go services, manual instrumentation is the safer long-term standard.

docs.title: Rollout Procedure
docs.text:
1. Select one non-critical workload and one runtime.
  2. Record the baseline: request latency, error rate, pod restart count, CPU, memory, log volume, and current trace volume if any.
  3. Create or update the runtime-specific Instrumentation resource.
  4. If the target is Go, obtain explicit security approval for the privileged sidecar before continuing.
  5. Validate the operator, webhook, collector service, and Instrumentation resource before touching the workload.
  6. Add the injection annotation to the workload pod template.
  7. Let the normal deployment mechanism create new pods, then watch rollout status.
  8. Validate the pod mutation, telemetry path, application health, and backend load.
  9. Wait through an agreed observation window before annotating another workload.

Example workload annotation:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: example-service
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "java-standard"
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/container-names: "example-app"

The Instrumentation resource must exist before the application pod is created. If the application pod already exists, the webhook cannot retroactively mutate it. A new pod must be created through a rollout.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validate in layers and stop at the first failed layer.

docs.title: Layer 1: Operator and webhook
docs.text:
kubectl -n opentelemetry-operator-system get pods
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations
kubectl -n opentelemetry-operator-system logs -l app.kubernetes.io/name=opentelemetry-operator --container manager --since=10m

Expected result:

  • Operator pods are Ready.
  • The mutating webhook configuration exists.
  • Operator logs do not show injection, certificate, or admission errors for the target namespace.

docs.title: Layer 2: Collector reachability
docs.text:
kubectl -n observability get svc otel-collector
kubectl -n observability get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/name=otel-collector
kubectl -n observability logs deploy/otel-collector --since=10m

Expected result:

  • The collector service exposes the protocol and port required by the target runtime.
  • Collector pods are Ready.
  • Collector logs do not show receiver bind failures, exporter authentication errors, queue saturation, or memory limiter drops during the test window.

docs.title: Layer 3: Instrumentation resource
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace describe otelinst java-standard

Expected result:

  • The endpoint points to the collector service, not localhost.
  • Propagators and sampler are explicit.
  • The runtime-specific section exists when runtime overrides are required.
  • Events are empty or explain a non-blocking condition.

docs.title: Layer 4: Workload mutation
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deploy/example-service
kubectl -n example-namespace get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
kubectl -n example-namespace describe pod -l app.kubernetes.io/name=example-service

Expected result:

  • New pods were created after the annotation was applied.
  • Events show the auto-instrumentation init container or injected agent behavior when applicable.
  • The target container, not a sidecar or unrelated init container, received the instrumentation.
  • Pods are Ready without abnormal startup delay.

docs.title: Layer 5: Telemetry and application health
docs.text:
Validate both observability and service behavior:

  • The backend receives spans from example-service within the agreed test window.
  • Trace service name, namespace, environment, and version attributes are usable for filtering.
  • Request latency, error rate, restart count, and memory remain within the rollout threshold.
  • Log volume does not spike because of agent debug output.
  • Collector queue, dropped spans, and backend ingestion latency remain below alert thresholds.

If application health regresses, roll back. Do not "leave it enabled and watch" unless the service owner explicitly accepts the risk.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Injection happens when pods are created, so rollback must create clean replacement pods.

  1. Remove the injection annotation from the workload pod template.
  2. Remove runtime-specific container-name annotations from the same pod template.
  3. Roll out the workload through the normal deployment mechanism.
  4. Confirm new pods no longer contain the auto-instrumentation init container, injected agent, or injected OpenTelemetry environment variables.
  5. Confirm application latency, error rate, restart count, and memory return to the pre-injection baseline.
  6. Keep the Instrumentation resource until no annotated workload depends on it.

Only remove the Operator or Instrumentation resource after annotated workloads have been cleaned up. Removing the platform component first can turn later pod creation into an admission or startup failure.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Annotation on the wrong object Deployment has an annotation, but new pods are unchanged Annotation placed under metadata.annotations instead of spec.template.metadata.annotations Move annotation to pod template and create new pods. Resource created too late Annotation exists, but injection did not happen Instrumentation was created after the pod was created Create the Instrumentation first, then roll the workload. Collector unreachable Agent logs connection failures and backend has no spans Wrong service name, namespace, port, protocol, or endpoint defaulted to localhost Fix spec.exporter.endpoint; validate collector receiver ports. Wrong runtime annotation Pod mutates incorrectly or not at all Runtime annotation does not match application language Replace with the correct language annotation. Wrong container targeted Pod is healthy, but no useful spans appear Multi-container pod without explicit container targeting Add container-names or language-specific container name annotation. Backend overload Backend latency, dropped spans, or storage spike after rollout Sampling too high or too many workloads enabled at once Lower sampling, pause rollout, and validate collector queue limits. Startup regression Pods take longer to become Ready or crash on startup Agent image/runtime incompatibility or resource pressure Roll back and test the runtime image/version in staging. Sensitive attribute leakage Traces contain tokens, request bodies, headers, user data, or query strings Default instrumentation captured fields that should have been filtered Disable the offending instrumentation or filter at collector and application layers. Go multi-container failure Go workload does not instrument as expected Go auto-instrumentation does not support multi-container pods Do not use Go auto-instrumentation for that pod shape; use manual instrumentation or split workload.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Treat telemetry egress as a data export path. Review destination, authentication, TLS, and retention before broad rollout.
  • Do not capture request bodies, full headers, query strings, authorization headers, cookies, or user identifiers by default.
  • Pin or approve auto-instrumentation images. An injected agent runs inside every annotated pod.
  • Do not enable Go auto-instrumentation without a security exception for its eBPF and privileged sidecar model.
  • Keep backend credentials in the collector, not in each application workload, whenever possible.
  • Limit who can modify Instrumentation resources and workload injection annotations.
  • Apply resource limits to the collector and monitor queue saturation so a backend outage does not turn into node pressure.
  • Use safe resource attributes such as service name, namespace, environment, team, and version; avoid customer, tenant, email, or request-level identifiers.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: platform or observability team.
  • Review on every OpenTelemetry Operator upgrade.
  • Review on every Kubernetes minor upgrade.
  • Review when enabling a new runtime language.
  • Review when trace volume, backend cost, or collector drops exceed the agreed threshold.
  • Revalidate sampling whenever a high-traffic service is added.

docs.title: Related Documents

• SigNoz Observability Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines how to write, review, and operate Prometheus alerts for public reusable DevOps documentation.

docs.title: Alert Design Principles
docs.text:
1. Alert only on symptoms that need human action.
  2. Use warning and critical levels with different urgency.
  3. Include service, environment, owner, and runbook labels.
  4. Avoid alerting on short transient spikes unless the service requires it.
  5. Every critical alert must have a runbook.
  6. Alert messages must be safe to share and must not include secrets.

docs.title: Recommended Labels
docs.text:
labels:
  severity: warning
  team: platform
  service: example-service
  runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook

docs.title: Recommended Annotations
docs.text:
annotations:
  summary: "PVC usage is above warning threshold"
  description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is using {{ $value }} percent of available capacity."
  action: "Check recent growth, confirm owner, and follow the PVC usage runbook."

docs.title: Example Rule: PVC Capacity
docs.text:
groups:
  - name: kubernetes-storage
    rules:
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageHigh
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 85
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: platform
          service: storage
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 85%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 85% usage for 15 minutes."
          action: "Review growth, confirm owner, and prepare cleanup or expansion."
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageCritical
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: platform
          service: storage
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 90%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 90% usage for 10 minutes."
          action: "Execute the PVC usage runbook and notify the service owner."

docs.title: Alert Review Checklist
docs.text:
Before enabling a new alert:

  • The alert has a clear owner.
  • The expression has been tested in Prometheus.
  • The threshold is based on operational risk, not arbitrary values.
  • The for duration filters normal noise.
  • The alert includes a runbook label.
  • The message does not expose secrets, real internal domains, or customer data.
  • The notification route matches severity.
  • The alert has been observed in a staging or test environment when possible.

docs.title: Notification Routing
docs.text:
Use this model:

Severity Route Expected Response info Dashboard or low-priority channel Review during business hours. warning Team notification channel Acknowledge and plan action. critical On-call channel Immediate response.

Public examples should use placeholder channels such as platform-alerts and never include webhook URLs.

docs.title: Common Anti-Patterns
docs.text:
• Alerting on every pod restart without context.
  • Using critical severity for capacity warning.
  • Embedding real hostnames or domains in alert examples.
  • Creating alerts without runbooks.
  • Alerting on metrics that disappear during outages without using absent() where appropriate.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes when and how to use SigNoz or OpenTelemetry-compatible observability stacks.

docs.title: Coverage
docs.text:
• Metrics.
  • Traces.
  • Logs.
  • Service maps.
  • Application latency and error analysis.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
• Define retention.
  • Restrict UI access.
  • Configure OpenTelemetry Collector.
  • Avoid sending secrets in spans or logs.
  • Validate ingestion with a test service.

docs.title: Kubernetes Deployment
docs.text:
Decide first what SigNoz is for: a lab tool, a team observability platform, or a production service. The answer sets the storage, access, and rollback requirements.

Before deploying:

  • Confirm the default StorageClass supports volume expansion and uses a suitable reclaim policy if persistent storage will be used.
  • Pin the chart version.
  • Create a dedicated namespace.
  • Review the resource requests and limits for ClickHouse, the collector, Alertmanager, the frontend, and the query service.

docs.title: Deployment validation
docs.text:
• All SigNoz pods are Ready and the ClickHouse cluster is healthy.
  • ClickHouse tables are initialized before the collector starts ingesting.
  • OpenTelemetry collector pods are running and reachable from instrumented applications.
  • The frontend is reached through a controlled access method, not a kubectl port-forward mistaken for production access.
  • A known test workload sends spans or metrics; expand the UI time range when first validating.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
• helm uninstall the release.
  • Delete PVCs only after explicit approval, because they hold telemetry data.
  • Delete the namespace only after confirming no shared resources live in it.
  • Remove instrumentation annotations or agent configuration from applications that pointed at this stack.

docs.title: Known failure modes
docs.text: Failure mode Cause Collector starts before tables exist ClickHouse schema not initialized Storage cannot grow StorageClass lacks expansion or uses an unsuitable reclaim policy No application telemetry Collector endpoint wrong for the instrumented application Exposure mistaken for production Platform reached only through port-forward

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Standard
  • Java Application OpenTelemetry Instrumentation Runbook
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Loki Logging Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to validate application health during maintenance, deployment, patching, scaling, failover, or infrastructure change.

The core judgment:

A health check is a decision gate, not a green endpoint.

Maintenance is safe only when the team knows what to check before change, what signals to watch during change, and what conditions trigger rollback.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • Deployment windows.
  • OS patching or host reboot.
  • Kubernetes node drain or upgrade.
  • Database, cache, queue, or middleware maintenance.
  • Network, ingress, certificate, or DNS changes.
  • Post-incident recovery validation.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define every application's business logic. It defines the layered health model each application should map to its own checks.

docs.title: Health Layers
docs.text:
Validate in layers:

Layer Question Example Signal Process Is the service running? Process, pod, container, systemd, Supervisor. Dependency Can it reach required dependencies? Database, cache, queue, object storage, API. Readiness Can it accept real traffic? Readiness endpoint, queue consumer state. User path Does a critical journey work? Login, create, read, payment, search, upload. Error budget Is the service within reliability guardrail? Error rate, latency, saturation, burn rate.

Do not rely on process health alone.

docs.title: 1. Capture baseline
docs.text:
Before maintenance, record:

  • Current version or host state.
  • Request rate.
  • Error rate.
  • Latency.
  • Saturation.
  • Queue backlog.
  • Dependency health.
  • Current alerts.

docs.title: 2. Define rollback triggers
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Error rate exceeds guardrail for the observation window.
  • Readiness stays below minimum capacity.
  • Critical user path fails.
  • Queue backlog grows beyond recovery threshold.
  • Dependency connection errors increase.

If rollback triggers are not defined before change, the team will debate them during incident pressure.

docs.title: 3. Monitor during change
docs.text:
Watch both platform and application signals:

curl -fsS https://<service-endpoint>/healthz
curl -fsS https://<service-endpoint>/readyz

For Kubernetes:

kubectl -n <namespace> get deploy,pod
kubectl -n <namespace> get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

docs.title: 4. Validate after change
docs.text: Run the agreed smoke test and observe for the required window. The service is not healthy just because the change command returned successfully.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Health and readiness endpoints return expected status.
  • Critical user journey succeeds.
  • Error rate and latency are within baseline or planned range.
  • No unexpected restart or crash loop.
  • Dependency errors are not increasing.
  • Alerts are clear or acknowledged with owner and deadline.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Only checks /healthz Broken dependency goes unnoticed. Add readiness and dependency checks. No baseline Team cannot tell whether change worsened service. Capture metrics before change. No rollback trigger Recovery decision stalls. Define thresholds before change. Smoke test too shallow User path remains broken. Include critical business journey. Alerts silenced too broadly Real incident hidden. Scope maintenance silence tightly. No post-window observation Delayed failure missed. Set observation duration.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback to the previous safe state when rollback triggers fire and the current change is the likely cause.

Rollback may mean:

  • Revert deployment.
  • Restore previous config.
  • Stop maintenance.
  • Re-enable previous route or DNS.
  • Re-add drained capacity.
  • Restore host or node state.

If rollback cannot recover because data or schema changed, escalate to the service owner and incident commander.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not put sensitive details in public health endpoints.
  • Protect admin and diagnostic endpoints.
  • Do not expose dependency credentials through health responses.
  • Maintenance access should be time-bounded and audited.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review after failed maintenance, unclear rollback decisions, repeated false-green health checks, or new critical user paths.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • System OS Patching Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when the TLS certificate for Harbor, or an equivalent self-hosted container registry, must be rotated — on scheduled renewal, before expiry, or after a certificate or key compromise.

For the upstream detection and expiry-tracking workflow, see the TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• The maintenance window and rollback owner are agreed.
  • The new certificate, private key, and full chain are available and the formats are confirmed (PEM, correct chain order, key matches certificate).
  • You know where the registry terminates TLS: directly in the registry component, or in a fronting reverse proxy or ingress.
  • A backup location for the current certificate and key is prepared.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
• TLS rotation interrupts registry availability only during the reload or restart of the TLS-terminating component. Time it for a low-traffic window.
  • A wrong chain or a key that does not match the certificate breaks every docker login, pull, and push until corrected — including image pulls for running deployments that need to scale or restart.

docs.title: Key Permission Rule (Important Correction)
docs.text:
Some older notes suggest making certificate files broadly readable or world-writable to "make it work." Do not do this. A registry private key that is world-readable or world-writable is a credential leak.

The safe rule:

  • The private key must be readable only by the service account or root-controlled process that needs it.
  • Use restrictive permissions: private key 600 (or 640 owned by the service group that reads it), certificate and chain 644.
  • Never use world-writable permissions on any TLS file.
  • Confirm ownership matches the user the registry or proxy runs as.
# Private key: owner read/write only
sudo chmod 600 /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.key
sudo chown harbor:harbor /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.key

# Certificate and chain: world-readable is fine, not writable
sudo chmod 644 /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.crt

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify the exact TLS termination point and its certificate and key paths.
  2. Confirm the new files: chain order is leaf → intermediate(s), and the key matches the certificate: 
    openssl x509 -noout -modulus -in registry.example.com.crt | openssl md5
openssl rsa  -noout -modulus -in registry.example.com.key | openssl md5
    The two checksums must match. The -modulus form applies to RSA keys only. For an ECDSA certificate and key, compare the public keys instead: 
    openssl x509 -in registry.example.com.crt -noout -pubkey | openssl md5
openssl pkey -in registry.example.com.key -pubout | openssl md5
  3. Back up the current certificate and key to the prepared backup location before changing anything.
  4. Install the new certificate and key at the expected paths.
  5. Apply the restrictive permissions from the key permission rule above.
  6. Reload or restart only the TLS-terminating component (proxy, ingress, or the registry service), not the entire platform, to limit blast radius.
  7. Proceed to validation before announcing completion.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
1. The served chain is the expected new certificate: 
    openssl s_client -connect registry.example.com:443 -servername registry.example.com </dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -issuer -subject -dates
  2. Login succeeds: 
    docker login registry.example.com
  3. Pull and push tests pass: 
    docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
docker push registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.1
  4. No client reports a trust or chain error, including Kubernetes nodes and CI runners that pull from this registry.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
1. Restore the previous certificate and key from the backup taken in step 3.
  2. Reapply the restrictive permissions.
  3. Reload or restart the TLS-terminating component.
  4. Re-run validation against the restored certificate.
  5. Escalate if clients still fail after a confirmed-good chain and matching key — the issue is likely an intermediate certificate missing from the chain or a stale client trust store.

Keep the rollback files until the observation window passes.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Private key permissions are restrictive and ownership matches the running service.
  • No certificate or key material was committed to a repository or pasted into a ticket or chat.
  • The new expiry date is recorded in the certificate tracking workflow.
  • If this rotation followed a compromise, the old key is destroyed after rollback is no longer needed.

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text:
• Harbor Registry Standard
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This runbook defines how to respond when a Kubernetes PersistentVolumeClaim approaches or reaches its capacity threshold.

The core judgment is:

A PVC usage alert is a data-risk alert, not a disk-cleanup ticket.

The response must identify the owning workload, confirm real filesystem usage, understand whether the data is disposable or business-critical, and choose a recovery path that does not silently delete state.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook for alerts such as:

  • KubePersistentVolumeFillingUp
  • KubePersistentVolumeUsageHigh
  • KubeletVolumeStatsUsedBytes threshold alerts
  • NFS or shared filesystem capacity alerts linked to Kubernetes PVCs
  • Application alerts that indicate failed writes caused by full storage

Recommended threshold model:

Severity Suggested Threshold Expected Response Warning 75 percent used or forecast full within 7 days Review owner and growth pattern. High 85 percent used or forecast full within 72 hours Prepare cleanup or expansion. Critical 90 to 95 percent used or writes failing Act immediately and escalate if owner is unavailable.

Adjust thresholds for workload growth rate. A slow archival volume and a rapidly growing upload volume should not share the same operational urgency.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before acting, confirm:

  • Namespace, PVC name, PV name, StorageClass, and owning workload.
  • Whether the volume is block, file, local, cloud disk, or NFS-backed.
  • Whether the data is disposable, cache-like, reconstructable, or business-critical.
  • Whether expansion is supported by the StorageClass and backend.
  • Whether backup or snapshot exists for business-critical data.
  • Whether the application owner is available.

If ownership is unknown and the alert is critical, escalate to the platform owner and incident commander before deleting anything.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
Check whether the application is already affected:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc>
kubectl -n <namespace> describe pvc <pvc>
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -o wide | grep <workload-or-pvc-hint>
kubectl -n <namespace> get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

Look for:

  • Failed writes.
  • Pod restarts.
  • Readiness failures.
  • Application errors related to no space left on device.
  • Ingress or API errors caused by the workload.
  • Queue backlog or delayed jobs.

If user impact exists, treat the alert as an incident, not routine maintenance.

docs.title: Confirm Real Usage
docs.text:
Kubernetes PVC capacity and actual filesystem usage are not always the same signal.

For kubelet volume stats:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc> -o wide
kubectl -n <namespace> describe pod <pod-using-pvc>

From inside an approved diagnostic pod or the application pod:

df -h <mount-path>
du -xh --max-depth=1 <mount-path> 2>/dev/null | sort -h

For NFS-backed PVCs, confirm usage on the NFS server or the platform monitoring path as well. A PVC may request 100Gi, while the real shared filesystem is a larger pool with many claim directories.

Record:

  • PVC requested capacity.
  • Filesystem total, used, available, and percent used.
  • Largest directories or file classes.
  • Recent growth rate.
  • Owning workload and owner.

docs.title: Decision Tree
docs.text:
Choose the response based on data type and growth behavior:

Condition Preferred Action Disposable cache or temporary files consume space. Clean up through the application-approved path. Business-critical data is growing normally. Expand the volume or backend capacity. Business-critical data is growing unexpectedly. Expand only if needed for safety, then investigate growth source. Logs or exports are written to persistent data path. Stop the writer or change path, then clean up approved files. NFS backend pool is filling across many PVCs. Treat as shared storage incident; capacity action belongs to NFS layer. StorageClass does not support expansion. Migrate to a larger volume or new storage profile. Owner is unknown. Do not delete. Escalate and apply only non-destructive mitigation.

docs.title: 1. Identify owner and data class
docs.text:
Find the workload using the PVC:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{range .spec.volumes[*]}{.persistentVolumeClaim.claimName}{" "}{end}{"\n"}{end}' | grep <pvc>

Identify labels, deployment, StatefulSet, CronJob, or Helm release:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pod <pod> --show-labels
kubectl -n <namespace> get ownerreferences <pod>

If the second command is unavailable in your kubectl version, inspect owner references with:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pod <pod> -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences}'

docs.title: 2. Stop unsafe growth if needed
docs.text:
If the volume is filling fast, stop the source before cleaning:

  • Pause the job or CronJob writing the data.
  • Disable the feature flag producing files.
  • Scale down a non-critical writer if approved.
  • Redirect logs or exports away from the persistent path.

Do not scale down stateful systems blindly. For databases, brokers, and queues, follow the service-specific runbook.

docs.title: 3. Clean only approved data
docs.text:
Cleanup is allowed only when the data class is understood.

Safe examples:

  • Expired temporary files owned by the application.
  • Rebuildable cache directories approved by the service owner.
  • Completed export files after retention period.
  • Duplicate diagnostic dumps after incident evidence is preserved.

Unsafe examples:

  • Database directories.
  • Queue segments.
  • User uploads.
  • Object metadata.
  • Files with unknown ownership.

Before deletion, prefer moving a small sample to a quarantine path when the application owner needs proof.

docs.title: 4. Expand when data must be retained
docs.text:
Confirm expansion support:

kubectl get storageclass <storageclass> -o yaml
kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc> -o yaml

If supported, request a larger size:

kubectl -n <namespace> patch pvc <pvc> \
  --type merge \
  -p '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"<new-size>"}}}}'

Then verify:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc> -w
kubectl -n <namespace> describe pvc <pvc>

Check the mounted filesystem after expansion. For some drivers, filesystem resize completes only after pod restart or remount.

docs.title: 5. Migrate when expansion is not available
docs.text:
If the StorageClass cannot expand or the backend is wrong for the workload:

  • Provision a new PVC using the approved storage profile.
  • Stop writes.
  • Copy data with an approved tool and checksum or application-level validation.
  • Start the workload against the new PVC.
  • Keep the old PVC retained until validation and rollback window expire.

Do not delete the old PVC immediately after migration unless the data is explicitly disposable.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The response is complete only when:

Check Pass Condition Usage PVC or backend usage is below the recovery threshold and no longer rising unexpectedly. Application Writes succeed and readiness is stable. Alerts Critical alert cleared or downgraded with an owner and deadline. Data safety No business-critical data was deleted without owner approval. Monitoring Dashboards show the same capacity reality as the application sees. Follow-up Growth cause, owner, and next review date are recorded.

docs.title: Rollback and Escalation
docs.text:
Cleanup rollback is only possible if files were moved or backed up before deletion. If data was deleted, recovery depends on backups or application reconstruction.

Escalate when:

  • User-facing writes are failing.
  • The PVC is above critical threshold and owner is unavailable.
  • Data class is unknown.
  • Expansion fails or stays pending.
  • Backend storage is full even after PVC-level action.
  • Multiple PVCs on the same NFS or shared storage backend are filling.

Include namespace, PVC, PV, StorageClass, workload, usage percent, growth rate, and suspected largest paths.

docs.title: Post-Incident Review
docs.text:
After recovery, answer:

  • Why was the data growth not predicted earlier?
  • Should the threshold or forecast alert change?
  • Does the workload need retention policy?
  • Should logs, exports, or temporary files move to object storage?
  • Is the StorageClass appropriate for this workload?
  • Does the owner mapping need to be added to the platform inventory?

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook
Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when a Linux volume is approaching capacity and must be expanded, typically on:

  • A disk-usage alert (e.g. filesystem > 85%) for a host or data volume.
  • A planned capacity increase ahead of growth.
  • An incident where a full filesystem is causing write failures.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before any destructive command, confirm all of:

  • A verified backup exists. LVM expansion is largely irreversible online; the real rollback is restore. Do not proceed without one.
  • The exact target disk/volume is identified and approved — wrong-disk operations destroy data.
  • The filesystem type is known (XFS vs ext4 — they grow with different tools).
  • There is a change window or explicit approval, and a rollback owner is reachable.

Core judgment: the dangerous part of LVM expansion is not the lvextend — it is acting on the wrong device. Almost all data-loss incidents here come from pvcreate/mkfs on a disk that was actually in use. Identify before you act.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
lsblk                 # block devices, sizes, mountpoints
blkid                 # filesystem types and UUIDs
df -hT                # usage and fs type per mount
sudo pvs; sudo vgs; sudo lvs   # current LVM layout

Identify and write down: the new/empty disk name, the existing volume group, the target logical volume, the filesystem type, and confirm the new disk is not mounted and not already in a VG.

docs.title: 1. Add the new disk to LVM
docs.text:
Confirm the target disk is the new, unused one (e.g. /dev/sdb) — verify with lsblk that it has no partitions, no filesystem, and no mountpoint.

sudo pvcreate /dev/sdb
sudo vgextend example_vg /dev/sdb

docs.title: 2. Extend the logical volume
docs.text:
# Grow the LV into the new free space:
sudo lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/example_vg/example_lv

docs.title: 3. Grow the filesystem (online)
docs.text:
Match the tool to the filesystem; both can grow a mounted filesystem.

# XFS (cannot shrink) — grow by mountpoint:
sudo xfs_growfs /data

# ext4 — grow by device:
sudo resize2fs /dev/example_vg/example_lv

Alternatively, lvextend -r grows the LV and filesystem in one step.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
df -hT                 # new capacity visible on the target mount
sudo pvs; sudo vgs; sudo lvs
dmesg | tail           # no filesystem/IO errors

Confirm:

  • The mountpoint shows the increased size, and free space is reclaimed.
  • The application can write to the expanded path (test a real write).
  • Monitoring/alerting reflects the new capacity and the usage alert clears.
  • No filesystem or I/O errors in dmesg.

docs.title: Rollback / Escalation
docs.text:
• There is no clean online shrink for XFS, and ext4 shrink is offline and risky. Treat expansion as forward-only.
  • If the wrong device was modified or data is damaged, stop immediately and restore from the verified backup; do not attempt to "undo" with more LVM commands.
  • If lvextend succeeded but xfs_growfs/resize2fs failed, the LV is larger but the filesystem is unchanged — safe to retry the grow step after diagnosing; the data is intact.
  • Escalate to the storage/platform owner if the VG has no free space to extend into, or if the volume is on shared/SAN storage where the disk addition is owned by another team.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Capacity headroom is back above the alert threshold with margin.
  • The capacity-growth trend is recorded so the next expansion is planned, not reactive.
  • Update the host's documented disk layout.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Linux Server Baseline Standard
  • Kubernetes PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This runbook handles TLS certificate expiry alerts for public endpoints, ingress resources, or reverse proxies.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when certificate expiry is below the warning or critical threshold.

Recommended thresholds:

Severity Days Remaining Warning 30 Critical 7

docs.title: Initial Triage
docs.text:
Check the remote certificate:

echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates

For Kubernetes:

kubectl get ingress -A
kubectl get secret example-tls -n example-namespace

Do not print or export private keys during triage.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Certificate near expiry Renewal job failed or was not configured. Wrong certificate served Ingress or proxy references the wrong secret or file. Browser hostname warning Certificate SAN does not include requested host. Chain warning Intermediate certificate missing or invalid.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
1. Identify certificate owner.
  2. Confirm renewal method.
  3. Renew certificate through approved CA process.
  4. Update secret or proxy certificate files.
  5. Validate configuration.
  6. Reload ingress controller or proxy if required.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
curl -Iv https://service.example.com

Confirm:

  • Certificate expiry date is extended.
  • Hostname matches.
  • Chain is valid.
  • Application health endpoint works.

docs.title: Closure Criteria
docs.text:
• Certificate is valid past the warning threshold.
  • Monitoring alert has recovered.
  • Renewal process is documented or fixed.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
  • Harbor Certificate Rotation Runbook

docs.title: Standards Manual
docs.text:
Standards and templates are production engineering infrastructure. They reduce repeated mistakes, but they do not replace deep articles or case reviews.

This section organizes all operational standards by technical domain. It is a lookup area, not the conceptual spine of the site. You come here for answers — commands, standards, templates, runbooks — and you do not care whether it is called a standard or a technical KB.

Standards must explain scope, mandatory items, recommended items, exception handling, validation, and maintenance ownership.

docs.title: Containers
docs.text:
• Docker Host Operations Standard
  • BuildKit Build Standard
  • Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Harbor Registry Standard
  • ChartMuseum Helm Repository Standard

docs.title: Databases
docs.text:
• MySQL Operations Standard
  • Redis Operations Standard
  • MongoDB Operations Standard
  • etcd Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to write and operate shell scripts used for DevOps automation.

The core judgment:

A shell script is production automation when it changes infrastructure, data, access, or deployment state.

Small scripts often become operational control planes. They need the same discipline as other automation: clear scope, safe inputs, idempotency, logs, validation, and rollback.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for scripts that:

  • Change servers, containers, Kubernetes objects, or cloud resources.
  • Run in CI/CD jobs.
  • Rotate files, logs, backups, or certificates.
  • Perform cleanup or migration.
  • Are shared between operators or teams.

One-off local exploration does not need the full template, but it must not be promoted into shared automation without review.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not force every task into shell. If the logic needs complex state, parsing, API retries, or long-term maintainability, use a language and library better suited to the job.

docs.title: Start every script with an operating contract
docs.text:
The top of the script or adjacent README must define:

  • Purpose.
  • Target environment.
  • Required permissions.
  • Inputs and defaults.
  • What resources it changes.
  • Dry-run behavior.
  • Validation command.
  • Rollback or recovery boundary.

If the script cannot say what it changes, it is not safe to run unattended.

docs.title: Use strict mode deliberately
docs.text:
Baseline:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -Eeuo pipefail

trap 'echo "error: line ${LINENO}" >&2' ERR

Strict mode reduces silent failure, but it does not replace explicit checks. Handle expected non-zero results deliberately.

docs.title: Validate inputs before changing state
docs.text:
Validate:

  • Required commands exist.
  • Required files exist.
  • Target namespace, host, cluster, or account is explicit.
  • Destructive flags require explicit confirmation or --yes.
  • Output paths do not point to root or empty variables.

Avoid scripts that infer production targets from current shell context.

docs.title: Make repeated runs safe
docs.text:
Prefer idempotent operations:

  • Check before create.
  • Compare before replace.
  • Backup before overwrite.
  • Skip already-complete steps.
  • Treat missing optional resources as expected only when documented.

docs.title: Provide dry-run and audit output
docs.text:
For shared scripts, support:

./script.sh --dry-run
./script.sh --target <target> --yes

Dry-run should show intended actions without modifying state. Logs should include timestamp, target, action, result, and operator context when available.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before approving a script:

bash -n script.sh
shellcheck script.sh
./script.sh --dry-run --target <test-target>

Pass conditions:

  • Static syntax check passes.
  • ShellCheck findings are fixed or justified.
  • Dry-run output is understandable.
  • Test run changes only the intended test target.
  • Failure exits non-zero.
  • Logs show enough evidence for incident review.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Empty variable in destructive path Deletes or edits the wrong target. Use strict mode and explicit path guards. Implicit current context Runs against wrong cluster or account. Require target argument and print target before action. No idempotency Re-running doubles changes or fails midway. Check state before each mutation. Hidden partial failure Pipeline succeeds with failed command. Use pipefail and explicit checks. No logs No one can reconstruct what happened. Log action and result. Secrets in output Credentials leak to CI logs. Mask sensitive values and avoid set -x in shared scripts.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
A script must state its rollback boundary:

  • Reversible by running another command.
  • Reversible from backup.
  • Not safely reversible.
  • Requires manual recovery.

If a script deletes data, rotates credentials, changes firewall rules, or modifies production access, the rollback plan must be reviewed before first production use.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not store credentials in scripts.
  • Do not print tokens or private keys.
  • Avoid curl | bash for production automation.
  • Pin downloaded artifacts and verify checksums where possible.
  • Use least privilege service accounts in CI.
  • Review scripts that run with sudo, cluster-admin, or cloud admin rights.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review shared automation before first use, after incidents, when permissions change, and when target platforms or APIs change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • System OS Patching Standard
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard

docs.title: Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to plan and execute cloud network and object storage migrations across accounts, regions, or providers.

The core judgment:

A cloud migration fails less often because copying is impossible, and more often because routes, identity, DNS, metadata, and rollback were treated as afterthoughts.

Network migration and object storage migration should be planned together when application traffic, private connectivity, and data paths change at the same time.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Moving workloads between cloud accounts, regions, or providers.
  • Rebuilding VPC/VNet network boundaries.
  • Migrating object storage buckets or prefixes.
  • Changing DNS, private connectivity, NAT, or egress paths.
  • Preparing a cutover where application reads and writes move to a new location.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not cover database migration internals, application refactoring, or vendor-specific console steps. It defines the control model for migration safety.

docs.title: Separate migration planes
docs.text:
Plan three planes independently:

Plane Scope Failure Signal Network CIDR, routes, firewall rules, private links, DNS, egress. Application cannot connect, connects to old target, or bypasses policy. Data Object count, size, metadata, encryption, versioning, lifecycle. Missing object, wrong metadata, access denied, stale reads. Application Config, endpoint selection, cutover switch, read/write behavior. Writes split across systems or users hit inconsistent data.

Do not declare migration complete because data copied once. Completion requires traffic and ownership to move safely.

docs.title: Use placeholders in public documentation
docs.text: Public docs must not include real cloud account IDs, VPC IDs, subnet IDs, bucket names, domains, private endpoints, or IP addresses. Use placeholders such as <source-vpc>, <target-bucket>, and <private-service-endpoint>.

docs.title: Build a route and identity matrix
docs.text:
Before data movement, document:

  • Source and target network boundaries.
  • Required application connections.
  • DNS names and where they resolve.
  • Security groups, firewall policy, and route tables.
  • NAT or egress path.
  • Private connectivity approval.
  • IAM or role mapping for migration jobs.

The matrix should show both allowed and deliberately blocked paths.

docs.title: Migrate object storage in phases
docs.text:
Use phased migration:

  1. Inventory source buckets or prefixes.
  2. Classify data sensitivity and retention.
  3. Create target bucket, encryption, lifecycle, logging, and access policy.
  4. Run initial bulk sync.
  5. Run incremental sync until drift is small.
  6. Freeze or dual-write only if the application design supports it.
  7. Cut reads and writes to the target.
  8. Validate, observe, and retain the source for rollback window.

Avoid permanent dual-write unless the application has conflict resolution and ownership rules.

docs.title: Network validation
docs.text:
Validate from the workload perspective, not only from a bastion host:

curl -fsS https://<service-endpoint>/healthz
dig <service-name>
nc -vz <private-service-endpoint> <port>

Expected evidence:

  • DNS resolves to the intended target.
  • Private endpoints are reachable from approved networks only.
  • Egress follows the intended path.
  • Denied paths are actually denied.
  • Logs show the expected source identity.

docs.title: Object validation
docs.text:
Validate:

  • Total object count.
  • Total logical size.
  • Sample checksums.
  • Metadata and content type.
  • Encryption state.
  • Access policy from application role.
  • Application read path.
  • Write path after cutover.

For large stores, use deterministic sampling plus inventory comparison rather than trusting a single sync command summary.

docs.title: Cutover
docs.text:
Cutover must have:

  • Freeze or final sync plan.
  • DNS or config switch owner.
  • Application smoke test.
  • Monitoring owner.
  • Rollback decision window.
  • Communication to affected teams.

Do not cut over if the rollback condition is vague. The team must know what signal triggers rollback and who can decide.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback is possible when the source remains intact and writes have not diverged beyond reconciliation.

Rollback options:

  • Repoint DNS or config to source.
  • Re-enable source route.
  • Replay accepted writes if captured.
  • Restore from retained source copy.

Rollback is risky when:

  • Writes occurred in both source and target.
  • Object versioning or metadata changed incompatibly.
  • Source lifecycle policy deleted old data.
  • Application schema or URL generation changed.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention CIDR or route overlap Traffic reaches wrong or no target. Validate route matrix before cutover. DNS TTL ignored Some clients keep old endpoint. Lower TTL and test resolver behavior. IAM mismatch Sync succeeds but application cannot read. Validate with application role. Metadata lost Downloads, caching, or content type break. Compare metadata, not only bytes. Source deleted early No rollback. Retain source through rollback window. Permanent dual-write Inconsistent objects. Prefer freeze, final sync, and single writer.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Use temporary migration roles with least privilege.
  • Encrypt objects in transit and at rest.
  • Do not copy public access policy by accident.
  • Log data access during migration.
  • Remove migration roles and temporary routes after cutover.
  • Keep migration evidence generic in public documentation.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard before any migration wave, after each cutover, and after any route, DNS, IAM, object metadata, or rollback incident.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Object Storage Migration Case
  • Terraform IaC Standard
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to use BuildKit and Docker Buildx for container image builds without leaking secrets, producing unreproducible images, or hiding supply-chain risk.

The core judgment:

A container build is a supply-chain event, not a packaging shortcut.

BuildKit improves speed, caching, multi-platform output, and secret handling. Those benefits matter only when build inputs, secrets, cache sources, image tags, and validation are controlled.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Building container images in CI/CD.
  • Enabling docker buildx or remote BuildKit builders.
  • Using build cache across pipelines.
  • Building multi-platform images.
  • Passing private dependency credentials to a build.
  • Publishing images to a shared registry.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define application Dockerfile style in full, registry governance, or runtime Kubernetes deployment policy. It only covers build-time controls.

docs.title: Separate build inputs from deployment state
docs.text:
The build should consume source code, Dockerfile, lockfiles, build arguments, and approved secrets. It should not depend on mutable host state, local developer files, or runtime .env.

Required inputs:

  • Dockerfile path.
  • Build context.
  • Base image tag or digest.
  • Dependency lockfiles.
  • Target platforms.
  • Image tag and immutable digest output.
  • Build secret names, not secret values.

docs.title: Pin base images and dependencies
docs.text:
Use explicit base image versions. For highly controlled environments, pin by digest and refresh through a scheduled update process.

Avoid:

  • FROM ubuntu:latest
  • Unpinned package managers in release builds.
  • Downloading scripts from arbitrary URLs during build.
  • Embedding environment-specific configuration into image layers.

docs.title: Use secret mounts, not build args
docs.text:
Build arguments become visible in image history and build metadata. Use BuildKit secret mounts for private dependency tokens or package repository credentials.

Pattern:

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.7
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=package_token \
    ./scripts/install-private-deps.sh

Build command:

docker buildx build \
  --secret id=package_token,env=PACKAGE_TOKEN \
  --tag registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0 \
  --push \
  .

Do not print secret values in build logs.

docs.title: Treat cache as shared state
docs.text:
Cache improves speed but can carry stale or unsafe assumptions.

Cache Type Use Rule Local cache Developer builds. Never relied on for release correctness. Registry cache CI speed-up. Namespace per project or trust boundary. Inline cache Rebuild optimization. Publish only for approved images.

Invalidate cache deliberately when base images, package indexes, lockfiles, or security patches change.

docs.title: Produce traceable output
docs.text:
Every release image should have:

  • Immutable digest.
  • Source revision.
  • Build timestamp.
  • Builder identity.
  • Target platform.
  • SBOM or equivalent dependency inventory where available.
  • Vulnerability scan result or exception record.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before publishing a release image:

docker buildx version
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --tag registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0 --push .
docker buildx imagetools inspect registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0

Pass conditions:

  • Build completes from a clean runner.
  • Image digest is recorded.
  • Target platforms match the release plan.
  • No secret values appear in logs or image history.
  • Vulnerability scan result is attached to the release record.
  • A smoke test runs against the built image, not a local container.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Secret passed as build arg Secret can leak through history or metadata. Use BuildKit secret mounts. Unpinned base image Rebuild changes without source change. Pin versions or digests. Shared cache across trust boundaries One project influences another build. Scope cache by repository and trust domain. Multi-platform image untested Wrong architecture fails at runtime. Inspect manifest and smoke-test target platform. Build downloads mutable scripts Supply-chain drift. Vendor scripts or verify checksums. Image pushed without scan Known vulnerabilities ship silently. Make scan part of release gate.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback uses the previous approved image digest. Do not rebuild the old tag and assume it is identical.

Keep:

  • Previous digest.
  • Dockerfile revision.
  • SBOM or dependency inventory.
  • Registry retention long enough for rollback.

If a base image vulnerability triggers rebuild, treat the new image as a new release even when application code did not change.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not use privileged remote builders without an owner and isolation model.
  • Keep build credentials scoped to the registry or package repository they need.
  • Avoid mounting the host Docker socket into untrusted build jobs.
  • Do not copy .git, local credentials, or developer caches into the image context.
  • Keep .dockerignore reviewed.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard when enabling a new builder, changing cache strategy, adding private dependencies, adding a platform, or changing registry promotion rules.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Docker Host Operations Standard
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate ChartMuseum or a similar self-hosted Helm chart repository.

The core judgment:

A Helm repository is a deployment input. If it is mutable or unaudited, releases become hard to trust.

ChartMuseum is useful for bounded internal chart distribution, but it must have authentication, publishing control, retention, backup, and version immutability expectations.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use ChartMuseum when:

  • A team needs a simple private Helm chart repository.
  • Registry-native OCI chart storage is not yet available or not approved.
  • Chart publishing and consumption are internal and bounded.

Prefer OCI registry-based chart storage when it matches the platform registry standard.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define Helm chart authoring style, application release policy, or a full artifact registry strategy. It covers repository operation and publishing control only.

docs.title: Control publishing
docs.text:
Only CI/CD or approved release owners should publish charts. Publishing from laptops should be treated as an exception.

Each chart version should map to:

  • Source revision.
  • Release owner.
  • Chart version.
  • Application version.
  • Validation result.

docs.title: Protect repository access
docs.text: Require TLS and authentication for private charts. Separate read and write credentials.

docs.title: Retain and back up charts
docs.text: Back up chart storage and repository index. Define retention for old versions, but keep rollback versions long enough for production recovery.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
helm repo add example https://charts.example.com
helm repo update
helm search repo example
helm pull example/<chart-name> --version <version>

Pass conditions:

  • Repository index is reachable.
  • Chart version can be pulled.
  • Write access is restricted.
  • Old rollback chart still exists.
  • Backup includes chart packages and index.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Chart version overwritten Release is not reproducible. Treat versions as immutable. Shared publish credential No accountability. Use CI identity or named owner. No TLS Chart and credentials exposed. Require HTTPS. Retention deletes rollback chart Rollback blocked. Keep rollback retention window. Repository index corrupt Deployments fail. Back up index and storage.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text: Rollback requires the previous chart version and compatible values. Do not overwrite chart versions to roll back; deploy the previous version explicitly.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not embed secrets in chart templates or values.
  • Protect write credentials.
  • Review chart dependencies.
  • Avoid publishing internal hostnames or environment-specific defaults in public examples.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review when adding publishers, changing storage backend, migrating to OCI registry, or after failed chart deployment.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Harbor Registry Standard
  • Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • BuildKit Build Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines when Docker Compose is an acceptable operating model and how to run Compose stacks without turning them into undocumented production platforms.

The core judgment:

Docker Compose is useful for bounded stacks, not for hidden orchestration.

Compose is strongest when the service boundary is small, the host is explicit, the data model is understood, and recovery is documented. It becomes risky when teams use it as an invisible replacement for scheduling, high availability, secrets management, and production deployment control.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use Docker Compose for:

  • Local development and integration environments.
  • Demonstrations and disposable test stacks.
  • Small internal services with low availability requirements.
  • Transitional workloads before Kubernetes or a managed service migration.
  • Single-host tools where the failure domain is accepted.

Do not use it for workloads that require multi-node scheduling, automated failover, horizontal autoscaling, strong tenant isolation, or complex release governance.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not replace Kubernetes, service mesh, managed database, or full platform operations standards. It also does not approve publishing real .env files, production hostnames, private registry URLs, or credentials.

docs.title: Define the stack contract
docs.text:
Every Compose stack needs a short contract:

Area Required Decision Owner Team or person responsible for deployment, logs, backup, and restore. Host Where the stack runs and whether it is disposable. State Which volumes contain business data and which are cache-like. Exposure Which ports are reachable and from where. Secrets Where runtime secrets come from; never commit .env. Recovery How to restart, restore, or rebuild the stack. Upgrade How images are pinned and promoted.

If nobody owns restore, the stack is not production-ready even if the containers are running.

docs.title: Pin images and keep build separate from run
docs.text:
Use pinned image tags or digests for services. Do not let a production-like stack pull latest.

Compose may build images for development, but production-like stacks should consume images produced by a controlled build pipeline. This keeps build credentials, source code, and runtime deployment separate.

docs.title: Treat volumes as data contracts
docs.text:
Named volumes and bind mounts must be documented.

Volume Type Use Requirement Named volume Normal single-host persistent data. Backup and restore path required. Bind mount Host-integrated files, configs, or uploads. Exact host path must be owned and permissioned. tmpfs Temporary sensitive or disposable runtime data. Must not hold business data.

Do not delete a volume until the data class and owner are known.

docs.title: Keep network exposure deliberate
docs.text:
Avoid publishing every container port to the host. Prefer an internal Compose network and expose only the edge service.

Document:

  • Host ports.
  • Protocols.
  • Allowed clients.
  • Reverse proxy or TLS boundary.
  • Health endpoint.

docs.title: Standard service pattern
docs.text:
services:
  app:
    image: registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped
    env_file:
      - .env
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - app-data:/var/lib/app
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "100m"
        max-file: "3"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:8080/healthz"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3

volumes:
  app-data:

Publish .env.example with placeholder keys. Never publish the actual .env.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Run before deployment or after change:

docker compose config
docker compose pull
docker compose up -d
docker compose ps
docker compose logs --tail=100 app

Pass conditions:

  • docker compose config renders without surprises.
  • All intended services are healthy or explicitly one-shot.
  • Only intended ports are exposed.
  • Logs are bounded and readable.
  • State volumes are visible and backup scope is known.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention latest image drift Restarts deploy unreviewed versions. Pin tags or digests. Undocumented bind mount Data loss during host cleanup. Inventory host paths and owners. No log limits Host disk fills. Set log rotation options. Port sprawl Internal services become reachable. Expose only edge ports. .env committed Secret leakage. Commit only .env.example; use local secret distribution. No restore test Backup confidence is false. Run restore verification for stateful services.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback is safe only when the previous image and data format are compatible.

Use:

docker compose pull
docker compose up -d

after changing the image tag back to the approved previous version.

Escalate before rollback when the release changed database schema, volume layout, or file format. In those cases, recovery may require restoring data, not only reverting containers.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Keep .env files out of git.
  • Avoid privileged containers unless there is a documented host integration reason.
  • Do not mount the Docker socket into application containers.
  • Run containers as non-root where images support it.
  • Limit host port exposure.
  • Keep registry credentials outside the Compose file.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review a Compose stack before first production-like use, before image major upgrades, after host incidents, and before migrating the workload to Kubernetes or a managed service.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Docker Host Operations Standard
  • BuildKit Build Standard
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes installation, maintenance, cleanup, and recovery practices for Docker hosts.

docs.title: When To Use
docs.text: Use this standard for single-host Docker workloads, development hosts, lightweight internal services, and build machines. For production orchestration, prefer Kubernetes or another managed scheduler.

docs.title: Installation Principles
docs.text:
1. Install Docker from trusted repositories.
  2. Pin versions when reproducibility matters.
  3. Keep Docker data root on a volume with enough capacity.
  4. Configure logging limits before running long-lived containers.
  5. Avoid running business-critical services from unreviewed Compose files.

docs.title: Host Preparation
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /etc/docker
sudo mkdir -p /var/lib/docker

Recommended daemon configuration:

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "100m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2"
}

Apply:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
docker info

docs.title: Routine Maintenance
docs.text:
Check disk use:

docker system df
df -h /var/lib/docker

List resources:

docker ps -a
docker images
docker volume ls
docker network ls

docs.title: Disk Pressure Cleanup (guarded)
docs.text:
Use when build cache, old images, stopped containers, or unused networks consume disk.

Preconditions:

  • docker system df reviewed to see what is actually reclaimable.
  • Critical images and containers identified.
  • Current deployment rollback images confirmed available locally or in the registry.
  • Volume cleanup explicitly approved or excluded.

Safe cleanup order (least to most risky):

docker container prune -f   # 1. stopped containers
docker network prune -f     # 2. unused networks
docker image prune -f       # 3. dangling images only (does NOT remove tagged images)
docker builder prune -f     # 4. build cache

Reclaiming "old" tagged images requires docker image prune -a, which removes every image not used by a running container — including the rollback images identified in the preconditions. Use it only after confirming those rollback images are available in the registry, and only with explicit approval:

# High risk: removes all unused images, including local rollback images.
# Only after rollback images are confirmed available in the registry.
docker image prune -a

Skip volume cleanup unless the volume ownership and data lifecycle are known. docker volume prune is shown without -f on purpose, so its interactive confirmation stays as a last guard against deleting stateful data:

# High risk: only with explicit approval and known-disposable volumes.
docker volume prune

Validation:

  • docker system df shows the expected reclaimed space.
  • Running containers remain healthy.
  • Rollback images are still available locally or in the registry.
  • The disk alert clears.

Failure modes:

Failure mode Cause Lost fast rollback Removed an image needed for rollback Data loss Pruned a volume holding stateful data Disrupted release Ran cleanup during an active deployment

docs.title: Docker Network Recovery and Reset
docs.text: Symptoms of Docker network issues include containers failing to resolve names, bridge network conflicts, services becoming unreachable after IP range changes, or an incorrect bridge CIDR.

docs.title: Low-risk checks (do these first)
docs.text:
docker network ls
docker network inspect bridge
ip addr
iptables -S

docs.title: Low-risk recovery
docs.text:
1. Stop affected non-critical containers.
  2. Remove only unused Docker networks.
  3. Restart Docker.
  4. Recreate containers from reviewed Compose or deployment files.

docs.title: Guarded bridge reset (higher risk)
docs.text:
Use a full bridge reset only when the bridge is corrupted, conflicts with host routing, or uses an incorrect CIDR.

Preconditions:

  • Maintenance window approved.
  • Running containers identified.
  • /etc/docker/daemon.json backed up.
  • New bridge CIDR chosen and checked against host, VPN, Kubernetes, and cloud ranges.
  • Remote access does not depend on the Docker bridge being reset.

Procedure:

  1. Stop Docker cleanly (systemctl stop docker).
  2. Confirm no critical container is expected to keep running.
  3. Update /etc/docker/daemon.json with the approved bridge CIDR ("bip" or "default-address-pools") and log rotation settings.
  4. Restart Docker (systemctl start docker). Docker rebuilds its default bridge from the new configuration on start; in the normal case you do not delete the bridge by hand.
  5. Only if the stale docker0 interface persists after Docker is stopped and blocks the new CIDR, remove it explicitly while Docker is stopped — never while it is running: 
    sudo ip link set docker0 down
sudo ip link delete docker0
  6. Validate bridge address, container networking, NAT, and service reachability.

Rollback: restore the previous daemon.json from backup and restart Docker.

Do not delete /var/lib/docker as a normal repair step. Data deletion is a separate action that requires its own backup and approval gate; never include it in routine network repair.

docs.title: Compose File Safety
docs.text:
Before running a Compose stack:

  • Review images and tags.
  • Replace hardcoded credentials with environment variables or secret references.
  • Confirm volumes and bind mounts.
  • Confirm exposed ports.
  • Add logging limits.
  • Add restart policies.
  • Document backup and restore for stateful services.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard describes safe operation of Harbor or similar private container registries.

docs.title: Core Controls
docs.text:
• Enable authentication.
  • Use project-level permissions.
  • Enable vulnerability scanning where available.
  • Configure retention policies.
  • Back up registry metadata and image storage.
  • Use TLS for all registry traffic.
