  • Do not publish robot account tokens.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text: Area Check TLS Certificate valid and renewal documented. Storage Object storage or persistent volume backed up. Access Admin account protected, robot accounts scoped. Retention Unused tags cleaned safely. Replication If used, credentials stored securely.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
docker login registry.example.com
docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
docker push registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.1

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Harbor Certificate Rotation Runbook
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate etcd safely as the source of truth for Kubernetes and other distributed systems: how quorum determines availability, why disk latency and database size quietly cause outages, and how to take a snapshot you can actually restore.

The core judgment of this standard:

etcd is not "a database the cluster uses" — for Kubernetes it is the cluster's memory. Losing quorum stalls the control plane; losing the data loses the cluster. A snapshot you have never restored is not a recovery plan.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run a self-managed etcd cluster (standalone or as the Kubernetes control-plane store).
  • Need a backup/restore and disaster-recovery procedure for cluster state.
  • Plan an etcd or Kubernetes control-plane upgrade or member change.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Managed Kubernetes, where the provider owns control-plane etcd. You still need application-level backups (Velero etc.); etcd internals are the provider's.
  • Application data modeling on top of etcd.
  • Tuning beyond the production-critical settings below.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Treat etcd as critical state with strict change control.
  2. Use an odd number of members (3 or 5) so the cluster keeps quorum when one member is lost.
  3. Put etcd on fast, low-latency disks — etcd is fsync-bound, and slow disks cause leader changes and request timeouts.
  4. Take a verified snapshot before any cluster change.
  5. Monitor leader changes, disk fsync/backend-commit latency, database size, and raised alarms.

docs.title: Quorum and member count
docs.text: etcd needs a majority (quorum) of members to serve writes. With 3 members it tolerates 1 failure; with 5 it tolerates 2. Even member counts add cost without improving fault tolerance and can split votes. Spread members across failure domains.

docs.title: Disk and size are the silent killers
docs.text:
• etcd commits every write to disk; high fsync/backend-commit latency manifests as frequent leader elections and etcdserver: request timed out.
  • The database has a quota (--quota-backend-bytes, default ~2 GiB). When exceeded, etcd raises a NOSPACE alarm and goes read-only — a cluster-wide outage. Stay well under the quota; compact and defragment on a schedule.
# compaction (keep current revision), then defragment to reclaim space:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl compact <rev>
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl defrag
# if a NOSPACE alarm is set, clear it after reclaiming space:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl alarm list
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl alarm disarm

Defrag is disruptive per member (it blocks that member briefly); do it one member at a time, starting with followers.

docs.title: Backup: Snapshot, Proven by Restore
docs.text:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl --endpoints=<endpoint> \
  --cacert=<ca> --cert=<cert> --key=<key> \
  snapshot save /backup/etcd-snapshot.db

# verify the snapshot is consistent:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /backup/etcd-snapshot.db --write-out=table
  • Take snapshots on a schedule and before every cluster change (upgrade, member add/remove).
  • Store snapshots encrypted and off the etcd hosts; they contain all cluster state, including Secrets.
  • Restore-test on a schedule into an isolated environment. A snapshot that has never been restored is a hope, not a backup.

docs.title: Disaster Recovery (Restore)
docs.text:
Restoring etcd is destructive and must be coordinated — for a Kubernetes control plane, the API server must be stopped while you restore.

  1. Stop the etcd (and dependent API server) processes on the target.
  2. Restore the snapshot into a new data directory on each member with matching peer URLs.
  3. Start etcd as a new cluster from the restored data, then restart the API server.
  4. Validate health and that cluster state matches the snapshot point in time.

Do this from a runbook, with an owner, never improvised during an incident.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health --cluster
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status --cluster --write-out=table
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl alarm list

Confirm:

  • All endpoints are healthy; exactly one leader; no excessive leader changes.
  • Database size is well under quota; no NOSPACE/CORRUPT alarms.
  • For Kubernetes, the API server is responsive and controllers reconcile normally.
  • The latest snapshot passed a restore test within its interval.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Lost quorum Writes fail; control plane stalls Too many members down / even count Restore a member or recover from snapshot; use odd counts. NOSPACE / read-only Cluster refuses writes DB exceeded backend quota Compact, defrag, then alarm disarm; raise quota deliberately. Frequent leader elections Timeouts, instability Slow disk fsync, network latency Move to fast disks; check network; isolate noisy neighbors. Snapshot won't restore Restore fails Never restore-tested / inconsistent snapshot snapshot status to verify; schedule restore tests. Cert/peer mismatch Members won't join Wrong peer URLs or certs on restore Restore with matching peer URLs; protect/rotate certs.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Always snapshot before a cluster change so you have a known-good point.
  2. To roll back a failed member change, recover the member from the pre-change snapshot and rejoin.
  3. To roll back catastrophic data loss, perform the disaster-recovery restore from the most recent verified snapshot.
  4. Protect and rotate etcd peer/client certificates after any recovery.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never publish real etcd endpoints, certificates, keys, snapshots, or member names.
  • Snapshots contain all cluster state including Kubernetes Secrets — encrypt and access-control them like secrets.
  • Enable mutual TLS between peers and clients; restrict who can reach the client port.
  • Limit who can run snapshot, defrag, alarm, and member operations.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/cluster team.
  • Verify a successful snapshot restore test each interval.
  • Re-check database size, quota headroom, and disk latency on a regular cadence.
  • Re-confirm the DR runbook before each control-plane upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • etcd disaster recovery documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run MongoDB safely in production: the topology that gives real availability, the write-concern choices that decide whether an acknowledged write can still be lost, and a backup you have actually restored.

The core judgment of this standard:

A single mongod is not "MongoDB in production" — it is a database with no failover and a write concern that can acknowledge data you are about to lose. Availability and durability in MongoDB are configuration decisions (replica set + write concern), not defaults you inherit.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run self-managed MongoDB (VM, bare metal, or container/operator).
  • Need to choose a topology and write/read concern for a given durability requirement.
  • Plan a version upgrade, host move, or migration.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Schema/document modeling and index design.
  • Managed MongoDB (Atlas-style) internals — use the provider's HA and backup contract; the write-concern and restore-validation judgment still applies.
  • Sharding design beyond noting when to consider it.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Run a replica set (odd number of voting members) in production — never a standalone for data you cannot lose.
  2. Enable authentication and role-based access; bind only to private interfaces.
  3. Choose write concern and read concern deliberately per workload; they are the durability/consistency dial.
  4. Prove backups by restoring them, and record the restore time.
  5. Monitor replication lag, connections, locks/contention, storage, and oplog window.

docs.title: Topology: replica set, not standalone
docs.text: A replica set provides automated failover (a member is elected primary if the current primary fails) and a source for backups/reads. Use an odd number of voting members (commonly 3) so elections have a quorum. Spread members across failure domains (hosts/zones).

docs.title: Durability is set by write concern
docs.text:
This is the decision most teams get wrong by leaving it default.

Write concern Acknowledged when Loss risk w:1 Primary has it (not yet replicated) A primary failover can lose the last acknowledged writes w:majority A majority of members have it Survives a single-member failover w:majority, j:true Majority and journaled to disk Strongest common durability

For data that must survive a node loss, use w:majority. Reserve w:1 for caches/derived data where loss is acceptable, and write that decision down.

docs.title: Authentication and access
docs.text:
• Enable auth; create least-privilege roles (application users get DB-scoped read/write, not cluster admin).
  • Bind to private interfaces only; never expose MongoDB to the public internet.
  • Keep credentials in a secret manager; never publish connection strings, users, passwords, or internal hosts.

docs.title: Backup and Restore
docs.text:
For small/medium datasets, logical backup is portable:

mongodump --uri="mongodb://<user>@<host>/?authSource=admin" --out /backup/mongodb
# restore:
mongorestore --uri="mongodb://<user>@<host>/?authSource=admin" /backup/mongodb

For large datasets, prefer filesystem/volume snapshots of a stopped or fsync-locked secondary, or a managed/continuous backup. Whatever the method:

  • Encrypt backups and restrict who can read them (a backup is a full copy of the data).
  • Restore-test on a schedule into an isolated instance; verify collection counts and an application-level check. Record the restore time — it is your real recovery objective.

docs.title: Upgrade and Migration
docs.text:
MongoDB upgrades must follow the supported version path and the featureCompatibilityVersion (FCV) rules — skipping versions or raising FCV prematurely can block rollback.

  1. Confirm the supported upgrade path; do not skip major versions.
  2. Take a verified backup first.
  3. For a replica set, upgrade secondaries first, then step down the primary and upgrade it last (rolling upgrade keeps the set available).
  4. Raise FCV only after the whole set is upgraded and you are confident you will not roll back.
  5. Validate (below) before considering it done.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
mongosh --host <host> --eval 'rs.status()'      # member states, who is primary
mongosh --host <host> --eval 'rs.printSecondaryReplicationInfo()'   # lag
mongosh --host <host> --eval 'db.serverStatus().connections'

Confirm:

  • One PRIMARY and healthy SECONDARY members; no member stuck in RECOVERING/DOWN.
  • Replication lag is near zero and within the oplog window.
  • The latest restore test passed within its interval.
  • Connections are well below the limit; no sustained lock contention.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Acknowledged write lost Data gone after failover w:1 on durable data Use w:majority for data that must survive failover. No failover Writes fail when a node dies Standalone, or even member count / lost quorum Run an odd-numbered replica set across failure domains. Replication falls behind Secondary lags, oplog rolls over Undersized oplog, slow disk, heavy writes Resize oplog; fix disk; reseed the secondary. Backup won't restore Restore fails / incomplete Never restore-tested Schedule restore tests; validate counts. Upgrade can't roll back Stuck after upgrade FCV raised too early Raise FCV only after committing to the version. Exposed instance Unauthorized access Bound to public interface / no auth Bind private; enable auth; restrict network.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep a verified backup and the prior binaries until post-change validation passes.
  2. For a failed rolling upgrade (before FCV raised), step down and reinstall the prior version on the affected member; let the set re-sync.
  3. If data was corrupted, restore from the pre-change backup into a clean instance and re-point the application.
  4. Rotate credentials exposed during the change.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enable auth and TLS; bind to private interfaces.
  • Use least-privilege roles; separate admin from application users.
  • Encrypt and access-control backups.
  • Never publish connection strings, credentials, or internal hostnames.
  • Restrict who can run administrative commands and trigger elections.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the database/platform team.
  • Re-confirm write-concern choices when a workload's durability requirement changes.
  • Verify a successful restore test each interval.
  • Re-confirm the supported upgrade path and FCV plan before each major upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • MySQL Operations Standard
  • Redis Operations Standard
  • MongoDB production notes

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines safe, reusable practices for running self-managed MySQL in production: how to set up an instance, how to back it up so the backup is actually recoverable, how to migrate or upgrade without data loss, and how to recover under pressure.

The core judgment of this standard:

A backup you have never restored is a hypothesis, not a backup. Treat restore time and restore success — not backup completion — as the metric that matters.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run self-managed MySQL (bare metal, VM, or container) where you own backup and recovery.
  • Plan a version upgrade, host move, or cross-environment migration.
  • Need a defensible account, backup, and recovery posture for an audit or incident review.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Managed MySQL (RDS-style) internals. Use the provider's backup, failover, and point-in-time-recovery contract; this standard's account model and migration validation still apply.
  • Query and schema design, indexing, or application-level performance tuning.
  • Provider-specific commands, which belong in a cloud appendix, not here.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Prefer a managed database service when operational maturity or on-call depth is limited.
  2. Separate administrative accounts from application accounts; grant least privilege.
  3. Never publish initial root passwords, generated credentials, or connection strings.
  4. Prove backups by restoring them on a schedule, not by checking that the backup job exited 0.
  5. Make major upgrades and migrations controlled changes with a window, a rollback plan, and an owner.

docs.title: Installation Baseline
docs.text:
For self-managed MySQL:

  • Use a supported MySQL version with a defined end-of-life date.
  • Run as a dedicated mysql system user; keep data on a dedicated volume.
  • Set character set and collation explicitly (utf8mb4 unless you have a specific reason).
  • Enable slow query logging and a sane long_query_time.
  • Manage the service through systemd so restart and boot behavior are predictable.
[mysqld]
datadir=/var/lib/mysql
socket=/var/lib/mysql/mysql.sock
character-set-server=utf8mb4
collation-server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci
slow_query_log=ON
long_query_time=1
max_connections=500

Size max_connections to what the host can actually serve; an unreachable connection ceiling just converts a load spike into a different failure.

docs.title: Account Model
docs.text:
Use least privilege and scope hosts tightly:

CREATE USER 'app_user'@'10.%' IDENTIFIED BY '<from-secret-manager>';
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON example_db.* TO 'app_user'@'10.%';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
  • The password placeholder must be supplied through a secret manager, never committed.
  • Application users get DML on their schema only — not ALL PRIVILEGES, not *.*.
  • Administrative and migration accounts are separate and used deliberately, not shared with the app.

docs.title: Backup Standard
docs.text:
Choose the backup method by dataset size and recovery objective.

Method Good for Restore characteristic Logical (mysqldump / mysqlpump) Small/medium datasets, portability, single-table recovery Slower restore; replays SQL Physical (e.g. Percona XtraBackup) Large datasets, fast full restore Fast restore; version/layout sensitive Binary logs Point-in-time recovery on top of a base backup Replays to a target timestamp

Logical backup example:

mysqldump --single-transaction --routines --triggers --events example_db > example_db.sql

For every backup, document: type, schedule, retention, encryption, restore-test frequency, and the owner/escalation path. Combine a periodic base backup with binary logs when you need point-in-time recovery.

docs.title: Prove the backup
docs.text:
On a schedule, restore the latest backup into an isolated instance and validate it:

  • Row counts on critical tables match expectations.
  • Application-level integrity checks or checksums pass.
  • Record the time to restore — it is your real recovery objective, and it only gets worse as data grows.

docs.title: Migration and Upgrade
docs.text:
Before:

  • Confirm source and target versions and the supported upgrade path (do not skip unsupported jumps).
  • Check character set/collation compatibility.
  • Estimate data size, restore time, and required downtime.
  • Validate network throughput for the transfer.
  • Freeze schema changes for the window.
  • Write the rollback plan and confirm the owner.

Replication-based migration is usually safer than a stop-the-world dump: replicate to the target, let it catch up, then cut over during a short window.

Validation:

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM important_table;
SHOW REPLICA STATUS\G   -- MySQL 8.0.22+
SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G     -- older versions

Confirm Replica_IO_Running / Replica_SQL_Running are Yes, Seconds_Behind_Source is 0, and Last_Error is empty before cutover. Use checksums (e.g. pt-table-checksum) or application-level validation for critical tables.

docs.title: Break-Glass: Root Password Reset
docs.text:
Root password reset (--skip-grant-tables or equivalent) is a break-glass procedure, not routine maintenance. It removes authentication from the running server, so require:

  • Approval and a maintenance window.
  • Console or host-level access (never over an exposed network port).
  • An audit record of who did it and why.
  • Immediate credential rotation and a return to normal authentication afterward.

Do not publish environment-specific reset passwords or internal access paths.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Service is up via systemd; error log shows no crash-recovery loops.
  • Replication (if used) is running with zero lag and no errors.
  • The most recent restore test passed within its scheduled interval.
  • Slow query log is being written and reviewed.
  • Application connects with the least-privilege account, not an admin account.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Backup completes but won't restore Restore fails or data is incomplete Never restore-tested; missing routines/events Restore-test on a schedule; back up with --routines --triggers --events. Replication stops silently Lag grows; Last_Error set Schema drift, conflicting writes, binlog gap Read SHOW REPLICA STATUS; fix root cause; reseed if needed. Cutover data loss Rows missing after migration Cut over before replica caught up Require Seconds_Behind_Source = 0 and checksums before cutover. Connection storms Too many connections max_connections too low or leaked connections Fix pooling; size limits to host capacity. Character set corruption Mojibake after migration Source/target charset mismatch Verify utf8mb4 end to end before migrating.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep the source instance and the pre-change backup until post-migration validation passes.
  2. To roll back a cutover, point applications back to the source and confirm row counts and replication.
  3. For a failed upgrade, restore from the pre-upgrade backup onto the prior version; do not attempt to downgrade data files in place.
  4. Rotate any credentials exposed during the change.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Bind to private interfaces; never expose MySQL to the public internet.
  • Encrypt backups and restrict who can read them — a backup is a full copy of the data.
  • Keep credentials in a secret manager; pass them via protected client config or injection, not on the command line (it leaks into process lists and shell history).
  • Audit administrative access; rotate after any break-glass event.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the database/platform team.
  • Verify a successful restore test at the scheduled interval (the single most important check).
  • Re-confirm the supported upgrade path before each MySQL major upgrade.
  • Re-check account privileges when a new service starts using the database.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Redis Operations Standard
  • MySQL backup and recovery documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run Redis safely in production, and how to change a running Redis deployment without losing data or availability.

The core judgment of this standard:

Redis behaves very differently as a disposable cache than as a system of record. Decide which one each instance is before you choose persistence, eviction, and failover — most Redis incidents come from treating one as the other.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run self-managed Redis for caching, session storage, rate limiting, queues, or locks.
  • Need to choose between RDB, AOF, or both for a given workload.
  • Plan a Redis version upgrade, host move, or topology change.
  • Need a memory-pressure and failover posture you can defend in an incident review.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Application data-model design (key naming, TTL strategy, hot-key avoidance).
  • Managed Redis services where the provider owns persistence and failover. Use the provider's durability and failover contract instead.
  • Redis as a primary database for data that cannot be reconstructed. That is a deliberate architecture decision with its own review.
  • Module-specific operations (search, time series, JSON) beyond core server behavior.

docs.title: Decide the Role First
docs.text:
Role Loss tolerance Persistence Eviction Failover Pure cache Data is reconstructable RDB or none allkeys-lru / allkeys-lfu Optional; clients tolerate cold cache State store (sessions, queues, locks) Loss causes user-visible failure AOF (often AOF + RDB) noeviction Required, with replica + automated failover

Write the role down per instance. Every later setting follows from it. A cache that quietly switched to noeviction will start returning write errors under memory pressure; a state store left on allkeys-lru will silently evict the data you needed.

docs.title: Host tuning
docs.text:
Redis is sensitive to a few host settings. Set them deliberately and record them as part of the host baseline.

# Transparent Huge Pages cause latency spikes during fork/save.
echo never | sudo tee /sys/kernel/mm/transparent_hugepage/enabled

# Allow background-save fork to succeed under memory pressure.
echo 'vm.overcommit_memory = 1' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-redis.conf
sudo sysctl --system

Raise file-descriptor limits to cover maxclients plus headroom:

redis soft nofile 65535
redis hard nofile 65535

docs.title: Instance baseline
docs.text:
Start from a minimal, explicit configuration. Bind to private interfaces only, keep protected mode on, and set a memory ceiling and eviction policy that match the role.

bind 127.0.0.1 <private-interface>
protected-mode yes
port 6379
dir /var/lib/redis

# Role: state store example
appendonly yes
appendfsync everysec
maxmemory 4gb
maxmemory-policy noeviction

Supply requirepass and any ACL users through a secret manager. Never commit a real password, and never publish a real bind address or hostname.

docs.title: Choose persistence from the role, not by default
docs.text:
Mode Recovery behavior Cost Use when RDB only Point-in-time snapshot; loses writes since the last save Cheap, fast restart Cache, or state where minute-scale loss is acceptable AOF only Replays the command log; loses at most ~1s with everysec Larger files, periodic rewrite State store needing low loss AOF + RDB RDB for fast restart, AOF for durability Highest disk/IO State store needing both fast restart and low loss

appendfsync always is durable but slow; reserve it for cases that genuinely cannot tolerate the everysec window, and load-test it first.

docs.title: Make failover an explicit design, not an afterthought
docs.text:
A single Redis instance is a single point of failure for whatever depends on it. For a state store:

  • Run at least one replica on a separate host/zone.
  • Use Redis Sentinel or Redis Cluster for automated failover; do not rely on manual promotion during an incident.
  • Confirm the client library refreshes topology on failover. Many outages are client-side: the server failed over correctly but clients kept dialing the old primary.
  • Test failover under load in staging before you depend on it.

docs.title: Migration Procedure
docs.text:
Use this for a host move, version upgrade, or topology change. Treat it as a change with a rollback owner present.

  1. Record the baseline: role, INFO persistence, INFO replication, key count, memory, and the application error rate.
  2. Confirm source and target Redis versions are compatible (AOF/RDB format and replication compatibility).
  3. Stand up the target and replicate from the source (REPLICAOF) so it catches up live, rather than copying a stale dump where possible.
  4. If using a file copy instead, stop writes or enter a maintenance window, generate the AOF/RDB, copy over a secure channel, and verify the checksum:
sha256sum appendonly.aof
  1. For Redis Cluster, migrate one shard/replica group at a time and confirm slot ownership after each step.
  2. Cut clients over to the target.
  3. Validate (see below) before decommissioning the source.
  4. Keep the source intact until post-migration checks pass through an agreed observation window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
redis-cli -h <redis-host> -p 6379 INFO persistence
redis-cli -h <redis-host> -p 6379 INFO replication
redis-cli -h <redis-host> -p 6379 CLUSTER INFO     # cluster only

Confirm:

  • INFO persistence shows the expected aof_enabled, last save/rewrite succeeded, and no rdb_last_bgsave_status:err.
  • INFO replication shows replicas connected and master_link_status:up; replication offset lag is bounded.
  • For cluster, cluster_state:ok and the expected slot count are assigned.
  • Key count and memory match the baseline; the application error rate is unchanged.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Write errors under load OOM command not allowed maxmemory reached with noeviction Add capacity, shard, or change policy only if the role allows eviction. Silent data loss Keys disappear unexpectedly Eviction policy evicting a state store Set noeviction for state stores; size memory for the working set. Latency spikes during save Periodic stalls THP enabled, or fork under memory pressure Disable THP; set overcommit_memory=1; ensure save headroom. Stale reads after failover Clients hit the old primary Client not refreshing topology Use a Sentinel/Cluster-aware client; verify failover behavior. Restart loses recent writes Data older than expected after restart RDB-only on a state store Enable AOF for state stores. AOF won't load Redis refuses to start Corrupt/truncated AOF Repair with redis-check-aof --fix on a copy; restore from backup if needed.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep the source instance and its data until validation passes; do not delete on cutover day.
  2. To roll back a migration, point clients back at the source and confirm INFO replication and key counts.
  3. To roll back a config change, restore the previous config file and restart through the service manager during a window.
  4. Rotate requirepass/ACL credentials after any emergency access.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never expose Redis to the public internet. Restrict by security group, firewall, or network policy to known clients.
  • Require authentication (requirepass or ACL users); store credentials in a secret manager.
  • Use TLS where the workload requires confidentiality in transit.
  • Disable or rename dangerous commands (FLUSHALL, CONFIG, KEYS) for application-facing users via ACLs.
  • Rotate credentials after migration, host moves, or emergency access.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the team that owns the dependent service.
  • Re-confirm the documented role per instance on each major change.
  • Review eviction and maxmemory whenever the working set grows.
  • Re-test failover on each Redis minor upgrade and each client-library upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Redis persistence documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to use Terraform so that infrastructure changes are reproducible, reviewable, and recoverable — and so that the two most dangerous Terraform failure modes (a corrupted/lost state file and an apply that destroys something it should have replaced) are designed out.

The core judgment of this standard:

The Terraform state file is your infrastructure's source of truth, and terraform plan is the only place you find out what apply will actually do. Protect the state, and never apply a plan you have not read.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Provision cloud or on-prem infrastructure with Terraform/OpenTofu.
  • Work in a team where more than one person can run apply.
  • Need an auditable, rollback-aware infrastructure change process.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Module design patterns for a specific provider in depth.
  • Choosing a cloud provider or designing the target architecture (that is an architecture decision).
  • CI system specifics; this defines the Terraform discipline that any pipeline must enforce.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
1. Store state remotely, encrypted, with locking enabled.
  2. Lock state during every apply so two operators cannot race.
  3. Keep secrets out of .tf files and, as far as possible, out of state.
  4. Use modules and pinned versions for repeatable infrastructure.
  5. Read every plan before applying; gate apply behind review for shared environments.

docs.title: Remote, locked, encrypted state
docs.text:
Local state on a laptop is a single point of failure and cannot be shared safely. Use a remote backend with locking and encryption.

terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.6"
  required_providers {
    example = {
      source  = "example/example"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "example-tf-state"     # placeholder; never publish the real name
    key            = "env/prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "example-region"
    dynamodb_table = "example-tf-locks"     # state locking
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
  • Enable versioning on the state store so you can recover a previous state.
  • Use a separate state key per environment; never share one state across prod and non-prod.
  • The lock table/mechanism is not optional in a team — without it, concurrent applies corrupt state.

docs.title: Pin versions
docs.text: Pin both required_version and provider versions. Unpinned providers mean an unrelated change can silently alter a plan. Commit the .terraform.lock.hcl lock file.

docs.title: Plan, review, apply
docs.text:
terraform fmt -check
terraform validate
terraform plan -out=plan.tfout      # save the exact plan
# review plan.tfout output, then apply exactly that plan:
terraform apply plan.tfout

Saving the plan and applying that file guarantees apply does only what you reviewed — not whatever the world looks like a few minutes later. In the plan output, the symbols that matter most:

  • + create, ~ update in place — usually safe.
  • -/+ destroy and recreate — this is where data-bearing resources get lost. Stop and confirm before applying any -/+ on a database, volume, or anything stateful.

docs.title: Keep secrets out of .tf and state
docs.text:
• Pass secrets via environment variables or a secret manager / vault provider, not literals in .tf.
  • Remember that many resources write sensitive values into state even if you injected them safely — so the state file must be encrypted and access-controlled like a secret.
  • Mark sensitive outputs sensitive = true; never commit *.tfstate or *.tfvars containing secrets.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
terraform validate          # config is internally consistent
terraform plan              # no unexpected changes after apply (clean plan = no drift)
terraform state list        # expected resources are tracked

Confirm:

  • A post-apply plan is clean (empty) — meaning real state matches desired state.
  • No -/+ destroy/recreate happened on stateful resources unintentionally.
  • State is stored remotely and the lock was acquired and released.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response State lost or corrupted apply errors; resources "missing" from Terraform Local state, no versioning, or manual edit Restore from state-store versioning; never hand-edit state. Concurrent apply State conflict / overwrite No locking backend Enable a lock table; serialize applies. Unintended destroy A resource is deleted/recreated Forced replacement from a changed immutable attribute Read -/+ in the plan; use create_before_destroy or prevent_destroy. Drift Plan shows changes nobody made Out-of-band console changes Reconcile: import, or revert the manual change; enforce change-through-Terraform. Secret in state/repo Credential exposed Literal secret in .tf or committed state Rotate the secret; remove from history; encrypt state. Provider upgrade surprise Plan changes after no code change Unpinned provider version Pin versions; commit the lock file.

docs.title: Destroy and Rollback
docs.text:
Terraform has no universal "undo" — rollback is forward-applying a previous known-good configuration, plus protecting against accidental destroy.

  1. Guard stateful resources with lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } so a stray plan cannot delete them.
  2. To roll back a change, check out the previous configuration revision and plan/apply it — review the plan, because reversing a change can itself force replacements.
  3. If state was damaged, restore the prior state version from the backend's versioning before applying anything.
  4. Use terraform plan -destroy to preview a teardown; never run terraform destroy against shared environments without explicit approval and a saved plan.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never publish real account IDs, resource IDs, backend bucket names, regions, state files, or provider credentials.
  • Treat the state file as secret-bearing: encrypt at rest, restrict read access, and audit it.
  • Use least-privilege credentials for the Terraform runner; do not run prod applies with broad admin keys casually.
  • Review module sources; pin them to trusted versions/commits, not floating refs.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/infrastructure team.
  • Re-pin and review provider/module versions on a regular cadence.
  • Confirm state-store versioning, encryption, and locking remain in place each quarter.
  • Re-review prevent_destroy coverage on stateful resources when the architecture changes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Terraform documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines a safe, repeatable baseline for preparing Linux servers for production workloads: what every new server gets before it runs anything, hardened in a way you can re-apply and verify.

The core judgment of this standard:

A baseline is only useful if it is repeatable and auditable. A server configured by hand, once, drifts and cannot be trusted; a baseline that bakes a real SSH key, password, or internal mirror into a bootstrap script becomes a leaked credential the moment it is shared. Make it idempotent, and keep secrets out of it.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • New servers, rebuilds, and post-provisioning hardening.
  • Defining the baseline a configuration-management tool (Ansible, cloud-init, image build) should enforce.
  • Auditing whether an existing host meets a known-good baseline.

docs.title: Scope and Non-Goals
docs.text: Applies to common distributions (RHEL/Rocky-compatible, Ubuntu/Debian). Provider-specific build steps belong in cloud image templates, not here. This standard does not cover application deployment, container-host specifics (see the Docker host standard), or full CIS-benchmark compliance — it is the practical baseline beneath those.

docs.title: Baseline Principles
docs.text:
1. Make every step idempotent so the baseline can be re-applied safely.
  2. Keep access control separate from application deployment.
  3. Prefer SSH key authentication; disable password and root login.
  4. Record every non-default setting; an unrecorded tweak is future drift.
  5. Never embed real users, public keys, passwords, or internal mirror URLs in public scripts — use placeholders.
  6. Validate after each major block, not just at the end.

docs.title: 1. Identify the system
docs.text:
cat /etc/os-release; uname -a; hostnamectl

Record distribution, kernel, architecture, timezone, hostname, and primary interface.

docs.title: 2. Patch and install base tooling
docs.text:
# RHEL/Rocky:
sudo dnf makecache && sudo dnf update -y
sudo dnf install -y vim curl wget tar unzip lsof sysstat chrony bash-completion
# Debian/Ubuntu:
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade -y
sudo apt-get install -y vim curl wget tar unzip lsof sysstat chrony bash-completion

Patching is part of the baseline, not a later task — see the OS patching standard for the ongoing cadence.

docs.title: 3. Time synchronization
docs.text:
sudo systemctl enable --now chronyd 2>/dev/null || sudo systemctl enable --now chrony
timedatectl
chronyc sources -v

Clock skew breaks TLS, tokens, logs, and distributed systems; treat working time sync as mandatory.

docs.title: 4. SSH hardening
docs.text:
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
UseDNS no

Validate before reloading so a bad config never locks you out:

sudo sshd -t
sudo systemctl reload sshd 2>/dev/null || sudo systemctl reload ssh

Keep an active session open while changing SSH, and confirm a new login works before closing it. Use placeholders in any published example (example-user, ssh-ed25519 AAAA... <key-comment>) — never a real key.

docs.title: 5. Kernel parameters and limits
docs.text:
cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-baseline.conf
vm.swappiness = 10
fs.file-max = 1048576
EOF
sudo sysctl --system

cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/security/limits.d/99-baseline.conf
* soft nofile 65535
* hard nofile 65535
* soft nproc 65535
* hard nproc 65535
EOF

Set net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1 only on hosts that actually route (gateways, container hosts) — not as a blanket default.

docs.title: 6. Firewall: default-deny inbound
docs.text:
Open only documented ports. On cloud hosts, align the OS firewall with security groups rather than running two conflicting policies.

sudo systemctl enable --now firewalld
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=ssh --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

docs.title: 7. Data disks
docs.text: Inspect before touching any disk; only format disks explicitly approved and not mounted. See the Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Packages updated; reboot taken if a kernel update requires it.
  • Time sync enabled and healthy (chronyc sources).
  • sshd -t passes; password and root login disabled; key login confirmed.
  • Firewall enabled (or deliberately delegated to cloud security groups).
  • Baseline sysctl and limits applied (sysctl -a | grep swappiness, ulimit -n).
  • Disk layout documented.
  • No plaintext credentials, real keys, or internal URLs in any bootstrap script.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Locked out of SSH Cannot log in after change Reloaded a bad sshd config / disabled key login without a working key Always sshd -t first; keep a session open; fix via console. Drift Hosts diverge over time One-off manual changes Drive the baseline from config management; re-apply idempotently. Secret leaked Credential exposed Real key/password baked into a shared script Use placeholders + secret injection; rotate the leaked secret. Clock-skew failures TLS/token/log errors Time sync not running Ensure chrony is enabled and sources are reachable. Conflicting firewalls Intermittent connectivity OS firewall vs cloud SG disagree Pick one authority; align rules.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Each block is idempotent and independently reversible; keep backups of files you modify (sshd_config, sysctl, limits).
  2. To roll back SSH changes, restore the previous sshd_config, sshd -t, reload — using a console session if locked out.
  3. For sysctl/limits, remove the baseline drop-in file and re-apply (sysctl --system).
  4. Re-run the validation checklist after any rollback.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• SSH keys only; no password or root login. Restrict who has keys.
  • Default-deny inbound; document every open port and its owner.
  • Do not store credentials, real keys, internal mirror URLs, or hostnames in public/bootstrap scripts.
  • Use centralized audit logging for regulated systems; shell history is not an audit log.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/infrastructure team.
  • Re-baseline images on a regular cadence as distributions and recommendations change.
  • Re-audit hosts against the baseline after incidents or major changes.
  • Review SSH and firewall policy when access requirements change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook
  • Docker Host Operations Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines when Supervisor is acceptable for managing Linux processes and how to operate it safely.

The core judgment:

Supervisor is a process guardian, not a platform.

It can keep simple processes running on a host, but it does not replace systemd, containers, orchestration, service discovery, secret management, or high availability.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use Supervisor for:

  • Legacy applications that are not packaged as systemd services.
  • Small background workers on a single host.
  • Transitional processes before containerization.
  • Development or internal tooling with explicit host ownership.

Prefer systemd for host-native services and containers or Kubernetes for portable services that need scheduling, scaling, or standard deployment.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not approve running critical distributed systems as ad hoc Supervisor programs. It also does not cover application-level lifecycle design.

docs.title: Define process ownership
docs.text:
Each program needs:

  • Owner.
  • Host.
  • Runtime user.
  • Working directory.
  • Command.
  • Environment source.
  • Logs.
  • Restart policy.
  • Health check.
  • Stop and rollback procedure.

docs.title: Run as a dedicated user
docs.text: Do not run application processes as root unless there is a documented low-level system reason. Use a dedicated service user with minimum filesystem permissions.

docs.title: Keep configuration explicit
docs.text:
Example:

[program:example-worker]
command=/opt/example/bin/worker --config /etc/example/config.yml
directory=/opt/example
user=example
autostart=true
autorestart=unexpected
startsecs=10
stopsignal=TERM
stopwaitsecs=30
stdout_logfile=/var/log/example/worker.log
stderr_logfile=/var/log/example/worker.err.log
environment=APP_ENV="production"

Avoid embedding secrets in Supervisor configuration. Load them from the approved secret source or protected environment file.

docs.title: Bound restarts
docs.text:
Automatic restart is useful only when the process can recover safely. If a process crashes repeatedly, Supervisor may hide an incident while consuming resources.

Alert on:

  • Restart count.
  • Fatal exit.
  • Backoff or flapping.
  • Log error rate.
  • Host disk usage from logs.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
supervisorctl status
supervisorctl tail example-worker
supervisorctl restart example-worker

Pass conditions:

  • Program runs as intended user.
  • Logs are written to known paths.
  • Restart works.
  • Stop signal terminates cleanly.
  • Health check or external monitoring confirms service behavior.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Process runs as root Host compromise blast radius increases. Use dedicated user. Secrets in config Credentials leak through config backups. Use protected secret source. Infinite restart loop Incident is hidden and host load grows. Alert on restarts and fatal exits. No log rotation Disk fills. Configure log rotation or external logging. No health check Process exists but service is broken. Monitor behavior, not only PID. Manual binary replacement Unknown version runs. Record release version and rollback artifact.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback requires a known previous binary or release directory.

Recommended pattern:

  • Deploy versioned release directory.
  • Update a current symlink.
  • Restart via Supervisor.
  • Keep previous release through validation window.

If configuration changed, preserve the previous config before reload.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Restrict write access to Supervisor config.
  • Restrict supervisorctl to operators.
  • Avoid world-readable environment files.
  • Keep logs from exposing credentials.
  • Review processes that bind privileged ports or access host devices.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review when onboarding a new process, after repeated restarts, before host migration, and before deciding whether the workload should move to systemd or containers.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Application Health Check Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to patch Linux operating systems without turning routine security work into unplanned outages.

The core judgment:

OS patching is a reliability change with security motivation.

Patching must be scheduled, observable, reversible where possible, and tied to ownership. "Run package upgrade everywhere" is not a patching strategy.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • Monthly or emergency Linux patching.
  • Kernel and libc updates.
  • Container host patching.
  • Bastion, CI runner, monitoring, and platform host maintenance.
  • Fleet patch compliance review.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define application dependency upgrades, container image rebuild policy, or managed OS patching service internals.

docs.title: Maintain patch inventory
docs.text:
Each host or node group needs:

  • Owner.
  • Environment.
  • Criticality.
  • OS version.
  • Kernel version.
  • Workload role.
  • Reboot tolerance.
  • Backup or rebuild path.
  • Maintenance window.

Unknown owner hosts should not be silently patched; they should be escalated as governance risk.

docs.title: Classify patch urgency
docs.text:
Class Use When Timeline Routine Normal security and stability updates. Scheduled window. Expedited Vulnerability has exploitability or high exposure. Within approved accelerated window. Emergency Active exploitation or critical exposure. Incident-managed change.

Emergency patching still needs communication, rollback thinking, and post-change validation.

docs.title: Patch by failure domain
docs.text:
Patch in waves:

  1. Non-production or canary host.
  2. One host in each role.
  3. One availability zone or node pool.
  4. Remaining fleet.

Do not patch every host in the same service role simultaneously unless the workload is known to tolerate it.

docs.title: 1. Prepare
docs.text:
uname -a
cat /etc/os-release
df -h
systemctl --failed

Confirm:

  • Backups or rebuild path.
  • Monitoring is healthy.
  • Service owner is aware.
  • Capacity can absorb one host unavailable.
  • Reboot requirement is understood.

docs.title: 2. Apply patches
docs.text:
Use the distribution package manager and approved repository sources. Avoid ad hoc package downloads during production patching.

For Debian or Ubuntu style systems:

sudo apt update
sudo apt list --upgradable
sudo apt upgrade

For RHEL style systems:

sudo dnf check-update
sudo dnf upgrade

docs.title: 3. Reboot when required
docs.text:
Kernel, libc, systemd, and driver changes often require reboot to become effective.

Before reboot:

  • Drain workload if applicable.
  • Stop or fail over stateful services according to their runbook.
  • Confirm alert silence or maintenance window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After patching:

uname -a
systemctl --failed
uptime
journalctl -p err -b --no-pager | tail -50

Pass conditions:

  • Host is reachable.
  • Expected kernel or package versions are active.
  • Critical services are running.
  • Monitoring reports healthy.
  • No unexpected boot or service errors.
  • Application smoke test passes.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Patch all nodes at once Service outage. Patch by failure domain. Skip reboot Vulnerable kernel remains active. Track reboot-required state. Patch without owner Unknown service impact. Maintain inventory and escalation. Repository drift Different hosts receive different packages. Use approved mirrors and version records. No disk check Package install fails mid-change. Check free disk before patching. No rollback path Failed patch becomes extended outage. Backup, snapshot, or rebuild plan.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback depends on change type:

  • Package downgrade may be possible for userland packages.
  • Kernel rollback may use previous boot entry.
  • Host rebuild may be safer than manual repair.
  • Data services require service-specific recovery.

If a patch breaks boot, use console access, previous kernel, snapshot restore, or rebuild from image depending on host class.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Patch sources must be trusted repositories.
  • Do not disable security controls to force a patch.
  • Record exceptions with owner, reason, expiry, and compensating control.
  • Keep emergency patch credentials time-bounded.
  • Verify patch compliance after maintenance.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard after emergency patching, patch-related incidents, new host classes, or changes to OS distribution support windows.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • Shell Automation Standard
  • Application Health Check Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard summarizes safe operating practices for Kubernetes API gateways such as APISIX, Kong, or Nginx-based ingress gateways.

docs.title: Gateway Responsibilities
docs.text:
An API gateway commonly handles:

  • Routing.
  • TLS termination.
  • Authentication and authorization integration.
  • Rate limiting.
  • Request and response transformation.
  • Observability.
  • Canary or weighted routing.

docs.title: Deployment Checklist
docs.text:
Before production use:

  • Gateway controller is highly available.
  • Data plane pods have readiness and liveness probes.
  • Admin API is not publicly exposed.
  • Dashboard access is protected.
  • TLS certificate lifecycle is documented.
  • Metrics are scraped by Prometheus.
  • Access logs are routed to the logging platform.
  • Rate limiting and timeout defaults are defined.

docs.title: Route Template
docs.text:
Use placeholders in public examples:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-gateway-route
  namespace: example-namespace
spec:
  ingressClassName: example-gateway
  rules:
    - host: api.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: example-api
                port:
                  number: 80

docs.title: Security Controls
docs.text:
• Disable anonymous admin access.
  • Restrict admin endpoints by network policy.
  • Store admin keys in a secret manager.
  • Use separate routes for internal and external APIs.
  • Enforce request size and timeout limits.
  • Enable audit logging for route changes.

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
Minimum metrics:

  • Request rate.
  • Error rate.
  • Latency percentiles.
  • Upstream health.
  • Gateway pod CPU and memory.
  • Route-level 4xx and 5xx counts.

docs.title: Kubernetes Deployment Profile
docs.text:
Treat a Kubernetes API gateway (such as APISIX) as a platform component, not a single application's dependency. Its failure or misconfiguration affects every route behind it.

Decide these points before production traffic:

  • Exposure model: NodePort, LoadBalancer, or ingress-managed.
  • Control-plane datastore topology and persistence (for example etcd members and their storage).
  • StorageClass and reclaim policy for any persistent state.
  • Admin API access boundary.
  • Dashboard authentication and network restriction.
  • Prometheus metric exposure.
  • Resource limits for the gateway and its datastore.

Production judgments:

  • Keep admin APIs internal and network-restricted; never expose them publicly.
  • Avoid wildcard admin allowlists.
  • Do not commit admin tokens or keys into values files; use a secret manager.
  • Pin the chart and gateway versions.
  • Validate datastore (for example etcd) health before routing production traffic.
  • Treat plugin and route configuration as change-managed platform config.

docs.title: Deployment validation
docs.text:
• Datastore members (for example etcd) are healthy.
  • Gateway pods are Ready and the gateway service receives traffic.
  • The admin API is reachable only from allowed network paths.
  • The Prometheus endpoint exposes gateway metrics.
  • A test route can be created, queried, and removed cleanly.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
1. Review route manifest.
  2. Validate service and port.
  3. Apply to staging.
  4. Run smoke test.
  5. Apply to production.
  6. Confirm metrics and logs.
  7. Record rollback command.

Rollback:

kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-api
kubectl -n example-namespace delete ingress example-gateway-route

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate DolphinScheduler or a similar distributed workflow scheduler.

The core judgment:

A scheduler is a production dependency when business processes wait for it.

Workflow platforms need metadata protection, worker capacity control, dependency isolation, retry discipline, alerting, and recovery procedures.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Batch jobs or data workflows run through DolphinScheduler.
  • Failed schedules can delay business processes.
  • Multiple teams share workers or projects.
  • Workflows call databases, object storage, APIs, or shell tasks.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not teach workflow modeling, data pipeline design, or DolphinScheduler installation. It covers production operation boundaries for an already selected scheduler.

docs.title: Protect metadata first
docs.text: The metadata database contains project definitions, schedules, task state, users, and execution history. Back it up and test restore before relying on the scheduler for critical jobs.

docs.title: Isolate worker capacity
docs.text:
Separate workers by workload class when possible:

  • Lightweight control tasks.
  • Heavy compute tasks.
  • Data movement tasks.
  • Risky shell tasks.

Avoid letting one noisy workflow consume all worker capacity.

docs.title: Control retries and dependencies
docs.text:
Retries should match the failure type. Retrying a destructive or non-idempotent task can make failure worse.

Document:

  • Retry count.
  • Retry interval.
  • Timeout.
  • Idempotency expectation.
  • Upstream and downstream dependency.
  • Owner.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Master, worker, alert, and API services are healthy.
  • Metadata database is reachable and backed up.
  • Test workflow runs successfully.
  • Failed workflow sends alert.
  • Worker capacity and queue backlog are visible.
  • Permissions prevent cross-team modification where required.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Metadata database lost Workflow definitions and state disappear. Back up and test restore. Worker saturation Schedules miss windows. Monitor worker slots and backlog. Unsafe retry Duplicate writes or side effects. Mark idempotency and retry rules. Shared shell permissions One workflow can affect host or other teams. Isolate workers and users. No alerting Failed workflows go unnoticed. Configure alert channels and owners.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery may include:

  • Restart scheduler components.
  • Disable a bad workflow.
  • Restore metadata database.
  • Re-run idempotent tasks.
  • Manually reconcile non-idempotent task output.

Do not blindly re-run workflows that write external state until idempotency is known.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Restrict project and workflow edit permissions.
  • Protect data source credentials.
  • Avoid broad shell execution rights.
  • Audit workflow changes for critical projects.
  • Do not publish real workflow names, database names, or credentials.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review after missed schedules, metadata incidents, worker saturation, major version upgrades, or onboarding new teams.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kafka and ZooKeeper Operations Standard
  • Nacos Operations Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run Kafka safely in production — including the ZooKeeper-backed clusters still common in the field — and how to migrate or upgrade one without losing messages or stalling consumers.

The core judgment of this standard:

Kafka's durability is not "replication factor 3" — it is the combination of replication factor, min.insync.replicas, and producer acks. Get those three wrong together and an acknowledged message can still be lost on a single broker failure, no matter how many replicas you configured.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Operate a self-managed Kafka cluster (ZooKeeper-backed or KRaft).
  • Need durability, monitoring, and migration practices you can defend in an incident review.
  • Plan a broker addition, version upgrade, or cluster migration.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Stream-processing application design (Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, connectors).
  • Managed streaming services internals — use the provider's durability/HA contract; the producer/consumer and durability judgment still applies.
  • The full ZooKeeper-to-KRaft migration procedure beyond noting it as a planned, version-gated change.

docs.title: Planning Checklist
docs.text: Area Questions Brokers How many brokers, on what storage class, across how many failure domains? Metadata ZooKeeper or KRaft? Does the Kafka version still require ZooKeeper? Topics Partition count, replication factor, retention, and cleanup policy per topic? Durability acks, min.insync.replicas, and replication factor — do they agree? Clients Can producers/consumers tolerate rolling restarts and rebalances? Security Authentication (SASL), authorization (ACLs), and TLS required? Observability Are under-replicated partitions, offline partitions, consumer lag, and JVM/GC collected?

docs.title: Durability is three settings, not one
docs.text:
For a topic that must not lose acknowledged writes:

  • Replication factor ≥ 3 — survive a broker loss.
  • min.insync.replicas = 2 — a write is only acknowledged when at least 2 replicas have it.
  • Producer acks=all — wait for the in-sync replicas before acknowledging.

Replication factor 3 with acks=1 still loses data if the leader dies before replicas catch up. The three settings must be chosen together. The cost is latency and that a topic with RF=3/min.insync=2 goes read-only if it drops below 2 in-sync replicas — a deliberate availability-vs-durability trade.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
1. Keep broker IDs stable; brokers are identity-bearing.
  2. Store log segments on dedicated persistent volumes sized for retention × throughput.
  3. Spread brokers (and ZooKeeper/KRaft quorum members) across failure domains; use an odd quorum count.
  4. Set retention deliberately per topic (size and/or time); unbounded retention fills disks and takes the cluster down.

docs.title: Monitoring that matters
docs.text:
• Under-replicated partitions > 0 and offline partitions > 0 are leading indicators of data-loss risk — alert on them.
  • Consumer lag per group — the signal that consumers are falling behind.
  • Broker disk usage vs. retention; JVM heap and GC.

docs.title: Migration / Upgrade
docs.text:
Treat as a controlled change with a defined cutoff and rollback owner.

  1. Freeze topic-topology changes for the window.
  2. Capture the topic list, partition counts, replication factors, configs, and consumer groups.
  3. Stand up / upgrade the target; for upgrades, do a rolling broker restart (one broker at a time, waiting for under-replicated partitions to return to 0 before the next).
  4. For a cluster migration, mirror data with an approved tool (e.g. MirrorMaker 2) and configure secure connectivity.
  5. Validate topic offsets and consumer lag on the target.
  6. Cut over producers first, then consumers.
  7. Keep the source cluster read-only until the rollback window closes.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server <broker>:9092 --describe --under-replicated-partitions
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server <broker>:9092 --describe --topic example-topic
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server <broker>:9092 --all-groups --describe

Confirm:

  • Zero under-replicated and zero offline partitions.
  • Each critical topic has the intended replication factor and min.insync.replicas.
  • Consumer lag is bounded and draining, not growing.
  • After a rolling restart/upgrade, every broker rejoined the ISR.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Acknowledged message lost Data gone after broker failure acks=1 or min.insync.replicas=1 Use acks=all + min.insync.replicas=2 + RF≥3. Topic goes read-only Producers blocked In-sync replicas dropped below min.insync.replicas Restore a broker; this is the durability trade-off working as designed. Consumers fall behind Growing lag Slow consumers, repartition, or broker pressure Scale consumers/partitions; investigate broker load. Disk fills Broker down / cluster unstable Unbounded retention Set retention; alert on disk; expand or trim. Rolling restart causes loss Under-replicated during restart Restarted next broker too soon Wait for URP=0 between brokers. ZooKeeper quorum loss Cluster metadata unavailable Even count / members down Odd ZK/KRaft quorum across failure domains.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Define the cutover cutoff before migrating — once producers write to the target, rollback gets expensive.
  2. If producers have not yet cut over, roll back by leaving them on the source and tearing down the target.
  3. If they have cut over and bidirectional sync is not in place, rollback means stopping target writes and routing producers back to the source, accepting the gap — plan this explicitly.
  4. For a failed broker upgrade, reinstall the prior version on that broker and let it rejoin the ISR before touching the next.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enable SASL authentication and ACL authorization; do not run an open broker.
  • Use TLS for client and inter-broker traffic where confidentiality is required.
  • Never publish real broker addresses, topic names with business meaning, or credentials.
  • Restrict who can create/alter topics and ACLs.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/messaging team.
  • Re-confirm durability settings (acks/min.insync/RF) when a topic's loss tolerance changes.
  • Re-test rolling-restart behavior on each Kafka upgrade.
  • Plan the ZooKeeper-to-KRaft transition as a version-gated change before ZooKeeper support is dropped.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Kafka documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Nacos as a service discovery and configuration platform.

The core judgment:

Nacos is a runtime control plane. Treating it as a simple key-value UI creates production risk.

Service registration, discovery, and configuration changes can alter live traffic. Nacos requires ownership, namespace boundaries, change review, backup, monitoring, and rollback.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Applications depend on Nacos for service discovery.
  • Configuration is changed at runtime through Nacos.
  • Multiple teams share one Nacos cluster.
  • Nacos sits on the critical path for deployment or failover.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define application framework usage or every Nacos cluster topology. It defines operational controls.

docs.title: Define namespace and ownership boundaries
docs.text:
Each namespace or group must map to:

  • Environment.
  • Application owner.
  • Change approver.
  • Access role.
  • Backup and restore expectation.
  • Naming rule.

Do not mix production and non-production configuration in the same namespace unless access and naming rules prevent accidents.

docs.title: Control configuration changes
docs.text:
Runtime config changes need the same discipline as deployments.

Required fields:

  • Config key.
  • Application owner.
  • Reason.
  • Expected effect.
  • Validation method.
  • Rollback value.
  • Change window for high-risk services.

Avoid editing production config directly in the UI without a recorded change path.

docs.title: Protect discovery from false health
docs.text:
Service discovery is only useful when instance health reflects reality. Applications should register only when ready, deregister on shutdown, and expose meaningful health checks.

Watch for:

  • Stale instances.
  • Incorrect weight.
  • Wrong cluster or metadata.
  • Large registration churn.
  • Discovery latency.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Operational checks:

curl -fsS http://<nacos-endpoint>/nacos/v1/ns/operator/metrics
curl -fsS http://<nacos-endpoint>/nacos/v1/ns/instance/list?serviceName=<service-name>

Pass conditions:

  • Cluster nodes are healthy.
  • Applications can read expected config.
  • Config change event reaches clients.
  • Service instances match expected count.
  • Unhealthy instances are not served.
  • Backup exists for configuration and metadata.

docs.title: Backup and Restore
docs.text:
Backup:

  • Nacos database if externalized.
  • Configuration exports.
  • Namespace and group mapping.
  • Access policy.
  • Cluster configuration.

Restore must be tested in a non-production environment. A backup file that has never been restored is only a hope.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Unreviewed config edit Live service behavior changes unexpectedly. Require change record and rollback value. Shared namespace misuse One team breaks another service. Separate namespaces and roles. Stale service instance Traffic goes to dead target. Readiness-aware registration and health checks. No config backup Cannot recover after mistaken delete. Export and database backup. Admin account shared No auditability. Individual or role-based access. Client cannot reach Nacos Service startup or discovery fails. Monitor client errors and cluster endpoints.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback may mean:

  • Restore previous config value.
  • Revert namespace or group mapping.
  • Remove bad service instance metadata.
  • Restore database from backup for destructive changes.

For high-risk config, capture previous value before change and validate rollback in the same client path used for release validation.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enable authentication and avoid shared admin credentials.
  • Restrict namespace access by team and environment.
  • Do not store raw application secrets in plain config.
  • Audit production config changes.
  • Protect Nacos endpoints from public exposure.
  • Encrypt database and backup access where required.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review after config incidents, namespace growth, access changes, version upgrades, or when Nacos becomes a dependency for additional teams.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Application Health Check Standard
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines safe patterns for using HAProxy to distribute TCP/HTTP traffic across backends in production.

The core judgment of this standard:

A load balancer is only as good as its health check. A check that passes when the app is not actually ready will route users straight into errors; a check that is too aggressive will flap healthy backends out of rotation. The health check is the design, not a detail.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Balance traffic across multiple backends (HTTP or raw TCP).
  • Need a single validated reload procedure and a draining model for safe backend changes.
  • Are deciding between HAProxy (connection/L4-L7 balancing) and an application proxy.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • TLS termination patterns and HTTP header handling in depth — see the Nginx reverse proxy + TLS standard; the validated-reload and certificate-lifecycle principles there apply.
  • Kubernetes Ingress controllers.
  • WAF or DDoS mitigation.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Match the health check to real application readiness, not just "port is open".
  2. Validate config (haproxy -c) before every reload; reload (not restart) to avoid dropping connections.
  3. Choose L4 (mode tcp) vs L7 (mode http) deliberately — you only get HTTP-aware features in L7.
  4. Drain backends before maintenance instead of yanking them.
  5. Restrict the stats/admin socket; never publish real backend addresses.

docs.title: Frontend, backend, and a readiness-matching check
docs.text:
frontend example_http
  bind *:80
  mode http
  default_backend example_backend

backend example_backend
  mode http
  balance roundrobin
  option httpchk GET /health           # check a real readiness endpoint
  http-check expect status 200
  default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 # fail after 3 bad, recover after 2 good
  server app-a <backend-a-host>:8080 check
  server app-b <backend-b-host>:8080 check
  • option httpchk GET /health plus http-check expect status 200 means a backend is only "up" when its readiness endpoint actually returns 200 — not merely when the TCP port accepts a connection.
  • fall/rise give hysteresis so a single blip does not eject a healthy backend, and a single good response does not prematurely re-add a flapping one.

docs.title: L4 vs L7
docs.text: Mode Use for Note mode http (L7) HTTP services needing path/host routing, header logic, HTTP health checks Slightly more overhead; HTTP-aware mode tcp (L4) Databases, message brokers, TLS passthrough, non-HTTP protocols No HTTP features; check with option tcp-check

docs.title: Balancing algorithm
docs.text: roundrobin is a fine default. Use leastconn for long-lived connections (databases, websockets) where request duration varies widely. Persistent sessions (cookie/stick-table) only when the app genuinely needs affinity — sticky sessions undermine even balancing and complicate draining.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
1. Edit the config.
  2. Validate:
haproxy -c -f /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg
  1. Reload (graceful — existing connections finish):
systemctl reload haproxy
systemctl status haproxy --no-pager
  1. To take a backend out for maintenance, drain it first (set it to MAINT/drain via the admin socket or config) so in-flight requests complete, then remove it. Do not delete a live backend and reload — that drops its connections.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
haproxy -c -f /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg     # config valid
# via stats socket (restricted): show backend/server state
echo "show stat" | socat stdio /run/haproxy/admin.sock | cut -d, -f1,2,18

Confirm:

  • All intended servers show UP; none stuck DOWN or flapping.
  • The health check targets a real readiness endpoint and reflects app state.
  • After a reload, no connection errors or 5xx spike; traffic is distributed as intended.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Check / response Users hit errors despite "healthy" LB Health check passes before app is ready Point httpchk at a true readiness endpoint; add expect status. Backends flap in/out fall/rise/inter too aggressive, or backend overloaded Add hysteresis; investigate backend latency. Connections dropped on change Backend removed + reload instead of drain Drain to MAINT first, let connections finish, then remove. Uneven load Sticky sessions or roundrobin with long-lived conns Use leastconn; reconsider session affinity. TCP service mis-checked L4 service with HTTP check Use mode tcp + option tcp-check. Restart caused an outage Used restart, not reload Always reload for config changes.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep the previous config under version control or as a timestamped copy.
  2. Restore the prior config, haproxy -c, then systemctl reload haproxy.
  3. If a backend change went wrong, re-enable the drained/removed backend and reload; confirm it returns to UP.
  4. If a reload left HAProxy degraded and config won't validate, restore the last-known-good file before debugging further.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Restrict the stats page and admin socket to trusted operators/networks; they can change routing.
  • Do not publish real backend addresses, hostnames, or the admin socket path.
  • For TLS termination, use a managed certificate lifecycle (see the Nginx + TLS standard).
  • Keep HAProxy patched; it is on the request path and often internet-facing.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/networking team.
  • Re-validate that health checks still reflect readiness when an app's startup behavior changes.
  • Review balancing algorithm and affinity when traffic patterns change.
  • Confirm stats/admin access controls on a regular cadence.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • HAProxy configuration manual

docs.title: Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines safe patterns for deploying Nginx or OpenResty as a reverse proxy that terminates TLS in front of application upstreams.

The core judgment of this standard:

A reverse proxy is a live production dependency on the request path. Every config change must be validated before it is applied, and reversible after, because a bad reload takes down everything behind it — not one service.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Terminate HTTPS for one or more upstream services behind Nginx/OpenResty.
  • Need a consistent, reviewable proxy configuration pattern across services.
  • Manage TLS certificates and their renewal.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Ingress controllers in Kubernetes (use the Kubernetes ingress standard; the TLS and header principles still apply).
  • WAF rule design or DDoS mitigation.
  • L4/TCP load balancing as the primary concern — see the HAProxy standard for connection-level balancing.
  • CDN or edge-cache strategy.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Separate the public listener configuration from upstream/service configuration so each can be reviewed and changed independently.
  2. Source TLS certificates from a controlled lifecycle (automated issuance/renewal where possible).
  3. Never commit real certificates, private keys, domains, or upstream addresses.
  4. Always nginx -t before reload; never apply an unvalidated config to a live proxy.
  5. Keep access logs useful while avoiding sensitive request data (no auth headers, no full query strings with secrets).

docs.title: Reverse proxy with explicit upstream
docs.text:
Define upstreams separately, with health-related limits, so a single bad backend does not blackhole traffic.

upstream example_upstream {
    server <backend-a-host>:8080 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    server <backend-b-host>:8080 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name service.example.com;

    # Redirect all plaintext to HTTPS; do not serve the app over :80.
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

docs.title: TLS listener
docs.text:
server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    http2 on;
    server_name service.example.com;

    ssl_certificate     /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.crt;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.key;
    ssl_protocols       TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
    ssl_ciphers         HIGH:!aNULL:!MD5;
    ssl_prefer_server_ciphers on;
    ssl_session_cache   shared:SSL:10m;

    client_max_body_size 16m;   # size to the largest legitimate upload

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://example_upstream;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Host              $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP         $remote_addr;
