        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For   $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_connect_timeout 5s;
        proxy_send_timeout    60s;
        proxy_read_timeout    60s;
    }
}

Certificate and key paths are illustrative. The private key file must have restrictive permissions and must never be committed.

docs.title: Forwarded headers must be consistent
docs.text: X-Forwarded-Proto is the most common source of redirect loops: if the app builds HTTPS redirects from a header the proxy sets wrong, the browser bounces forever. Set it to $scheme on the listener that actually terminated TLS, and make sure the app trusts the proxy.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
Treat every change as a controlled change on the request path.

  1. Edit the relevant config fragment (listener or upstream), not a monolith.
  2. Validate syntax and referenced files:
sudo nginx -t
  1. Reload (graceful; in-flight requests finish on old workers):
sudo systemctl reload nginx
sudo systemctl status nginx --no-pager
  1. Validate behavior (see below) before considering the change done.
  2. If validation fails, roll back immediately — do not iterate live.

docs.title: Certificate Lifecycle
docs.text:
Automate issuance and renewal where possible; manual renewal is a recurring outage waiting for the day someone forgets.

Check expiry of a live endpoint:

echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -dates

Check a local certificate file:

openssl x509 -in /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.crt -noout -dates
  • Test renewal in staging before production.
  • Alert on certificate expiry well ahead of the deadline (see the Prometheus alerting / blackbox TLS-expiry check).
  • Reload Nginx after renewal so it picks up the new certificate.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
nginx -t                                  # config valid
curl -I https://service.example.com       # status line + headers
curl -sS https://service.example.com/health

Confirm:

  • TLS handshake succeeds with the expected protocol and a non-expired certificate.
  • The expected status code returns and X-Forwarded-Proto reaches the app correctly (no redirect loop).
  • Upstream is reachable from the proxy host; both backends serve traffic.
  • Error and access logs show no spike of 5xx after reload.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Check / response 502 Bad Gateway Upstream down or wrong port curl the upstream from the proxy host; fix proxy_pass/upstream. 504 Gateway Timeout Upstream slow; proxy timeout too low Raise proxy_read_timeout only if the upstream is legitimately slow; otherwise fix the upstream. TLS warning / handshake failure Expired or wrong certificate, missing chain openssl x509 -noout -dates; ensure full chain is served. Infinite redirect loop App builds HTTPS redirects from a wrong X-Forwarded-Proto Set X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme; ensure app trusts the proxy. Large upload rejected (413) client_max_body_size too small Raise to the largest legitimate upload. Reload "succeeded" but old config served Reload skipped or failed silently nginx -t then systemctl reload; check systemctl status.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep the previous config (version control or a timestamped copy) so you can restore it in one step.
  2. Restore the prior config fragment, run nginx -t, then systemctl reload nginx.
  3. If a reload left Nginx serving errors and the config won't validate, restore the last-known-good file and reload; do not leave a broken proxy while debugging.
  4. For a certificate rollback, restore the previous valid certificate/key pair and reload.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Store private keys with restrictive permissions (owner-only); never commit them.
  • Do not log Authorization headers, cookies, or full query strings that may carry tokens.
  • Do not expose admin or internal routes through a public listener unless explicitly required and access-controlled.
  • Disable old TLS versions (keep TLS 1.2+); review cipher suites on a schedule.
  • Keep the proxy and OpenResty/Lua modules patched; the proxy is internet-facing.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/networking team.
  • Review cipher and protocol settings on a regular cadence as recommendations change.
  • Confirm certificate renewal automation and expiry alerting still work each quarter.
  • Re-validate forwarded-header handling whenever an upstream app changes its redirect logic.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Mozilla SSL configuration generator

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate OpenVPN as a controlled remote access mechanism.

The core judgment:

VPN access is privileged network reachability, not a convenience tunnel.

OpenVPN can be appropriate for small or transitional environments, but it must have identity ownership, network scope, logging, certificate lifecycle, revocation, and periodic access review.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Engineers need remote access to private systems.
  • A small environment does not yet have a stronger zero-trust access layer.
  • Vendor or emergency access needs a bounded temporary path.
  • Legacy systems require network-level reachability.

Prefer identity-aware access, bastion workflows, or workload-specific gateways when they provide stronger audit and least privilege.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not cover consumer VPN usage, anonymous browsing, or bypassing corporate access controls.

docs.title: Define access scope
docs.text:
Document:

  • Who can connect.
  • Which networks are reachable.
  • Which ports or services are allowed.
  • Which environments are excluded.
  • Who approves temporary access.
  • How access is revoked.

Avoid broad routes that make every private subnet reachable to every VPN user.

docs.title: Manage certificates and users
docs.text:
Each user or automation identity needs its own certificate or credential. Shared VPN profiles are not acceptable for production access.

Track:

  • User identity.
  • Issue date.
  • Expiry date.
  • Approval.
  • Revocation status.
  • Device or use case.

docs.title: Log and review access
docs.text: Logs should show connection time, user identity, assigned address, disconnect time, and source context where available. Access should be reviewed on a schedule and after personnel or vendor changes.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before granting access:

openvpn --version

From a test client:

curl -fsS https://<internal-service>/healthz
nc -vz <internal-host> <port>

Pass conditions:

  • User can reach only approved services.
  • Denied networks are unreachable.
  • DNS behavior is understood.
  • Logs identify the user.
  • Revocation test removes access.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Shared client profile No user accountability. Issue per-user credentials. Overbroad route push VPN users reach unrelated systems. Restrict routes and firewall rules. No revocation test Former users retain access. Test CRL or revocation path. Split DNS confusion Users hit wrong service endpoint. Document DNS behavior. No log retention Incident review lacks evidence. Retain access logs. Long-lived vendor access Temporary path becomes permanent. Expiry and review.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback access by:

  • Revoking user certificate.
  • Removing user profile.
  • Removing route or firewall rule.
  • Rotating shared secrets if a shared profile existed.
  • Disabling the VPN service only as an incident response measure.

Verify from the revoked client path that access is no longer possible.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Use MFA or an identity-aware layer where available.
  • Do not share .ovpn profiles.
  • Protect private keys on client devices.
  • Keep VPN endpoints patched.
  • Restrict management interface access.
  • Avoid publishing real routes, hostnames, or client names.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review VPN access monthly or quarterly depending on risk, after staff changes, after vendor work ends, and after any network exposure incident.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• JumpServer and Bastion Access Standard
  • Server Baseline Standard
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to handle OpenWrt-based network devices in lab, edge, or small-site environments.

The core judgment:

An edge router is infrastructure, even when it is small enough to sit on a desk.

OpenWrt devices can affect routing, VPN, DNS, firewall policy, and wireless access. Their configuration must be backed up, reviewed, and recoverable.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • Lab routing.
  • Small-office or edge routing.
  • VPN gateway experiments.
  • Network testing.
  • Custom firmware evaluation.

Do not use unmanaged OpenWrt devices as hidden production network dependencies.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not recommend OpenWrt as the default production router platform, nor does it document ISP-specific settings, Wi-Fi passwords, VPN profiles, or full firewall policies.

docs.title: Separate firmware, config, and secrets
docs.text:
Keep distinct records for:

  • Firmware version.
  • Package list.
  • Network configuration.
  • Firewall policy.
  • VPN configuration.
  • Wireless settings.
  • Secrets and keys.

Secrets must not be committed or published.

docs.title: Back up before change
docs.text:
Before changing firmware or network policy:

uci export network
uci export firewall
uci export dhcp

Store backups in an approved private location.

docs.title: Control exposure
docs.text:
Document:

  • Management interface.
  • Allowed admin networks.
  • WAN exposure.
  • VPN peers.
  • Firewall zones.
  • DNS behavior.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
uci show network
uci show firewall
logread | tail

Pass conditions:

  • Management access works from approved network.
  • Unapproved WAN admin access is blocked.
  • Required routes work.
  • DNS behavior matches plan.
  • Firewall zones match intended trust boundary.
  • Backup is current.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention No config backup Device change is hard to recover. Export config before changes. WAN admin exposed Device can be attacked remotely. Restrict management interfaces. VPN key published Private network exposed. Keep keys out of docs and repos. Firmware drift Behavior differs from expected. Record firmware and packages. Firewall zone mistake Networks over-trust each other. Validate zones and denied paths.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback options:

  • Restore previous UCI configuration.
  • Revert firewall rules.
  • Restore previous firmware if supported.
  • Factory reset and reapply backup.

For remote devices, ensure out-of-band recovery or onsite support before risky network changes.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish Wi-Fi passwords, VPN profiles, keys, ISP accounts, MAC lists, or real routes.
  • Change default passwords.
  • Restrict SSH and LuCI access.
  • Keep firmware updated.
  • Disable services that are not needed.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review after firmware upgrades, VPN changes, firewall changes, remote-access changes, and network incidents.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• OpenVPN Access Standard
  • Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
  • Server Baseline Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Samba file shares for internal SMB/CIFS access.

The core judgment:

A file share is a data boundary, not just a shared folder.

Samba shares need ownership, permission design, backup, audit, capacity monitoring, and recovery rules.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use Samba when:

  • Internal users or systems need SMB/CIFS access.
  • The share has a clear owner and retention policy.
  • Access can be controlled by named users or groups.
  • Data is backed up or explicitly disposable.

Prefer object storage, managed file services, or application-native storage when they provide stronger durability and governance.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not replace enterprise document management, object storage governance, or application data storage design. It covers bounded SMB/CIFS file sharing.

docs.title: Define share ownership
docs.text:
Each share needs:

  • Owner.
  • Purpose.
  • Data class.
  • User or group access.
  • Read/write policy.
  • Retention.
  • Backup.
  • Capacity alert.

Unknown-owner shares should be treated as risk.

docs.title: Avoid guest write access
docs.text: Guest write access makes attribution and deletion safety weak. Use named users and groups.

docs.title: Keep configuration explicit
docs.text:
Example:

[example-share]
  path = /srv/samba/example
  browsable = yes
  read only = no
  valid users = @example-team
  create mask = 0660
  directory mask = 0770

Use placeholders in public documentation.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
testparm
smbclient -L //<file-server> -U <user>

Pass conditions:

  • Configuration validates.
  • Only approved users can access the share.
  • Write behavior matches policy.
  • Backup includes required paths.
  • Disk usage and inode usage are monitored.
  • Logs are available where audit is required.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Guest write enabled No accountability. Use named users and groups. Broad filesystem permissions Users access unrelated data. Restrict path and masks. No backup Accidental deletion becomes data loss. Back up business data. No capacity alert Share fills underlying filesystem. Monitor bytes and inodes. Real paths published Internal structure leaks. Use placeholders in public docs.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery may include:

  • Restore deleted files from backup.
  • Revert Samba configuration.
  • Remove a user or group.
  • Move a share to a larger filesystem.
  • Disable the share during incident response.

Do not delete large directories until owner and data class are known.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real share names, users, hosts, or access paths.
  • Restrict admin access.
  • Use least-privilege groups.
  • Avoid storing secrets in shared folders.
  • Audit access for sensitive data.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review after access changes, data growth, file deletion incidents, backup failures, or share owner changes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • OpenVPN Access Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this template to plan a production migration before any irreversible network, data, platform, or application cutover.

The core judgment:

A migration plan is a decision record for risk, not a task list for copying resources.

It should make the target state, safety checks, rollback boundary, and ownership clear enough that another engineer can review the plan without knowing the original environment.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Fill every section with public-safe placeholders. Do not include real customer names, domains, IP addresses, account IDs, bucket names, credentials, private repository URLs, or raw production evidence.

docs.title: Migration Summary
docs.text: Field Value Migration name <short-name> Owner <team-or-role> Migration family Network / storage / database / application / platform / mixed Source state <generic-source-state> Target state <generic-target-state> Planned window <window> Rollback decision owner <role>

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
Explain why the migration is needed:

  • Current limitation.
  • Target outcome.
  • Business or reliability driver.
  • Constraints that shape the design.

Do not start with commands. Start with the operational problem.

docs.title: In Scope
docs.text:
• Systems or data included.
  • Traffic paths included.
  • Teams affected.
  • Validation responsibilities.

docs.title: Out of Scope
docs.text:
• Systems intentionally excluded.
  • Follow-up work that must not block the migration.
  • Adjacent modernization that is not part of this cutover.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
Describe the current architecture with generic labels:

  • Components.
  • Data flows.
  • Ownership.
  • Known risks.
  • Current monitoring.
  • Current rollback path.

docs.title: Target State
docs.text:
Describe the intended state:

  • Components.
  • Data flows.
  • Ownership changes.
  • Security changes.
  • Monitoring changes.
  • Decommission plan for source resources.

docs.title: Risk Register
docs.text: Risk Probability Impact Detection Mitigation Rollback Trigger Example: DNS clients keep old endpoint Medium High Resolver test and traffic logs Lower TTL before cutover More than expected traffic remains on old path

docs.title: Migration Sequence
docs.text:
1. Prepare target environment.
  2. Validate target access and observability.
  3. Sync data or configuration.
  4. Run pre-cutover smoke tests.
  5. Freeze or drain source writes if required.
  6. Execute cutover.
  7. Run post-cutover validation.
  8. Observe for the agreed window.
  9. Decide rollback, continue, or complete.
  10. Decommission source only after rollback window ends.

docs.title: Validation Plan
docs.text: Layer Check Expected Result Owner Platform Target resources are healthy. No critical platform errors. <role> Network Required paths work and denied paths fail. Route behavior matches plan. <role> Data Counts, size, checksum, or sample reads match. Drift is within accepted threshold. <role> Application Critical user path passes. Smoke test succeeds. <role> Monitoring Alerts and dashboards reflect target. Signals are visible. <role>

docs.title: Rollback Plan
docs.text:
Define:

  • Rollback trigger.
  • Rollback decision owner.
  • Latest safe rollback time.
  • Steps to return traffic or reads to source.
  • How writes are reconciled.
  • Data that must be preserved.
  • Conditions where rollback is unsafe.

If rollback requires restoring data, state the restore source and expected recovery time.

docs.title: Communication Plan
docs.text:
Include:

  • Change announcement.
  • Maintenance window.
  • Status update cadence.
  • Escalation channel.
  • Completion notice.
  • Rollback notice template.

docs.title: Post-Migration Review
docs.text:
After completion, record:

  • What changed.
  • What validation proved.
  • What failed or surprised the team.
  • Follow-up actions.
  • Decommission status.
  • Monitoring or runbook updates.

docs.title: Quality Gate
docs.text:
The migration plan is not ready if:

  • Rollback trigger is vague.
  • Validation checks are only infrastructure-level and ignore user paths.
  • Data ownership is unclear.
  • Source deletion happens before rollback window.
  • The plan contains environment-specific secrets or identifiers.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
  • Object Storage Migration Case
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this template for alerts, incidents, and repeatable operational procedures.

The core judgment:

A runbook is a decision path under pressure, not a command archive.

It should help an operator decide what is happening, what is safe to do, when to stop, and how to prove recovery.

docs.title: Runbook Summary
docs.text: Field Value Runbook name <name> Service or platform area <area> Owner <team-or-role> Severity range <severity> Last reviewed <date> Escalation owner <role>

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
State exactly when to use this runbook:

  • Alert name.
  • User symptom.
  • Maintenance task.
  • Operational request.
  • Manual detection condition.

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
Describe possible impact:

  • User impact.
  • Data impact.
  • Platform impact.
  • Security impact.
  • Blast radius.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
List what the operator needs before acting:

  • Access.
  • Tools.
  • Target namespace, host, cluster, or account.
  • Required approval.
  • Backup or snapshot requirement.
  • Known rollback owner.

docs.title: Initial Triage
docs.text:
Use commands that identify the target and risk before changing state:

example status command
example describe command
example recent-events command

Record current state, current symptom, and whether users are affected.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Symptom Check Likely Cause Next Path Example symptom Example check Example cause Path A

docs.title: Path A: Low-Risk Recovery
docs.text:
Use when impact is limited and the action is reversible.

  1. Step.
  2. Validate.
  3. Continue or escalate.

docs.title: Path B: High-Risk Recovery
docs.text:
Use when data, security, or broad availability is at risk.

  1. Stop and notify owner.
  2. Preserve evidence.
  3. Execute approved mitigation.
  4. Validate.
  5. Decide rollback or escalation.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Define healthy evidence:

example validation command

Pass conditions:

  • Service health recovered.
  • Error or symptom cleared.
  • User path works.
  • No new critical alert.
  • Owner accepts the result.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
State:

  • What can be rolled back.
  • What cannot be rolled back.
  • When to escalate.
  • Who approves risky actions.
  • What evidence to attach.

docs.title: Closure Criteria
docs.text:
Close only when:

  • Triggering alert is resolved or accepted.
  • Validation passes.
  • User impact is over.
  • Follow-up owner and due date exist.
  • Incident or change record is updated.

docs.title: Post-Incident Follow-Up
docs.text:
Record:

  • Root cause or suspected cause.
  • Prevention action.
  • Monitoring gap.
  • Runbook improvement.
  • Owner.
  • Due date.

docs.title: Quality Gate
docs.text:
The runbook is not ready if it:

  • Starts with destructive commands.
  • Has no impact check.
  • Has no rollback or escalation boundary.
  • Does not define healthy state.
  • Assumes one environment-specific target.
  • Contains secrets or real operational identifiers.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Standard Document Template
  • Migration Plan Template
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this template for public DevOps standards and operational references.

The core judgment:

A standard should explain the operating decision, not only document commands.

It should help a reader understand when to use the guidance, why the recommendation exists, how to validate it, and where the risks are.

docs.title: Front Matter
docs.text:
---
tags:
  - Reliability
  - Platform
description: One sentence that names the reader task and operational value.
---

Tags should be stable and useful for retrieval. Description should not be marketing copy.

docs.title: Title
docs.text:
Use a literal, searchable title:

<Technology or Capability> Standard

Avoid vague titles such as "Best Practices" without a domain.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Explain:

  • What problem the standard solves.
  • Which operational decision it helps.
  • The core judgment in one clear sentence.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text: List situations where the standard applies. Include enough detail that a reader can self-select.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: State what the standard does not cover. This prevents one page from becoming a dumping ground.

docs.title: Recommended Standard
docs.text:
Describe the recommended operating model:

  • Ownership.
  • Architecture or process boundary.
  • Required controls.
  • Safe defaults.
  • Exceptions.
  • Trade-offs.

Use tables when they clarify decisions.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
If the standard includes operational steps, present them in a safe order:

  1. Prepare.
  2. Capture baseline.
  3. Execute.
  4. Validate.
  5. Observe.
  6. Roll back or close.

Commands must use placeholders and must not expose real identifiers.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Define how to prove success:

  • Commands.
  • Metrics.
  • Logs.
  • User journeys.
  • Expected healthy state.
  • Observation window.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text:
Failure Mode Result Prevention Example failure Example result Example prevention

Failure modes make the standard useful to experienced readers. Do not skip them.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Explain:

  • What is reversible.
  • What is not safely reversible.
  • Who approves rollback.
  • What state or data must be preserved.
  • When to escalate.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
List security boundaries:

  • Secrets.
  • Access.
  • Network exposure.
  • Audit.
  • Data sensitivity.
  • Public documentation redaction.

For public documentation, remove or replace:

  • Credentials, tokens, private keys, certificates, and kubeconfig files.
  • Personal data, customer data, customer names, and company-specific business context.
  • Real domains, IP addresses, repository URLs, database names, bucket names, account identifiers, and production configuration.
  • Internal topology, private routes, VPN profiles, Wi-Fi secrets, and unrestricted dashboard or query output.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Say when the standard must be reviewed: after incidents, upgrades, ownership changes, or new workload classes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: Link to nearby standards, runbooks, cases, and templates.

docs.title: Quality Gate
docs.text:
The page is not ready if it:

  • Is only an installation note.
  • Has no reader task.
  • Has no validation or failure modes.
  • Has no rollback or escalation boundary where operational risk exists.
  • Contains real environment identifiers.
  • Exists in only one language.

docs.title: System Design
docs.text:
System design answers one question: can a system operate under real traffic, real failure, and real organizational constraints.

Architecture articles must explain constraints, alternatives, failure modes, validation, and rollback. A component diagram is not enough.

docs.title: Production Architecture
docs.text:
• AWS EKS Production High Availability Cluster Design and Delivery Standard — the site flagship and the quality benchmark for production depth.
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • kubeadm Installation Standard
  • EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management

docs.title: Reading Suggestion
docs.text: If you want one article to judge the depth of this site, read the EKS HA cluster standard. From conclusion-first structure, applicable and non-applicable boundaries, explicit assumptions, failure modes, to rollback paths, it is the quality template for everything else.

docs.title: AWS EKS Production High Availability Cluster Design and Delivery Standard
Executive Summary
docs.text:
Building a production-grade highly available EKS cluster on AWS is not mainly about increasing node count or creating a Kubernetes control plane. The real work is putting control plane access, worker capacity, networking, identity, ingress, storage, delivery, observability, upgrades, and recovery into one verifiable operating model.

A credible production EKS design should provide:

  • Workload placement across multiple Availability Zones.
  • Scheduling continuity when one AZ loses capacity.
  • Recoverable and scalable worker capacity.
  • Workload-level AWS permissions instead of broad node-level credentials.
  • Clear ownership for ingress, DNS, certificates, logs, metrics, and alerts.
  • Validation and rollback paths for each critical change.

If a guide stops at eksctl create cluster, it proves that a cluster can be created. It does not prove that the platform can carry production traffic.

docs.title: When To Use This Standard
docs.text:
Use this document when:

  • Creating a new production Amazon EKS cluster.
  • Migrating workloads from virtual machines or self-managed Kubernetes to EKS.
  • Hardening an existing EKS cluster for availability, security, observability, and release safety.
  • Building a reusable delivery checklist for platform or DevOps teams.

Do not use this as:

  • A beginner Kubernetes tutorial.
  • A short-lived proof-of-concept guide.
  • A replacement for multi-Region disaster recovery architecture.
  • A complete regulated-industry compliance blueprint.

Multi-AZ inside one Region is not the same as multi-Region resilience. Treat those as different architecture problems.

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
This standard assumes:

  • One AWS Region with at least three Availability Zones.
  • Mostly stateless services, with state placed in managed data services or carefully designed persistent storage.
  • GitOps or CI/CD based delivery.
  • Public or internal ingress traffic.
  • Least-privilege access, auditability, and rollback requirements.

For a small low-risk environment, reduce the design: two capacity classes, one ingress path, basic metrics, basic logs, and explicit backup. Do not add every production component just to make the platform look mature.

docs.title: Target Architecture
docs.text:
Users
  |
Route 53
  |
AWS Load Balancer Controller
  |
Ingress / Service
  |
Application Pods
  |
Managed data services / persistent storage

Control and operations plane:

EKS managed control plane
  - multi-AZ managed by AWS

Worker capacity
  - managed node groups or Karpenter
  - spread across at least 3 AZs
  - separate system and application capacity

Identity
  - IAM roles for cluster operators
  - EKS Pod Identity or IRSA for workload permissions

Observability
  - metrics
  - logs
  - events
  - alerts
  - audit trail

The key principle: AWS manages the EKS control plane, but application availability still depends on your VPC, subnets, node groups, scheduling rules, Pod replicas, disruption budgets, storage, ingress, and release process.

docs.title: 1. Multi-AZ Is The Baseline
docs.text:
A production EKS cluster should normally use at least three AZs. Two AZs can be acceptable for lower-risk systems, but capacity recovery and maintenance flexibility are weaker. A one-AZ EKS cluster should not be described as production high availability.

Check:

  • The VPC spans at least three AZs.
  • Each AZ has private subnets for worker nodes.
  • Node groups or Karpenter provision capacity across AZs.
  • Application replicas are spread with topology spread constraints or anti-affinity.
  • Storage classes are understood in terms of AZ binding.

Common failure mode: the cluster is configured with three AZ subnets, but actual worker capacity only exists in one AZ. The architecture diagram says multi-AZ; the scheduler experiences a single-AZ platform.

docs.title: 2. Worker Capacity Should Be Layered
docs.text:
Do not run every Pod on the same worker pool. A minimum production model has:

  • System capacity: CoreDNS, ingress controllers, log agents, monitoring agents.
  • Application capacity: user-facing and internal services.
  • Batch or Spot capacity: retryable jobs and interruptible workloads.

The purpose is not complexity. The purpose is to prevent business traffic or batch spikes from evicting the components that keep the cluster observable and reachable.

Recommended principles:

  • Keep system components on stable capacity.
  • Select instance families based on workload resource profiles.
  • Use Spot only for workloads that tolerate interruption and retry.
  • Use PodDisruptionBudgets for critical services.

docs.title: 3. Pods Should Not Inherit Broad Node Permissions
docs.text:
Production workloads should not rely on the node IAM role for AWS API access. Use EKS Pod Identity or IRSA so each workload gets the minimum IAM role it needs.

Acceptance criteria:

  • Each workload receives only the AWS permissions it needs.
  • Secrets, object storage, queues, and container registry permissions are separated.
  • Permission changes are auditable.
  • Removing an application also removes or disables its cloud permissions.

Common failure mode: S3, ECR, CloudWatch, and Secrets Manager permissions are all attached to the node role. It is convenient at first, but any compromised Pod can inherit excessive lateral movement capability.

docs.title: 4. Ingress Is More Than An Ingress Object
docs.text:
A production ingress path needs decisions for:

  • DNS ownership.
  • TLS issuance and renewal.
  • Public or internal load balancers.
  • WAF requirements.
  • Stable health check paths.
  • Traffic shifting for rollback or canary releases.

If a design only contains an Ingress YAML file, the ingress layer is not finished.

docs.title: 5. Default To Managed Services For State
docs.text:
EKS can run stateful systems, but production teams should not default to placing every database, queue, search engine, or cache inside the cluster. Prefer managed services such as RDS, ElastiCache, MSK, OpenSearch, and S3 where they fit.

Before running stateful services in the cluster, answer:

  • Where are backups stored?
  • How does recovery work across AZ failure?
  • How are failed upgrades rolled back?
  • What happens when a node is lost?
  • Does the team really have operational ownership of this component?

docs.title: Phase 1: Network And Account Boundary
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • VPC, subnets, route tables, NAT Gateway or an approved alternative.
  • Cluster IAM role.
  • Node IAM role.
  • Operator and CI/CD access boundaries.
  • Tagging standards.

Validate:

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws ec2 describe-subnets --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=<vpc-id>"
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster-name>

Risks: NAT Gateway cost is often underestimated; private cluster access is often forgotten; overly broad administrator access tends to become the default.

docs.title: Phase 2: Create The EKS Control Plane
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • EKS cluster.
  • Kubernetes version selection.
  • API server endpoint access policy.
  • Control plane logging.
  • Encryption configuration.

Validate:

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name <cluster-name> --region <region>
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get ns

Note: kubectl get nodes can be empty immediately after the control plane is created. A healthy control plane does not mean worker capacity has joined.

docs.title: Phase 3: Worker Capacity And System Components
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • Managed Node Groups or Karpenter.
  • Verified versions for CoreDNS, kube-proxy, and VPC CNI.
  • Scheduling separation for system and application components.
  • Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter scale-out behavior.

Validate:

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl -n kube-system get pods -o wide
kubectl describe node <node-name>
kubectl top nodes

Failure mode: nodes are Ready, but business Pods stay Pending because subnet IP capacity or CNI configuration is exhausted. The broken object is not the Deployment; it is the network capacity model.

docs.title: Phase 4: Identity, Permissions, And Secrets
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • EKS Pod Identity or IRSA.
  • Least-privilege IAM policies.
  • Secret management rules.
  • CI/CD deployment permissions.

Validate:

kubectl -n <namespace> describe serviceaccount <service-account>
kubectl -n <namespace> exec deploy/<deployment-name> -- aws sts get-caller-identity

The identity visible from the business Pod should be the workload role, not the node role.

docs.title: Phase 5: Ingress, Certificates, And Release Path
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • AWS Load Balancer Controller.
  • IngressClass.
  • DNS records.
  • TLS certificates.
  • Health checks.
  • Canary or blue-green release strategy.

Validate:

kubectl -n kube-system get deploy aws-load-balancer-controller
kubectl -n <namespace> get ingress
kubectl -n <namespace> describe ingress <ingress-name>
curl -I https://<service-domain>

Failure mode: the Ingress object is created, but no load balancer appears. The usual causes are controller permissions, subnet tags, or incorrect annotations.

docs.title: Phase 6: Observability And Alerting
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • Node metrics.
  • Pod metrics.
  • Application logs.
  • Kubernetes Events.
  • API server or audit logs.
  • Critical alerts.

Minimum alert set:

  • Node NotReady.
  • Pod CrashLoopBackOff.
  • Deployment replica shortage.
  • PVC high usage.
  • Ingress 5xx increase.
  • HPA unable to scale for a sustained period.
  • CoreDNS error rate increase.

Validate:

kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl top pods -A
kubectl -n <monitoring-namespace> get pods

docs.title: Rollback Strategy
docs.text:
EKS rollback cannot rely only on kubectl rollout undo. Roll back by layer.

Layer Rollback Method Application release Revert image tag, Helm release, or GitOps commit Ingress Restore old rule, old certificate, or old load balancer Worker nodes Revert launch template or create a previous-version node group Permissions Revert IAM policy or ServiceAccount binding Cluster add-ons Revert to a compatible add-on version or temporarily scale old capacity Kubernetes version Usually cannot be downgraded in place; recover through a new cluster migration plan

The Kubernetes version upgrade path is often underestimated. Before upgrading production, confirm compatibility for add-ons, node AMIs, controllers, and critical CRDs.

docs.title: Production Acceptance Checklist
docs.text:
Before production traffic:

  • The cluster spans three AZs.
  • Private subnet IP capacity is sufficient.
  • Worker capacity spans AZs and can recover automatically.
  • System and application components have capacity separation.
  • CoreDNS has enough replicas and is spread across nodes.
  • Critical applications have multiple replicas, PDBs, requests, and limits.
  • Ingress has DNS, TLS, health checks, and rollback.
  • Workloads use least-privilege identity.
  • Secrets are not stored in images, ConfigMaps, or Git.
  • Logs, metrics, events, and alerts are available.
  • CI/CD permissions are auditable.
  • Backup and restore have been tested.
  • Upgrade and failure drills are recorded.

docs.title: Mistake 1: Treating The Managed Control Plane As Application HA
docs.text: AWS reduces control plane operational burden, but application Pods, node capacity, ingress, storage, delivery, and monitoring remain your responsibility. Application availability is designed and verified, not purchased by cluster creation.

docs.title: Mistake 2: Counting Nodes Without Checking Placement
docs.text: Six nodes in one AZ still create an AZ-level failure risk. Replica count and node count only matter when combined with topology distribution.

docs.title: Mistake 3: Migrating Stateful Services First
docs.text: Start migrations with stateless services. Use them to prove image standards, release flow, ingress, logging, metrics, and rollback. Stateful services come later because their hardest problems are data consistency, recovery time, and recovery point.

docs.title: Mistake 4: Leaving Permissions To The End
docs.text: If permissions are handled last, temporary exceptions become permanent patterns. Pod identity and deployment access boundaries should exist from the start.

docs.title: Q&A Assistant Blocks
docs.text:
Question: Is a Managed Node Group enough for EKS high availability?

No. Managed Node Groups help with worker lifecycle management, but high availability also depends on multi-AZ subnets, scheduling spread, Pod replicas, PDBs, ingress, storage, permissions, monitoring, and rollback.

Question: How many AZs should a production EKS cluster use?

Usually at least three AZs. Two AZs can work for lower-risk systems, but recovery and maintenance flexibility are weaker. A one-AZ cluster should not be called production high availability.

Question: How should Pods access AWS services?

Use EKS Pod Identity or IRSA and bind each workload to a least-privilege IAM role. Do not let application Pods inherit broad permissions from the node role.

Question: Should stateful services run inside EKS?

Prefer managed AWS services by default. Run stateful systems inside EKS only when the team owns backup, recovery, upgrade, monitoring, and incident handling for that component.

docs.title: Official References
docs.text:
• Amazon EKS Best Practices Guide: Reliability
  • Amazon EKS Pod Identities
  • Amazon EKS security and IAM
  • Amazon EKS networking

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines a safe process for upgrading a managed (EKS-style) or self-managed Kubernetes cluster from one minor version to the next, without an unplanned outage.

The core judgment of this standard:

A Kubernetes upgrade is three coupled upgrades — control plane, add-ons, and nodes — that must happen in order, plus an API-compatibility check that catches breakage before you start. Most upgrade incidents are not the control plane; they are an add-on that no longer matches the version, or a workload using an API the new version removed.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Upgrade EKS, self-managed Kubernetes, or a compatible platform across a minor version.
  • Replace worker node groups as part of a platform upgrade.
  • Upgrade core add-ons (CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CSI drivers, ingress, observability agents).

This is the upgrade standard. Standing up a new cluster is covered by the cluster provisioning standard; this assumes a running cluster.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Initial cluster creation (see the Kubernetes cluster provisioning standard).
  • Application rollout mechanics (see the deployment rollback runbook).
  • A specific cloud provider's exact console/CLI steps — use the provider's documented upgrade path within this sequence.

docs.title: Upgrade Principles
docs.text:
1. Upgrade one minor version at a time unless the platform explicitly supports a larger jump.
  2. Treat add-ons as part of the cluster, not optional extras — version-skew between control plane and add-ons is a top cause of breakage.
  3. Upgrade in sequence: control plane → add-ons → nodes.
  4. Prefer new node groups + drain over in-place node mutation, so rollback is "stop draining" rather than "un-upgrade a node".
  5. Validate both Kubernetes health and application behavior before declaring done.
  6. Define rollback triggers up front and keep rollback paths open through the observation window.

docs.title: Pre-Upgrade Assessment
docs.text:
Capture a baseline and run the compatibility checks — this is where you prevent the outage.

kubectl version
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get deploy,statefulset,daemonset -A
kubectl get ingress,svc,pvc,storageclass -A
kubectl top nodes; kubectl top pods -A
Area Check Deprecated APIs Find workloads using APIs removed in the target version (e.g. with kubectl deprecations/pluto/kubent). This is the highest-value check. Add-ons CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CSI drivers, ingress controller, cert-manager, metrics-server compatibility with the target version. Workloads PodDisruptionBudgets, probes, resource requests, priority classes, security contexts. Storage CSI driver compatibility, PVC binding, snapshot/backup support. Networking Ingress class behavior, load-balancer annotations, NetworkPolicies. Access IAM/RBAC, service accounts, and a working break-glass path.

A PDB that blocks all eviction, or a deprecated API still in use, will stall the upgrade midway — find these now, not during the drain.

docs.title: 1. Freeze and announce
docs.text: Pause unrelated deploys and infra changes for the window. Communicate impact, rollback criteria, and owners.

docs.title: 2. Back up cluster state
docs.text:
kubectl get all -A -o yaml > cluster-workloads.snapshot.yaml
kubectl get configmap,ingress,svc,pvc,storageclass -A -o yaml > cluster-config.snapshot.yaml

Do not export Secret values to plaintext. Back up secrets via the platform's encrypted process; for self-managed clusters, back up etcd with the recommended encrypted method (see the etcd standard).

docs.title: 3. Upgrade the control plane
docs.text:
Upgrade via the provider/cluster tool; wait for the API server to be healthy.

kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose'
kubectl get nodes

docs.title: 4. Upgrade core add-ons to target-compatible versions
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl rollout status deployment/coredns -n kube-system
kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system

Do this before moving workloads — nodes joining on the new version need compatible CNI/kube-proxy.

docs.title: 5. Add a new node group, drain the old gradually
docs.text:
Create a new node group on the target version, then move workloads in small batches:

kubectl cordon <old-node>
kubectl drain <old-node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=10m

Validate application health after each batch before continuing. This is the reversible model: if a batch misbehaves, stop and keep the old (still-healthy) nodes.

docs.title: 6. Validate, then decommission old nodes
docs.text: After the observation window, scale down and remove the old node group. Keep snapshots and notes.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
kubectl get ingress,pvc -A

Application validation must include: HTTP health, a core API/login smoke test, background-job processing, database connectivity, external webhooks/callbacks, and that alerting/dashboards still work. Declare success only after these pass and metrics are stable through the observation window.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Deprecated API breakage Controllers/CI fail post-upgrade Workload used an API removed in target Find with pluto/kubent before upgrading; migrate manifests. Add-on version skew DNS/networking/storage flaky after control-plane upgrade Add-ons not upgraded to match Upgrade add-ons before moving nodes. Drain stalls kubectl drain hangs PDB blocks eviction / single replica Fix PDB/replicas; never --force blindly. Pods won't schedule Pending after node migration Taints/labels/capacity mismatch on new group Match labels/taints/size; check resource requests. Storage won't attach StatefulSet pods stuck CSI driver incompatibility Verify CSI compatibility in pre-assessment; roll back nodes. Control plane not reversible Can't downgrade after issue Managed upgrades are forward-only Recover via nodes/add-ons/app rollback + backups (below).

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Managed control-plane upgrades are usually not directly reversible — design rollback around the reversible layers.

  1. Stop node migration immediately; keep/recreate old node groups while they are still supported.
  2. Roll back add-ons to compatible versions where safe.
  3. Roll back application deployments (kubectl rollout undo).
  4. Restore stateful components from backups if data was affected.

Define rollback triggers before starting:

  • A core business smoke test fails.
  • Error rate exceeds threshold for more than ~10 minutes.
  • Critical pods cannot schedule.
  • Storage attach/mount errors hit stateful workloads.
  • Ingress/load-balancer behavior breaks external traffic.

docs.title: Post-Upgrade Review
docs.text: Record source/target versions, add-on versions before/after, node-group changes, deprecated-API findings, incidents and mitigations, and follow-up work.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never export Secret values to plaintext during backup; use encrypted/secret-manager backups.
  • Verify RBAC, IAM, and break-glass access still work on the new version before decommissioning old paths.
  • Do not publish real cluster names, account IDs, endpoints, or node names.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/cluster team.
  • Re-run the deprecated-API and add-on compatibility check before every upgrade.
  • Re-confirm rollback triggers and the backup/restore path before each upgrade window.
  • Track the Kubernetes version support window so upgrades are planned, not forced by end-of-support.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • etcd Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines when and how to use kubeadm for Kubernetes cluster installation.

The core judgment:

kubeadm installs Kubernetes; it does not operate the platform for you.

A kubeadm cluster is appropriate only when the team can own control-plane availability, etcd backup and restore, certificate lifecycle, node maintenance, add-on upgrades, and incident recovery.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use kubeadm when:

  • Managed Kubernetes is unavailable or unsuitable.
  • The team needs explicit control over control-plane components.
  • The environment has strong network or infrastructure constraints.
  • Operators can staff and test the full lifecycle.

Prefer managed Kubernetes when the team cannot own etcd recovery, certificate rotation, and control-plane patching.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard is not a step-by-step installation transcript. It does not replace the broader Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard.

docs.title: Decide the control-plane model first
docs.text:
Before running kubeadm, decide:

  • Single control plane or highly available control plane.
  • API endpoint and load balancer ownership.
  • etcd stacked or external.
  • Certificate authority ownership.
  • Upgrade sequence.
  • Backup and restore procedure.
  • Node replacement procedure.

Do not create a single-control-plane cluster and later call it production without a deliberate exception.

docs.title: Freeze the add-on sequence
docs.text:
Minimum sequence:

  1. OS and container runtime baseline.
  2. kubeadm, kubelet, kubectl versions.
  3. Control plane.
  4. CNI.
  5. CoreDNS.
  6. Metrics server.
  7. CSI if storage is required.
  8. Ingress if services are published.
  9. Observability agents.
  10. Backup and restore validation.

Applications should not be onboarded before CNI, DNS, storage, ingress, and observability are validated.

docs.title: Own etcd recovery
docs.text:
For kubeadm clusters, etcd is a first-class production dependency.

Required:

  • Snapshot schedule.
  • Snapshot retention.
  • Restore test.
  • Encryption and access control for snapshots.
  • Documentation of restore target and expected downtime.

If the team cannot restore etcd in a test environment, the cluster is not production-ready.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After installation:

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get componentstatuses

Also validate:

  • CNI pod-to-pod connectivity.
  • DNS resolution from a pod.
  • Service routing.
  • Metrics server.
  • StorageClass and PVC if used.
  • Ingress path if used.
  • etcd snapshot and restore rehearsal.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention No stable API endpoint Control-plane failover breaks clients. Use an owned HA endpoint. No etcd restore test Cluster state loss becomes unrecoverable. Test restore before onboarding workloads. Certificate lifecycle ignored Cluster fails unexpectedly at expiry. Track and rehearse rotation. CNI chosen after install Pod networking assumptions break. Select CNI before cluster init. Add-ons unversioned Upgrade drift. Maintain version matrix. Apps onboarded before observability First failure has no evidence. Install observability before workloads.

docs.title: Rollback and Rebuild Boundary
docs.text:
Prefer rebuild when:

  • Pod or service CIDRs are wrong.
  • Control-plane endpoint design is wrong.
  • etcd topology is wrong for production.
  • Certificate authority ownership is unclear.
  • The cluster is young and has no critical workloads.

Prefer in-place repair when:

  • Add-on configuration is wrong but data plane is recoverable.
  • Node pool needs replacement.
  • Version skew can be corrected within supported bounds.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Protect kubeconfig and certificate authority material.
  • Restrict control-plane host access.
  • Use least privilege for node and admin access.
  • Encrypt etcd backups.
  • Do not publish real cluster endpoints or certificates.
  • Keep audit logs where feasible.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review before each kubeadm cluster build, Kubernetes minor upgrade, certificate rotation, etcd incident, or control-plane topology change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • System OS Patching Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to provision a Kubernetes cluster that can safely host production workloads.

The most important judgment is simple:

Cluster provisioning is not the command that creates the control plane. It is the ordered construction of a platform dependency graph.

A cluster is not ready because kubectl get nodes returns Ready. It is ready only when workload identity, network boundaries, node pools, storage, ingress, observability, deployment controls, backup expectations, and ownership rules are all explicit and validated.

This standard applies to managed clusters such as EKS, CCE, ACK, GKE, and AKS, and to self-managed clusters built with kubeadm. The implementation details differ, but the readiness contract is the same.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Building a new production or staging Kubernetes cluster.
  • Rebuilding a cluster after a major network, identity, or runtime change.
  • Creating a repeatable cluster profile for multiple teams.
  • Reviewing whether an existing cluster is mature enough for business workloads.
  • Converting an experimental cluster into a platform-owned service.

Do not use this as a quick local development checklist. Development clusters can skip many production controls, but they should not be mistaken for production reference architecture.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • A vendor-specific click-by-click console procedure.
  • Full Kubernetes security hardening.
  • Application architecture inside the cluster.
  • Service mesh design.
  • Disaster recovery design beyond the provisioning readiness boundary.

docs.title: Provisioning Judgment
docs.text:
A production cluster must answer seven questions before the first business workload is deployed:

Question Required Decision Who owns the platform? Platform owner, escalation path, change window, and lifecycle policy. Where can traffic flow? VPC or VNet, CIDR, service CIDR, pod CIDR, DNS, egress, private endpoints, and ingress exposure. Who can act? Human access, workload identity, service accounts, emergency access, and audit logging. Where do workloads run? Node pool boundaries, taints, labels, autoscaling, runtime, images, and maintenance policy. How is state stored? StorageClass, CSI driver, expansion, backup, snapshot, reclaim policy, and topology constraints. How do users enter? Ingress class, load balancer model, TLS ownership, DNS ownership, and certificate renewal. How is failure seen and reversed? Metrics, logs, events, alerts, rollout controls, rollback procedure, and restore boundary.

If any answer is missing, the cluster may exist, but it is not production-ready.

docs.title: Decision Inputs
docs.text:
Capture these inputs before provisioning:

Area Required Input Kubernetes version Target version, supported skew, add-on compatibility, upgrade window. Network VPC or VNet, CIDR plan, pod/service ranges, DNS, egress NAT, private endpoint requirements. Identity Cluster admin model, group mapping, workload identity mechanism, break-glass account. Node pools CPU and memory profile, on-demand or spot policy, GPU or special hardware, taints, labels. Storage Default StorageClass, stateful workload needs, backup and restore expectation, expansion policy. Ingress Public or private exposure, controller, TLS issuer, DNS ownership, WAF or gateway boundary. Observability Metrics, logs, events, traces, alert routing, dashboard ownership. Delivery GitOps or pipeline ownership, namespace onboarding, rollback path, deployment policy. Governance Naming, tagging, cost allocation, resource quota, image policy, platform review cadence.

The provisioning record should be short, but it must be explicit. A cluster with undocumented defaults becomes an operational surprise during the first incident.

docs.title: 1. Define the platform boundary
docs.text:
Decide what the cluster is allowed to own. A cluster should not accidentally become the owner of DNS, secrets, certificates, ingress, CI/CD, databases, and monitoring simply because those things were installed during setup.

At minimum, define:

  • Production, staging, development, or shared platform scope.
  • Allowed workload classes.
  • Network exposure model.
  • Team onboarding process.
  • Ownership for platform add-ons.
  • Incident escalation path.

docs.title: 2. Freeze the version and add-on matrix
docs.text:
Select the Kubernetes version together with the add-ons that must run on it. Do not choose a cluster version first and discover compatibility later.

The matrix must include:

  • CNI plugin and version.
  • CoreDNS.
  • kube-proxy or replacement.
  • CSI drivers.
  • Ingress controller.
  • Metrics server.
  • Autoscaler or Karpenter-equivalent component.
  • Log and metric agents.
  • Policy controllers if used.

The minimum acceptable output is a table that says which versions are approved, which are blocked, and which upgrade sequence is required.

docs.title: 3. Provision network before nodes
docs.text:
The network is harder to change than the cluster. Validate it before creating node pools.

Required checks:

  • Pod and service CIDRs do not overlap with VPC/VNet, peered networks, VPN ranges, or on-premises ranges.
  • DNS resolution works for cloud APIs, internal services, and private endpoints.
  • Nodes can reach required image registries and control-plane endpoints.
  • Egress policy is deliberate, not an accidental open route.
  • Ingress subnets and internal load balancer subnets are separated when needed.

If the network plan is unclear, stop. Rebuilding a young cluster is cheaper than living with overlapping address space.

docs.title: 4. Configure identity and access before applications
docs.text:
Human and workload access must be set before application teams arrive.

Required decisions:

  • Which groups have cluster-admin access.
  • Which groups have namespace-scoped admin access.
  • How service accounts obtain cloud permissions.
  • How emergency access is granted, logged, and revoked.
  • Which audit logs are retained.

Avoid binding permanent cluster-admin rights to individual users. Use groups, time-bounded emergency access, and documented review.

docs.title: 5. Build node pools as workload contracts
docs.text:
Node pools are not just capacity. They are workload placement contracts.

Define at least:

  • General stateless pool.
  • System or platform add-on pool where appropriate.
  • Stateful or storage-sensitive pool if required.
  • Spot or preemptible pool only for tolerant workloads.
  • GPU or hardware-specific pools only when isolated by taints and labels.

Every pool should have labels, taints where appropriate, autoscaling boundaries, image lifecycle rules, and a patch or replacement strategy.

docs.title: 6. Install storage before workload onboarding
docs.text:
Install CSI drivers and StorageClasses before application deployment. A cluster without a tested default storage path is not ready for stateful workloads.

Minimum validation:

  • A PVC can be dynamically provisioned.
  • The mounted filesystem is writable from a pod.
  • Expansion behavior is known.
  • Reclaim policy is deliberate.
  • Backup or snapshot expectation is documented.
  • Zone or topology constraints are visible to schedulers.

Storage design details belong in Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard.

docs.title: 7. Install ingress and TLS before publishing services
docs.text:
Ingress must be a managed platform capability, not something each team improvises.

Validate:

  • IngressClass ownership.
  • Public versus private exposure.
  • TLS certificate issuer and renewal.
  • DNS ownership.
  • Request timeout, body size, and WebSocket expectations.
  • Access log and metric availability.
  • WAF or gateway boundary if required.

docs.title: 8. Install observability before business traffic
docs.text:
Observability is a launch dependency. Do not wait for the first incident to install it.

Minimum signals:

  • Node health and resource saturation.
  • Pod restart, scheduling, and image pull failures.
  • API server health if exposed by the platform.
  • Ingress request rate, latency, and error rate.
  • PVC usage and expansion risk where stateful workloads exist.
  • Event collection for debugging.
  • Alert routing to the owning team.

docs.title: 9. Add delivery and rollback controls
docs.text:
The first deployment mechanism should include rollback before it includes speed.

Required controls:

  • Namespace onboarding pattern.
  • Deployment owner.
  • Image provenance expectation.
  • Rollout strategy.
  • Rollback command or GitOps revert path.
  • Release health check.
  • Change record.

See Deployment Rollback Runbook for the operational rollback procedure.

docs.title: Managed Cluster Profile
docs.text:
For managed Kubernetes services, prefer the provider-managed control plane and avoid self-managing components that the provider can operate more safely.

Required managed-cluster decisions:

  • Private or public control-plane endpoint.
  • Control-plane access allowlist.
  • Managed node group versus self-managed node group.
  • IAM or workload identity integration.
  • Provider-supported CSI and load balancer controllers.
  • Provider version support window.
  • Upgrade ownership between platform team and provider.

Do not assume "managed" means "production-ready." The provider operates part of the platform; your team still owns workload access, add-ons, delivery, observability, data protection, and incident response.

docs.title: Self-Managed kubeadm Profile
docs.text:
For kubeadm clusters, the team must explicitly own the control plane.

Additional requirements:

  • Highly available API server endpoint.
  • etcd backup and restore test.
  • Certificate rotation procedure.
  • Control-plane node patching process.
  • CNI installation and upgrade policy.
  • kubelet and container runtime lifecycle.
  • Disaster recovery runbook.

If these cannot be staffed and tested, use a managed cluster unless there is a hard constraint that prevents it.

docs.title: Application Readiness Gate
docs.text:
Before accepting business workloads, run this gate:

Gate Pass Condition Nodes All intended node pools are ready, labeled, tainted as designed, and autoscaling within bounds. System pods CoreDNS, CNI, CSI, ingress, metrics, and observability agents are healthy. Storage A test PVC provisions, mounts, writes, expands if supported, and follows the intended reclaim policy. Ingress A test service is reachable through the intended public or private route with valid TLS. Identity A workload can use the intended cloud identity without static secrets. Observability Test failures produce searchable logs, metrics, events, and alerts. Delivery A test deployment can roll forward and roll back through the approved mechanism. Cost and ownership Namespaces, labels, quotas, and escalation routes are visible.

The cluster is not open for onboarding until every gate has an owner and a result.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Run validation from a clean operator workstation or CI runner with normal platform permissions:

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get ingressclass
kubectl top nodes
kubectl auth can-i create deployments --namespace <test-namespace>
kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as <non-admin-user>

Then create and remove a small validation workload:

  • Stateless deployment with readiness probe.
  • PVC-backed pod.
  • Internal service.
  • Ingress route with TLS.
  • Deliberate failed rollout followed by rollback.

Record the result in the cluster readiness document. A screenshot is weaker than a repeatable command and expected output.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Why It Hurts Prevention CIDR overlap Peering, VPN, and service routing fail later. Validate all address ranges before cluster creation. Add-on version mismatch CNI, CSI, or ingress breaks after upgrade. Freeze and review the version matrix. No tested StorageClass Stateful workloads block or lose data. Run PVC provision, mount, write, expand, and reclaim tests. Ingress before TLS ownership Services go public without renewal accountability. Define DNS and certificate ownership before exposure. App deployment before observability First incident has no useful evidence. Treat observability as a launch dependency. Permanent individual admin access Audit and offboarding become weak. Use groups, emergency access, and periodic review. Unbounded node autoscaling Cost spikes or scheduling instability. Set min, max, labels, taints, quotas, and alerts.

docs.title: Rollback and Rebuild Boundary
docs.text:
Some provisioning mistakes should be fixed by rebuilding the cluster, not patching around them.

Prefer rebuild when:

  • Pod or service CIDR conflicts with another network.
  • The cluster was created in the wrong VPC, region, or account boundary.
  • Identity integration was built around long-lived static credentials.
  • Add-on ownership is unclear and no team can safely upgrade it.
  • The cluster has accepted business workloads without baseline observability, and the migration cost is still low.

Prefer in-place correction when:

  • A node pool needs resizing or replacement.
  • An add-on configuration is wrong but version-compatible.
  • A StorageClass or IngressClass needs a new default after migration planning.
  • RBAC needs tightening with an auditable access review.

The earlier the problem is found, the more aggressive the rebuild option should be.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not store kubeconfigs, tokens, certificates, or cloud keys in public documentation.
  • Do not document private endpoint addresses, customer domains, or internal CIDR values in public examples.
  • Use symbolic placeholders such as <pod-cidr> or <service-cidr> instead of copied production ranges.
  • Prefer workload identity over static cloud credentials mounted into pods.
  • Keep audit logging enabled before granting team access.
  • Review cluster-admin bindings after every onboarding wave and after every incident.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • Before each new cluster build.
  • Before each Kubernetes minor version upgrade.
  • After any network, identity, storage, or ingress incident.
  • When a new workload class requires a new node pool or storage profile.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to design and validate Kubernetes storage and ingress before business workloads depend on them.

The core judgment is:

Storage and ingress are not add-ons. They are production contracts between the platform and every application team.

Storage decides whether state survives, expands, moves, and restores. Ingress decides how users reach services, how TLS is renewed, how traffic is logged, and where the external boundary of the cluster begins. If either is treated as a default installed component, production risk becomes invisible until the first outage.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Provisioning a new Kubernetes cluster.
  • Introducing or changing a CSI driver, StorageClass, IngressClass, or ingress controller.
  • Onboarding stateful workloads.
  • Publishing services through public or private ingress.
  • Reviewing a cluster before it becomes a shared production platform.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Full database backup architecture.
  • Service mesh traffic policy.
  • Cloud-specific load balancer billing optimization.
  • Application-level data model design.
  • Every possible ingress controller feature.

docs.title: Design Principle
docs.text:
A production platform should expose a small number of named choices, not a large pile of implicit defaults.

Application teams should be able to answer:

  • Which StorageClass should I use for normal persistent data?
  • Which StorageClass supports expansion?
  • Which storage profile supports ReadWriteMany, if any?
  • Who owns backup and restore?
  • Which IngressClass should expose public traffic?
  • Which IngressClass should expose private traffic?
  • Who owns DNS and TLS renewal?
  • What request limits and timeout defaults apply?

If these answers require reading the platform install history, the platform contract is not clear enough.

docs.title: Define storage profiles explicitly
docs.text:
At minimum, define storage profiles like this:

Profile Typical Use Access Mode Expansion Backup Expectation Notes standard-rwo Normal application persistence ReadWriteOnce Supported Application or platform policy Default only if it is safe for most apps. fast-rwo Latency-sensitive state ReadWriteOnce Supported Required Use only when performance need is proven. shared-rwx Shared files, uploads, model artifacts ReadWriteMany Platform-defined Required Often NFS or managed file storage; monitor real filesystem usage. ephemeral Disposable cache or temporary state Depends Not guaranteed None Must not hold business-critical data.

Do not make a StorageClass default until its behavior under provision, mount, expansion, deletion, and failure is known.

docs.title: Make reclaim policy a deliberate decision
docs.text:
Delete is convenient but can destroy data when a PVC is deleted. Retain protects data but creates cleanup responsibility.

Use this rule:

  • Use Delete only for disposable or easily recreated data.
  • Use Retain for business data unless a backup and restore contract says otherwise.
  • Document who can delete retained volumes and how they verify the data is no longer needed.

docs.title: Treat expansion as a runbook, not a checkbox
docs.text:
allowVolumeExpansion: true only means Kubernetes accepts the resize request. It does not prove that the underlying filesystem, CSI driver, quota, and application can safely absorb the change.

Before approving expansion for a profile, verify:

  • The CSI driver supports online or offline expansion.
  • The filesystem reflects the new size.
  • The application tolerates the resize path.
  • Monitoring follows the real filesystem after expansion.
  • Rollback expectations are clear. Shrinking a volume is usually not a safe rollback.

docs.title: Separate backup responsibility from storage provisioning
docs.text:
Provisioning a PV is not the same thing as protecting the data in it.

For every storage profile, document:

  • Whether backups are required.
  • Whether snapshots are crash-consistent or application-consistent.
  • Who owns restore testing.
  • How long recovery is expected to take.
  • Which workloads must not use the profile.

docs.title: CSI Validation
docs.text:
Validate CSI before onboarding stateful workloads:

kubectl get csidrivers
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

Then run a real PVC test:

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: storage-validation
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: standard-rwo
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: storage-validation
spec:
  restartPolicy: Never
  containers:
    - name: writer
      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "date > /data/proof && cat /data/proof"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
  volumes:
    - name: data
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: storage-validation

Pass conditions:

  • PVC becomes Bound.
  • Pod schedules on an expected node.
  • File write succeeds.
  • Events show no CSI errors.
  • Deleting the test resources produces the intended reclaim behavior.

docs.title: Define ingress classes as exposure contracts
docs.text:
IngressClass names should communicate exposure and ownership.

Recommended pattern:

IngressClass Exposure Typical Owner Required Controls public-web Internet-facing Platform or edge team TLS, DNS, access logs, WAF or gateway review if required. private-web Internal network Platform team Internal load balancer, private DNS, access logs. partner-web Restricted external access Platform plus security Allowlist, TLS, audit, explicit approval.

Avoid leaving multiple ingress controllers active without a defaulting rule. An application should not be exposed through the wrong controller because an annotation was omitted.

docs.title: Own TLS as a lifecycle
docs.text:
TLS is not complete when the first certificate is issued.

Document:

  • Certificate issuer.
  • Renewal mechanism.
  • DNS validation owner.
  • Expiry alert.
  • Secret namespace and access policy.
  • Emergency replacement procedure.

If TLS renewal is manual, the service must have an expiry alert and a named owner.

docs.title: Standardize ingress defaults
docs.text:
Set and document defaults for:

  • Request timeout.
  • Body size.
  • WebSocket support.
  • HTTP to HTTPS redirect.
  • Access log format.
  • Real client IP handling.
  • Health check path.
  • Rate limiting or WAF boundary if used.

These values affect application behavior. They must not be hidden inside an ingress controller chart with no platform documentation.

docs.title: Ingress Validation
docs.text:
Use a small validation service before exposing real traffic:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ingress-validation
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-production
spec:
  ingressClassName: public-web
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - example.devops.test
      secretName: ingress-validation-tls
  rules:
    - host: example.devops.test
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: ingress-validation
                port:
                  number: 80

Pass conditions:

  • Ingress is assigned to the intended controller.
  • Load balancer or gateway address appears.
  • DNS resolves to the expected target.
  • TLS certificate is valid and renewable.
  • Access logs and request metrics appear.
  • A 404, 5xx, and slow request can be observed in the expected dashboard or query path.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text: Area Check StorageClass Default is deliberate, documented, and tested. CSI Controller and node plugins are healthy across node pools. PVC Provision, mount, write, delete, and expansion behavior are known. Reclaim Data retention behavior matches workload criticality. Backup Restore owner and restore test expectation are documented. IngressClass Public and private exposure paths are distinct. TLS Issuer, renewal, expiry alert, and emergency replacement are known. Logs and metrics Storage and ingress failures are observable. Ownership Platform and application responsibilities are separate.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Symptom Prevention Missing default StorageClass PVC stays Pending. Either define a safe default or require explicit storageClassName. Wrong access mode Pod cannot mount volume or multi-writer workload corrupts data. Map workloads to approved profiles. CSI IAM or identity failure PVC never provisions, events show cloud permission errors. Validate workload identity before onboarding. Unclear reclaim policy Data is deleted too early or retained forever. Document reclaim policy and cleanup owner. NFS used as invisible capacity pool PVC appears fine while backend filesystem fills. Monitor real filesystem usage and PVC metadata together. Wrong IngressClass Service becomes unreachable or publicly exposed unintentionally. Use explicit ingressClassName and admission checks where possible. TLS renewal missing Service fails when certificate expires. Add expiry alert and named renewal owner. Controller defaults hidden Large uploads, WebSockets, or slow requests fail unexpectedly. Publish default timeout and body-size policy.

docs.title: Rollback and Change Boundary
docs.text:
Storage and ingress changes have different rollback properties.

Usually reversible:

  • Adding a new StorageClass.
  • Adding a new IngressClass.
  • Changing annotations on a validation ingress.
  • Replacing an ingress controller configuration after a canary.

High-risk or not safely reversible:

  • Changing the default StorageClass after workloads already depend on it.
  • Changing reclaim policy without a migration plan.
  • Shrinking storage.
  • Moving a stateful workload between storage backends without backup and restore proof.
  • Replacing a public ingress path without DNS, TLS, and client cache planning.

If the rollback path is "restore from backup," the change is not a normal configuration rollback. Treat it as a data recovery operation.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real hostnames, certificate details, private IPs, or customer domains in examples.
  • Restrict access to TLS secrets.
  • Avoid mounting broad cloud credentials into CSI components.
  • Use least-privilege workload identity for storage drivers.
  • Review public ingress exposure before DNS is delegated.
  • Keep access logs available for incident review, but avoid logging sensitive request bodies.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • Before a new cluster accepts workloads.
  • Before changing CSI drivers, StorageClasses, ingress controllers, or IngressClasses.
  • After any data-loss, storage saturation, certificate expiry, or public exposure incident.
  • Before onboarding a new class of stateful workload.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to manage capacity for Kubernetes PVCs backed by NFS or NFS-like shared file storage.

The core judgment is:

NFS-backed PVC capacity is a shared filesystem problem wearing Kubernetes metadata.

A PVC request tells Kubernetes what the workload asked for. It does not always tell you how much space exists on the backend filesystem, how many other PVCs share that backend, or whether the backend can enforce per-claim limits. Capacity management must join both layers.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Kubernetes PVCs are provisioned from an NFS server, managed file service, or shared filesystem.
  • Multiple PVCs map to directories under the same backend export.
  • PVC usage alerts do not match backend filesystem usage.
  • Application teams use shared files, uploads, model artifacts, reports, or batch outputs.
  • The platform needs a standard for capacity ownership and alert thresholds.

This standard is especially important when NFS is used as a convenient ReadWriteMany solution. Convenience does not remove the need for capacity isolation, monitoring, and cleanup rules.
